blocks:
Ayahs

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلنَّبِىُّ ٱتَّقِ ٱللَّهَ وَلَا تُطِعِ ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ وَٱلْمُنَـٰفِقِينَ ۗ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ كَانَ عَلِيمًا حَكِيمًا (١)
O Prophet! Be wary of Allah and do not obey the faithless and the hypocrites. Indeed Allah is all-knowing, all-wise. (1)
وَٱتَّبِعْ مَا يُوحَىٰٓ إِلَيْكَ مِن رَّبِّكَ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ كَانَ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ خَبِيرًا (٢)
Follow that which is revealed to you from your Lord. Indeed Allah is well aware of what you do. (2)
وَتَوَكَّلْ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ ۚ وَكَفَىٰ بِٱللَّهِ وَكِيلًا (٣)
And put your trust in Allah; Allah suffices as trustee. (3)

Naming and Virtue of Surah Al-Ahzab

By the consensus of Islamic scholars, this Surah was revealed in "Medina," and as we have mentioned, it consists of 73 verses. Since a significant portion of this Surah deals with the events of the "Battle of the Confederates" (Al-Ahzab/The Trench), this name was chosen for it.

Regarding the virtue of this Surah, it is sufficient to mention a Hadith from the noble Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله:

من قرء سورة الاحزاب و علمها اهله ... اعطى الامان من عذاب القبر:

"Whoever recites Surah Al-Ahzab and teaches it to his family... shall be granted safety from the punishment of the grave." [^1] And it is narrated from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام:

من كان كثير القراءة لسورة الاحزاب كان يوم القيامة فى جوار محمد (ص) و آله و ازواجه:

"Whoever recites Surah Al-Ahzab frequently shall be in the company of Muhammad صلى الله عليه وآله and his family and his spouses on the Day of Resurrection." [^2] We have repeatedly stated that such virtues and honors are not achieved through a soulless recitation devoid of any thought or action. A recitation is required that becomes the origin of reflection, and a reflection that illuminates the horizon of human thought such that its light manifests in one's actions.

[^1] and [^2]: "Majma' al-Bayan," Vol. 8, p. 334 (Beginning of Surah Al-Ahzab).

Content of Surah Al-Ahzab

This Surah is one of the most prolific Surahs of the Holy Quran, pursuing diverse and very important issues regarding the principles and branches of Islam.

The discussions presented in this Surah can be divided into seven sections:

Section One: The beginning of the Surah, which invites the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله to obey Allah and abandon following the disbelievers and the proposals of the hypocrites, and assures him that He will support him against their obstructions.

Section Two: It refers to some superstitions of the Age of Ignorance (Jahiliyyah), such as the issue of "Zihar" (a form of divorce), which they considered a means of separation between husband and wife, as well as the issue of adoption (Tabanni), and strikes a line of nullification through them, restricting kinship ties to real and natural bonds.

Section Three: Which is the most important part of this Surah, relates to the Battle of the Confederates (Ahzab) and its soul-shaking events, the miraculous victory of the Muslims over the disbelievers, the various obstructions and excuses of the hypocrites, and their breaking of covenants. Comprehensive and fascinating instructions are stated in this regard.

Section Four: Relates to the wives of the Prophet, who must be role models and examples for Muslim women in everything, and gives them important instructions in this regard.

Section Five: Deals with the story of "Zaynab," the daughter of "Jahsh," who was once the wife of the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله adopted son, "Zayd," and after separating from him, married the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله by Allah's command. This became a pretext for the hypocrites, and the Quran provides a sufficient response to the excuse-seekers in this regard.

Section Six: Speaks about the issue of Hijab, which is closely related to the previous sections, and advises all believing women to observe this Islamic command.

Section Seven: Which constitutes the final section, refers to the important issue of the "Resurrection" (Ma'ad), the way to salvation in that great arena, and also explains the great trust (Amanah) of man—namely, the issue of his commitment, obligation, and responsibility.

بِسْمِ اللَّهِ الرَّحْمنِ الرَّحِيمِ‌

يا أَيُّهَا النَّبِيُّ اتَّقِ اللَّهَ وَ لا تُطِعِ الْكافِرِينَ وَ الْمُنافِقِينَ إِنَّ اللَّهَ كانَ عَلِيماً حَكِيماً (1) وَ اتَّبِعْ ما يُوحى‌ إِلَيْكَ مِنْ رَبِّكَ إِنَّ اللَّهَ كانَ بِما تَعْمَلُونَ خَبِيراً (2) وَ تَوَكَّلْ عَلَى اللَّهِ وَ كَفى‌ بِاللَّهِ وَكِيلاً (3)

Occasion of Revelation

Commentators have narrated various occasions of revelation here, almost all of which pursue a single theme.

Among them, it is said that these verses were revealed regarding "Abu Sufyan" and some other leaders of disbelief and polytheism who, after the Battle of "Uhud," obtained safe passage from the "Prophet of Islam" صلى الله عليه وآله and entered Medina. Accompanied by "Abdullah ibn Ubayy" and some of their other friends, they came to the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله and said: "O Muhammad! Come and refrain from speaking ill of our gods—the idols of Lat, Uzza, and Manat—and say that they intercede for their worshippers, so that we too may leave you alone, and you are free to describe your God however you wish."

This proposal upset the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله. Umar stood up and said: "Allow me to put them to the sword!" The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله said: "I have given them safe passage; such a thing is not possible." However, he ordered them to be expelled from Medina. The above verses were revealed, commanding the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله not to pay heed to such proposals. [^1]

Commentary

Follow Only Divine Revelation

One of the most dangerous precipices on the path of great leaders is the issue of compromise proposals put forward by opponents. It is here that deviant lines are created in the path of leaders, attempting to pull them away from the main course, and this is a great test for them.

[^1]: Majma' al-Bayan under the verses in question and other commentaries.

The polytheists of "Mecca" and the hypocrites of "Medina" repeatedly tried to divert the noble Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله from the line of "Monotheism" (Tawhid) by proposing compromises, including what we read in the occasion of revelation above.

However, the first verses of Surah Al-Ahzab were revealed, ending their conspiracy and inviting the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله to continue his decisive method in the line of Monotheism without the slightest compromise.

These verses collectively give four important instructions to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله:

The first instruction is regarding piety and God-wariness (Taqwa), which is the groundwork for every other program. He says: "O Prophet! Fear Allah" (يا أَيُّهَا النَّبِيُّ اتَّقِ اللَّهَ‌).

The reality of "Taqwa" is that internal sense of responsibility, and as long as this sense of responsibility does not exist, man will not move toward any constructive program.

Taqwa is the motive for guidance and benefiting from Divine verses, as we read in the second verse of Surah Al-Baqarah: "هُدىً لِلْمُتَّقِينَ‌" (A guidance for the God-wary).

It is true that the final stage of Taqwa is achieved after faith and acting upon Allah's commands, but its initial stage precedes all these matters. For if a person does not feel a sense of responsibility within himself, he will not seek to investigate the call of the prophets, nor will he listen to their words. Even the issue of "averting potential harm," which scholars of theology and creed mention as the basis for the effort to know Allah (Ma'rifatullah), is in fact a branch of Taqwa.

The second instruction is the negation of obeying the disbelievers and hypocrites. He says: "And do not obey the disbelievers and the hypocrites" (وَ لا تُطِعِ الْكافِرِينَ وَ الْمُنافِقِينَ‌).

At the end of this verse, to emphasize this matter, He says: "Indeed, Allah is All-Knowing, All-Wise" (إِنَّ اللَّهَ كانَ عَلِيماً حَكِيماً).

If He commands you to abandon following them, it is based on His infinite knowledge and wisdom, for He knows what painful calamities and countless corruptions lie hidden in this following and compromise.

In any case, after Taqwa and the sense of responsibility, the first duty is to cleanse the page of the heart from strangers and to uproot the disturbing thorns from this land.

In the third instruction, He raises the issue of sowing the seeds of Monotheism and following Divine revelation, saying: "And follow what is revealed to you from your Lord" (وَ اتَّبِعْ ما يُوحى‌ إِلَيْكَ مِنْ رَبِّكَ‌). And be mindful and know that "Indeed, Allah is well-aware of what you do" (إِنَّ اللَّهَ كانَ بِما تَعْمَلُونَ خَبِيراً).

Therefore, first the demon must be driven out from within the soul so that the angel may enter.

The thorns must be cleared so that the seeds of flowers may grow.

One must engage in "Taghut-removal" so that the government of Allah and the Divine system may replace it.

And since there are many difficulties, threats, conspiracies, and obstructions along this path, He issues the fourth instruction as follows: "And put your trust in Allah, and do not fear their conspiracies" (وَ تَوَكَّلْ عَلَى اللَّهِ‌). "And Allah is sufficient as a Guardian and Protector for man" (وَ كَفى‌ بِاللَّهِ وَكِيلًا).

If a thousand enemies intend your destruction, since I am your Friend and Helper, have no fear of the enemies.

Although the addressee in these verses is the person of the Prophet, it is evident that it is an instruction for all believers and all Muslims of the world; it is a life-saving prescription and a life-giving elixir for every age and every time.

Some commentators have said that the address "Ya ayyuha" (O you...) is specific to cases where the goal is to draw general attention to a matter, even if the addressee is one person, unlike the address with "Ya" (O...), which is usually used in cases where the specific person addressed is intended. [^1] Since the verses under discussion begin with "Ya ayyuha," it emphasizes the universality of the objective of these verses.

Another piece of evidence for this generalization is that the phrase "إِنَّ اللَّهَ كانَ بِما تَعْمَلُونَ خَبِيراً" (Indeed, Allah is well-aware of what you [plural] do) is in the plural form. It says: "Allah is aware of the actions of all of you," whereas if only the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله were the addressee, it should have been said "Allah is aware of your [singular] action" (pay close attention).

It goes without saying that the concept of these instructions to the Prophet does not mean that he was negligent in the matter of Taqwa or in refusing to obey the disbelievers and hypocrites; rather, these statements, on one hand, serve as an emphasis regarding the duties of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, and on the other hand, are a lesson for all believers.

[^1]: Tafsir "Fakhr Razi," Vol. 25, p. 190, under the verses in question.

Ayahs

مَّا جَعَلَ ٱللَّهُ لِرَجُلٍ مِّن قَلْبَيْنِ فِى جَوْفِهِۦ ۚ وَمَا جَعَلَ أَزْوَٰجَكُمُ ٱلَّـٰٓـِٔى تُظَـٰهِرُونَ مِنْهُنَّ أُمَّهَـٰتِكُمْ ۚ وَمَا جَعَلَ أَدْعِيَآءَكُمْ أَبْنَآءَكُمْ ۚ ذَٰلِكُمْ قَوْلُكُم بِأَفْوَٰهِكُمْ ۖ وَٱللَّهُ يَقُولُ ٱلْحَقَّ وَهُوَ يَهْدِى ٱلسَّبِيلَ (٤)
Allah has not put two hearts within any man, nor has He made your wives whom you repudiate by zihar your mothers, nor has he made your adopted sons your [actual] sons. These are mere utterances of your mouths. But Allah speaks the truth and He guides to the [right] way. (4)
ٱدْعُوهُمْ لِـَٔابَآئِهِمْ هُوَ أَقْسَطُ عِندَ ٱللَّهِ ۚ فَإِن لَّمْ تَعْلَمُوٓا۟ ءَابَآءَهُمْ فَإِخْوَٰنُكُمْ فِى ٱلدِّينِ وَمَوَٰلِيكُمْ ۚ وَلَيْسَ عَلَيْكُمْ جُنَاحٌ فِيمَآ أَخْطَأْتُم بِهِۦ وَلَـٰكِن مَّا تَعَمَّدَتْ قُلُوبُكُمْ ۚ وَكَانَ ٱللَّهُ غَفُورًا رَّحِيمًا (٥)
Call them after their fathers. That is more just with Allah. If you do not know their fathers, then they are your brethren in the faith and your kinsmen. Excepting what your hearts may intend deliberately, there will be no sin upon you for any mistake that you may make therein. And Allah is all-forgiving, all-merciful. (5)
ٱلنَّبِىُّ أَوْلَىٰ بِٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ مِنْ أَنفُسِهِمْ ۖ وَأَزْوَٰجُهُۥٓ أُمَّهَـٰتُهُمْ ۗ وَأُو۟لُوا۟ ٱلْأَرْحَامِ بَعْضُهُمْ أَوْلَىٰ بِبَعْضٍ فِى كِتَـٰبِ ٱللَّهِ مِنَ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ وَٱلْمُهَـٰجِرِينَ إِلَّآ أَن تَفْعَلُوٓا۟ إِلَىٰٓ أَوْلِيَآئِكُم مَّعْرُوفًا ۚ كَانَ ذَٰلِكَ فِى ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ مَسْطُورًا (٦)
The Prophet is closer to the faithful than their own souls, and his wives are their mothers. The blood relatives are more entitled to inherit from one another in the Book of Allah than the [other] faithful and Emigrants, barring any favour you may do your comrades. This has been written in the Book. (6)

Commentary

Vain Claims

Following the previous verses which commanded the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) to follow only Divine revelation and not the disbelievers and hypocrites, the verses under discussion reflect the consequences of following them. Such following invites man to a collection of superstitions, falsehoods, and deviations, three instances of which are mentioned in the first verse under discussion:

First, He says: "Allah has not put for any man two hearts within his body" (مَا جَعَلَ اللَّهُ لِرَجُلٍ مِنْ قَلْبَيْنِ فِي جَوْفِهِ).

A group of commentators have written regarding the occasion of revelation for this part of the verse that during the Age of Ignorance (Jahiliyyah), there was a man named "Jamil ibn Ma'mar" who possessed a very strong memory. He used to claim: "Within my being, there are two hearts! With each of them, I understand better than Muhammad (صلى الله عليه وآله)!" Consequently, the polytheists of the Quraysh called him Dhu al-Qalbayn (the possessor of two hearts).

On the day of the Battle of Badr, when the polytheists fled, Jamil ibn Ma'mar was among them. Abu Sufyan saw him while he had one shoe on his foot and was holding the other in his hand as he fled. Abu Sufyan asked him: "What is the news?" He replied: "The army has fled." Abu Sufyan asked: "Then why do you have one shoe in your hand and the other on your foot?" Jamil ibn Ma'mar said: "Truly, I did not realize; I thought both were on my feet." (It became clear that despite all his claims, he was so bewildered that he did not understand as much as a person with even one heart—of course, "heart" in these instances refers to the intellect). [^1] In any case, following the disbelievers and hypocrites and abandoning the following of Divine revelation invites man to such superstitious matters.

However, beyond these, this sentence also carries a deeper meaning: that man has no more than one heart, and this heart cannot accommodate the love of more than one Deity. Those who call toward polytheism and multiple deities must possess multiple hearts so that each could be a center for the love of a deity! Fundamentally, the personality of a healthy human being is a single personality, and his line of thought is a single line. In solitude and in society, in the hidden and the manifest, internally and externally, in thought and in action—all must be one. Any kind of hypocrisy or duality in man's existence is an imposed matter and contrary to the requirements of his nature.

[^1]: Majma' al-Bayan under the verse discussed, and Tafsir al-Qurtubi.

By virtue of having only one heart, man must possess a single emotional center and be submissive to a single law. He should hold the love of one Beloved in his heart. He should pursue a specific path in life. He should harmonize with one group and one community; otherwise, fragmentation, multiplicity, divergent paths, and scattered goals will lead him toward futility and deviation from the innate path of Monotheism (Tawhid).

Thus, in a tradition from the Commander of the Faithful, Ali (عليه السلام), regarding the interpretation of this verse, we read that he said: "Our love and the love of our enemy do not gather within a person's chest, for Allah has not placed for a man two hearts in his chest, such that he loves with one and hates with the other. Our lovers are sincere in their love for us, just as gold is purified by fire until no dross remains in it. Whoever wishes to know this truth should test his heart; if anything of the love for our enemies is mixed in his heart with our love, he is not of us and we are not of him." [^1]

Therefore, a single heart is the center of a single belief, and that belief implements a single practical program. For man cannot truly believe in something yet be separated from it in action. Those in our era who claim multiple personalities for themselves—saying they performed a certain act from a political perspective, another from a religious one, and another from a social one, thereby justifying their contradictory actions—are ugly-natured hypocrites who wish to trample the law of creation with such talk. It is true that the aspects of human life are various, but a single line must govern them all.

The Quran then addresses another superstition from the Age of Ignorance, which is the superstition of Zihar. When they were upset with their wives and wanted to express detestation toward them, they would say: "Anti 'alayya ka-zahri ummi" ("You are to me like the back of my mother!"). With this statement, they considered her as their mother and this speech as a form of divorce! The Quran, in the continuation of this verse, says: "Allah has never made your wives whom you divorce by Zihar your mothers," and He has not decreed the rulings of a mother for them (وَمَا جَعَلَ أَزْوَاجَكُمُ اللَّائِي تُظَاهِرُونَ مِنْهُنَّ أُمَّهَاتِكُمْ).

Islam did not endorse this ignorant practice; rather, it established a penalty for it: the person who says this has no right to have intimacy with his wife until he pays the required expiation (kaffarah). If he does not pay the expiation and does not return to his wife, the wife can appeal to the "Religious Judge" (Hakim al-Shar') to compel him to do one of two things: either officially divorce her according to Islamic law and separate from her, or pay the expiation and continue the marital life as before. [^1] After all, what kind of talk is it that by saying "you are like my mother" to his wife, she should take the ruling of his mother? The relationship between mother and child is a natural one and can never be created by words. Thus, in Surah Al-Mujadilah, verse 2, it explicitly says: "إِنْ أُمَّهَاتُهُمْ إِلَّا اللَّائِي وَلَدْنَهُمْ وَإِنَّهُمْ لَيَقُولُونَ مُنْكَرًا مِنَ الْقَوْلِ وَزُورًا" ("Their mothers are none but those who gave birth to them, and they indeed utter an ill word and a lie!").

And if their goal in saying this was to separate from the woman—as was the case in the Age of Ignorance, where they used it instead of divorce—separating from a wife does not require such an ugly and offensive statement. Can the matter of separation not be expressed with a proper expression?

Some commentators have said that Zihar in the Age of Ignorance did not cause the separation of the man and woman from each other, but rather left the woman in a state of absolute limbo. If this is the case, the criminal nature of this act becomes even clearer, for by uttering a meaningless phrase, he would completely forbid marital relations with his wife upon himself without her being divorced. [^1]

Then it addresses the third ignorant superstition, saying: "Nor has He made your adopted sons your [real] sons" (وَمَا جَعَلَ أَدْعِيَاءَكُمْ أَبْنَاءَكُمْ).

To explain: in the Age of Ignorance, it was customary for some to choose children as their own sons and call them their sons. Following this naming, they would grant them all the rights a son had from a father; he would inherit from his adoptive father, and the adoptive father would inherit from him, and the prohibition of marrying the father's wife or the son's wife applied to them.

Islam severely rejected these illogical and superstitious regulations. As we shall see, the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله), in order to crush this false tradition, married the wife of his adopted son, "Zayd ibn Harithah," after she was divorced from Zayd, to make it clear that these hollow words cannot change realities. For the relationship of fatherhood and sonship is a natural relationship and is never achieved through words, contracts, or slogans.

Although we will mention later that the Prophet's marriage to the divorced wife of Zayd caused a great uproar among the enemies of Islam and became a pretext for their malicious propaganda, these uproars were worth the crushing of this ignorant tradition.

Therefore, the Quran adds after this sentence: "That is [only] your saying with your mouths" (ذَلِكُمْ قَوْلُكُمْ بِأَفْوَاهِكُمْ).

You say so-and-so is my son, while in your hearts you certainly know it is not so. These sound waves only swirl in the space of your mouths and exit; they never originate from a heart-felt belief.

These are nothing but vain words, "but Allah says the truth, and He guides to the [right] way" (وَاللَّهُ يَقُولُ الْحقَّ وَهُوَ يَهْدِي السَّبِيلَ).

"Truthful speech" is said of speech that corresponds with objective reality, or if it is a conventional matter, it is harmonious with the interests of all parties involved. We know that the distasteful issue of Zihar in the Age of Ignorance, or "adoption" which largely trampled the rights of other children, had neither objective reality nor was it a convention that preserved the public interest.

Then, for further emphasis and to clarify the correct and logical line of Islam, the Quran adds: "Call them by [the names of] their fathers; that is more just in the sight of Allah" (ادْعُوهُمْ لِآبَائِهِمْ هُوَ أَقْسَطُ عِنْدَ اللَّهِ).

The expression "more just" (aqsatu) does not imply that calling them by the names of their adoptive fathers is "just" and calling them by their real fathers is "more just." Rather, as we have often stated, the "superlative form" (af'al al-tafdil) is sometimes used in cases where the quality does not exist in the opposite side at all. For example, it is said: "It is better for a person to be cautious and not endanger his life." This does not mean that endangering one's life is "good" but caution is "better"; rather, the purpose is to compare "good" and "bad" with each other.

And to remove any excuses, it adds: "But if you do not know their fathers, then they are your brothers in religion and your mawali (proteges)" (فَإِنْ لَمْ تَعْلَمُوا آبَاءَهُمْ فَإِخْوَانُكُمْ فِي الدِّينِ وَمَوَالِيكُمْ).

That is, not knowing their fathers is no reason to place another person's name on them as "father"; rather, you can address them as brothers in religion or as friends and allies.

"Mawali" is the plural of "mawla," and commentators have mentioned several meanings for it. Some have considered it here to mean "friend," and some to mean "freed slave" (because some adopted sons were slaves who were purchased, then freed, and because they were favored by their masters, they were called their sons).

It is also necessary to note that the expression "mawla" in such cases where the party was a freed slave was because after being freed, they maintained their relationship with their owner—a relationship that, from a legal standpoint in some aspects, substituted for kinship and was referred to as "wala' 'itq."

Thus, we read in Islamic traditions that "Zayd ibn Harithah," after the Prophet freed him, was called "Zayd ibn Muhammad" until the Quran was revealed and brought the above command. From then on, the Prophet said to him: "You are Zayd ibn Harithah," and people called him "Mawla Rasul Allah" [^1]. It is also said that "Abu Hudhayfah" had a slave named "Salim"; he freed him and called him his son. When the above verse was revealed, they called him "Salim, the mawla of Abu Hudhayfah." [^2]

However, since sometimes a person, due to past habit, a slip of the tongue, or a mistake in identifying a person's lineage, might attribute someone to other than his father, and this is outside the realm of human choice, the Just and Wise Allah will not punish such a person. Therefore, at the end of the verse, it adds: "And there is no blame upon you for that in which you have erred" (وَلَيْسَ عَلَيْكُمْ جُنَاحٌ فِيمَا أَخْطَأْتُمْ بِهِ).

"But [there is blame for] what your hearts intended" (وَلَكِنْ مَا تَعَمَّدَتْ قُلُوبُكُمْ) [^3] "and Allah is ever Forgiving and Merciful" (وَكَانَ اللَّهُ غَفُورًا رَحِيمًا).

He forgives you for the past; He pardons oversight, forgetfulness, mistakes, and errors. But if, after the revelation of this ruling, you oppose it intentionally and by choice, calling individuals by names other than their fathers' and continuing the incorrect custom of "adoption," Allah will not forgive you.

Some commentators have said: the subject of "error" includes cases where a person says to someone out of affection: "My son!" or out of respect says: "My father!" Certainly, it is correct that these expressions are not sins, but not because of the title of "error," but because these expressions are metaphorical and figurative, and usually their context accompanies them. The Quran rejects literal expressions in this regard, not metaphorical ones.

The next verse addresses another important issue, namely the invalidation of the system of "Brotherhood" (Mu'akhat).

To explain: when the Muslims migrated from Makkah to Madinah and Islam completely severed their ties and relationships with their polytheist relatives who were in Makkah, the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله), by Divine command, established the system of the bond of brotherhood among them.

In this way, a covenant of brotherhood was concluded between the "Migrants" (Muhajirun) and the "Helpers" (Ansar) (two by two), and they inherited from each other just like two real brothers. However, this was a temporary ruling specific to this extraordinary situation. When Islam expanded and past connections were gradually restored, there was no longer a necessity to continue this ruling.

The above verse was revealed and invalidated the "system of brotherhood" in a way that it would substitute for lineage, and it restricted the ruling of inheritance and the like to real relatives.

Therefore, the system of brotherhood, although it was an Islamic system (unlike the system of adoption which was an ignorant one), had to be invalidated after the extraordinary situation was resolved, and so it was.

However, in the verse under discussion, before mentioning this point, two other rulings—namely "the priority of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) over the believers" and "the wives of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) being as mothers"—are mentioned as a preface:

He says: "The Prophet is closer to the believers than their own selves" (النَّبِيُّ أَوْلَى بِالْمُؤْمِنِينَ مِنْ أَنْفُسِهِمْ).

"And his wives are their mothers" (وَأَزْوَاجُهُ أُمَّهَاتُهُمْ).

Despite the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) being as a father and his wives as mothers to the believers, they never inherit from them; how then can one expect adopted sons to become heirs?

Then He adds: "And blood relatives are closer to one another in the decree of Allah than the [other] believers and the Migrants" (وَأُولُو الْأَرْحَامِ بَعْضُهُمْ أَوْلَى بِبَعْضٍ فِي كِتَابِ اللَّهِ مِنَ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ وَالْمُهاجِرِينَ).

Yet, in order not to completely close the way for Muslims to leave something for friends and those they care about—even if through a will regarding one-third of the property—He adds at the end of the verse: "Except that you may do a kindness to your friends" (إِلَّا أَنْ تَفْعَلُوا إِلَى أَوْلِيَائِكُمْ مَعْرُوفًا); this is not prohibited.

And in the final sentence, to emphasize all the previous rulings or the last ruling, He says: "This has been written in the Book [of Allah]" (كَانَ ذَلِكَ فِي الْكِتَابِ مَسْطُورًا).

This was a summary of the commentary on the above verse. Now we must elaborate on each of the four rulings mentioned in this verse:

A- What is meant by the Prophet's priority over the believers?

The Quran in this verse mentions the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله) priority over the Muslims in an absolute sense, and its meaning is that in all the authority that a "human" has over himself, the "Prophet" (صلى الله عليه وآله) is more entitled than the person himself.

Although some commentators have interpreted it as the matter of "managing social affairs," or "priority in the matter of judgment," or "obedience to command," in reality, we have no reason to restrict it to one of these three matters.

And if we see in some Islamic traditions that priority is interpreted as the matter of "governance," it is in fact the expression of one of the branches of this priority. [^1] Therefore, it must be said: the Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله) is more entitled than any human being over himself, both in social and individual/private matters, both in matters related to governance and judgment and invitation; and his will and desire take precedence over the person's own will and desire.

One should not be surprised by this, for the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) is infallible and, as the representative of God, considers nothing but the good and welfare of the society and the individual. He never follows whims and desires, and he never prioritizes his own interests over others; rather, on the contrary, his program in the conflict of interests is always altruism and sacrifice for the Ummah.

This priority is, in fact, a branch of the priority of the Divine Will, for whatever we have is from Allah.

In addition to this, man can only reach the peak of faith when his strongest affection—namely the love for his own self—is overshadowed by the love for the Essence of Allah and His representatives.

Thus, in a tradition, we read that he said: "None of you believes until his desire follows what I have brought." [^1]

And also in another tradition, it is stated: "By Him in Whose hand is my soul, none of you believes until I am more beloved to him than himself, his wealth, his children, and all of mankind." [^2]

And again, it is narrated from the Prophet himself that he said: "There is no believer except that I am the closest of people to him in this world and the Hereafter." [^3]

The Quran also says in verse 36 of this same Surah (Al-Ahzab): "مَا كَانَ لِمُؤْمِنٍ وَلَا مُؤْمِنَةٍ إِذَا قَضَى اللَّهُ وَرَسُولُهُ أَمْرًا أَنْ يَكُونَ لَهُمُ الْخِيَرَةُ مِنْ أَمْرِهِمْ" ("It is not for a believing man or a believing woman, when Allah and His Messenger have decided a matter, that they should [thereafter] have any choice about their affair").

We emphasize again that this statement does not mean that Allah has surrendered His servants entirely to the inclinations of an individual; rather, considering that the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) possesses the station of infallibility and according to "مَا يَنْطِقُ عَنِ الْهَوَى إِنْ هُوَ إِلَّا وَحْيٌ يُوحَى" (An-Najm: 3-4), whatever he says is the word of Allah and is from His side, and he is even more compassionate and kind than a father.

This priority is, in fact, in the path of the people's interests, both in the aspects of governance and management of the Islamic society, and in personal and individual matters.

[^1]: These traditions are found in Usul al-Kafi and the book 'Ilal al-Shara'i' (refer to Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, vol. 4, pp. 238-239). [^1] and [^2]: Tafsir Fi Zilal under the verses discussed. [^3]: Sahih Bukhari, vol. 6, p. 145 (Tafsir Surah Al-Ahzab) and Musnad Ahmad ibn Hanbal, vol. 2, p. 334.

For this reason, it often happens that this priority places heavy responsibilities on the Prophet's shoulders. Thus, in a famous narration found in both Shi'a and Sunni sources, we read: The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) said: "I am closer to every believer than his own self; whoever leaves behind wealth, it is for his heirs, and whoever leaves behind a debt or a family in need (diya'an), then it is to me and upon me." [^1] (It should be noted that "diya'" here means children or a family left without a guardian, and the mention of "debt" before it is a clear context for this meaning, as it refers to having debt without assets).

B- The second ruling is regarding the wives of the Prophet, that they are considered as mothers for all believers—of course, spiritual and metaphorical mothers, just as the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) is the spiritual and metaphorical father of the Ummah.

This spiritual connection and bond's only effect was the matter of "maintaining respect" and the "prohibition of marriage" with the Prophet's wives, as the explicit ruling of the prohibition of marrying them after the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله) passing appears in the verses of this same Surah. Otherwise, in terms of the matter of inheritance and also other "lineal" and "affinal" prohibitions, it has not the slightest effect; meaning, Muslims had the right to marry the Prophet's daughters, while no one can marry the daughter of his own mother. Also, the matter of mahramiyat and looking at the Prophet's wives was not permissible for anyone except their mahrams.

[^1]: In Wasa'il al-Shi'ah, vol. 17, p. 551, this statement is narrated from Imam al-Sadiq (عليه السلام) from the Noble Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله), and the same content with slight differences appears in Tafsir al-Qurtubi and Ruh al-Ma'ani under the verses discussed; it also appears in Sahih Bukhari, vol. 6, p. 145.

In a tradition, we read that a woman said to Aisha: "Ya ummah!" ("O mother!"). Aisha replied: "I am not your mother; I am only the mother of your men!" [^1]—referring to the fact that the purpose of this expression is the prohibition of marriage, and this applies only to the men of the Ummah.

However, as we said, besides the matter of marriage, the subject of respect and veneration is also intended; therefore, Muslim women could also address the Prophet's wives as their mother out of respect.

The evidence for this is that the Quran says: "النَّبِيُّ أَوْلَى بِالْمُؤْمِنِينَ مِنْ أَنْفُسِهِمْ"—the Prophet's priority is over all women and men, and the pronoun of the next sentence also refers back to this same title, which has a broad meaning. Thus, in a statement narrated from "Umm Salamah" (another of the Prophet's wives), we read that she says: "I am the mother of your men and women." [^2]

A question arises here: does the expression "أَزْوَاجُهُ أُمَّهَاتُهُمْ" ("his wives are the mothers of the believers") contradict what was mentioned a few verses earlier? For there it says: "Those who sometimes place their wives as their mothers [by Zihar] speak a false word; their mother is only the one who gave birth to them." Given this, how are the Prophet's wives, from whom the Muslims were not born, considered mothers?

In response to this question, one must note that addressing a woman as mother must be either from a physical or a spiritual perspective. From a physical perspective, this meaning is only real if the person was born from her, and this is what appeared in the previous verses—that the physical mother of a person is only the one who gave birth to him. But a spiritual father or mother is someone who has a kind of spiritual right over him, such as the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) who is considered the spiritual father of the Ummah, and for his sake, his wives have the respect of a mother.

The criticism directed at the pagan Arabs regarding Zihar was that when they called their wives "mother," they certainly did not mean a spiritual mother; rather, they meant that they were as physical mothers. Therefore, they considered it a type of divorce, and we know that a physical mother is not created by words, but rather its condition is physical birth. Therefore, their speech was an ill word, a lie, and false.

But in the case of the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله) wives, although they are not physical mothers, they are spiritual mothers out of respect for the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) and they hold the respect of a mother.

And if we see that the Quran in future verses has prohibited marriage with the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله) wives, that too is one of the aspects of respecting them and respecting the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله), as its explanation will come in detail, God willing.

Of course, a third type of mother exists in Islam as a foster mother (radha'i), which is mentioned in verse 23 of Surah An-Nisa ("وَأُمَّهَاتُكُمُ اللَّاتِي أَرْضَعْنَكُمْ..."), but that is in fact one of the branches of the physical mother.

C- The third ruling is the matter of the priority of relatives regarding inheritance over others. In the beginning of Islam, when Muslims had severed their ties with their relatives due to migration, the law of inheritance was organized based on "migration" and "brotherhood"; that is, the Migrants inherited from each other, or from the Helpers with whom they had established a bond of brotherhood. This was a temporary ruling that, with the expansion of Islam and the restoration of many past kinship ties due to their conversion to Islam, no longer required continuation (note that Surah Al-Ahzab was revealed in the fifth year of the Hijrah, the year of the Battle of the Trench).

Therefore, the above verse was revealed and established the priority of Ulul Arham (relatives) over others.

Of course, there are indications that the "priority" here is an obligatory priority, not a recommended one, for both the consensus of Islamic scholars is upon this meaning, and many traditions found in Islamic sources prove this subject.

Here, one must also carefully note that this verse is intended to state the priority of relatives over strangers, not the priority of the three classes of inheritance over each other. In other words, here the "those over whom priority is given" (mufadhdhal 'alayhim) are the believers and the Migrants mentioned in the text of the Quran (مِنَ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ وَالْمُهاجِرِينَ).

Therefore, the meaning of the verse becomes: "Relatives, in terms of inheriting from one another, are more entitled than non-relatives." As for how relatives inherit and based on what criteria and regulations? The Quran is silent here regarding this meaning, although it has discussed it in detail in the verses of Surah An-Nisa. [^1]

D- The fourth ruling, which appears as an exception in the above verse, is the matter of

[^1]: Therefore, the argument of some jurists using this expression for the priority of the classes of inheritance over each other does not seem correct. It seems the letter "bi" in "أَوْلَى بِبَعْضٍ" caused such a mistake, and they imagined the "one over whom priority is given" here is that same "some" (ba'dh), while the Quran explicitly brings the "one over whom priority is given" as "مِنَ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ وَالْمُهاجِرِينَ." Yes, the expression "أُولُو الْأَرْحَامِ" alone can suggest that the criterion for inheritance is kinship, and the higher the degree of kinship, the more the right of precedence (reflect on this).

benefiting friends and loved ones from the wealth one leaves behind, which is expressed with the phrase: "إِلَّا أَنْ تَفْعَلُوا إِلَى أَوْلِيَائِكُمْ مَعْرُوفًا" ("Except that you may do a kindness to your friends"). Its clear instance is the ruling of the will (wasiyyah), which a person can perform regarding one-third of his wealth for whomever he wishes.

In this way, when the foundation of inheritance was placed on kinship and replaced past bonds, man's connection is still not completely severed from his beloved friends and Muslim brothers; however, in terms of quality and quantity, it depends on the person's own will, but in any case, it is conditioned upon not exceeding one-third of the wealth. Of course, if a person refrains from making a will, all his wealth will be divided among his relatives according to the law of inheritance, and no third will be considered for him. [^1]

Key Point

Many traditions from the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (عليه السلام) have been narrated regarding the interpretation of the above verse concerning "أُولُو الْأَرْحَامِ." In some of them, this verse is interpreted as the "inheritance of wealth," as is well-known among commentators, while in another part, it is interpreted as the "inheritance of the Caliphate and Governance" within the family of the Prophet and the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (عليه السلام).

Among these, in a tradition from Imam al-Sadiq (عليه السلام), we read that when they asked about the interpretation of this verse, the Imam (عليه السلام) said: "This was revealed regarding the children of Husayn (عليه السلام)." When the narrator asked: "Is this not about the inheritance of wealth?" the Imam said: "No, it is about governance and authority (wilayah)." [^1]

It is obvious that the purpose of these hadiths is not to negate the matter of the inheritance of wealth; rather, the purpose is to draw attention to the point that "inheritance" has a broad meaning that includes both the inheritance of wealth and the inheritance of the Caliphate and Authority.

This inheritance has no similarity to the inheritance of monarchy in dynasties of kings; here it is an inheritance due to merits and capabilities. Therefore, among the children of the Imams, it only included those who possessed this merit, exactly like what Ibrahim (عليه السلام) asked of God for his children, and God said to him: "My covenant [of Imamate and Authority] does not reach the wrongdoers among your children, but is specific to the pure among them."

It is also similar to what we say in the visitations (ziyarat) before the graves of the martyrs in the way of Allah, including before the grave of Imam Husayn (عليه السلام): "Peace be upon you, O Husayn, who are the heir of Adam, the heir of Nuh, the heir of Ibrahim, and the heir of Musa, 'Isa, and Muhammad..."—which is an inheritance in the aspects of belief, morality, spirituality, and the soul.

[^1]: A group of commentators believe that the exception in the sentence "إِلَّا أَنْ تَفْعَلُوا..." is a "disconnected exception" (istithna' munqati'), because the ruling of "will" is separate from the ruling of "inheritance." But in our opinion, there is no obstacle to the exception being "connected" (muttasil) here, for the sentence "وَأُولُو الْأَرْحَامِ..." is evidence that relatives are more entitled than strangers regarding the wealth left by the deceased, except in the case where he has made a will; in that case, the "legatee" (musa lahu) is more entitled than the relatives within the limit of the third. This is in fact similar to the exceptions that appear in the verses of inheritance in the form of "مِنْ بَعْدِ وَصِيَّةٍ..." ("after any bequest..."). [^1]: These hadiths were narrated by the late Sayyid Hashim al-Bahrani in Tafsir al-Burhan, vol. 3, pp. 292-293, including the above hadith and hadith 16 from this series of traditions.

Ayahs

وَإِذْ أَخَذْنَا مِنَ ٱلنَّبِيِّـۧنَ مِيثَـٰقَهُمْ وَمِنكَ وَمِن نُّوحٍ وَإِبْرَٰهِيمَ وَمُوسَىٰ وَعِيسَى ٱبْنِ مَرْيَمَ ۖ وَأَخَذْنَا مِنْهُم مِّيثَـٰقًا غَلِيظًا (٧)
[Recall] when We took a pledge from the prophets, and from you and from Noah and Abraham and Moses and Jesus son of Mary, and We took from them a solemn pledge, (7)
لِّيَسْـَٔلَ ٱلصَّـٰدِقِينَ عَن صِدْقِهِمْ ۚ وَأَعَدَّ لِلْكَـٰفِرِينَ عَذَابًا أَلِيمًا (٨)
so that He may question the truthful concerning their truthfulness. And He has prepared for the faithless a painful punishment. (8)

Commentary

The Divine Firm Covenant

Since the previous verses explained the vast and extensive authority of the Prophet of Islam (peace be upon him and his family) under the title "The Prophet is closer to the believers than their own selves," the verses under discussion describe the heavy duties of the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family) and other great prophets. This is because we know that authority is always coupled with responsibility; wherever there is a "right," there is a corresponding "duty," and these two are never separated from one another. Therefore, if the Prophet of Islam (peace be upon him and his family) holds extensive rights, a heavy duty and responsibility have also been placed upon him in return.

First, He says: "And remember when We took from the prophets their covenant, and from you, and from Noah, Abraham, Moses, and Jesus son of Mary; and We took from them a solemn and firm covenant" (وَ إِذْ أَخَذْنا مِنَ النَّبِيِّينَ مِيثاقَهُمْ وَ مِنْكَ وَ مِنْ نُوحٍ وَ إِبْراهِيمَ وَ مُوسى‌ وَ عِيسَى ابْنِ مَرْيَمَ وَ أَخَذْنا مِنْهُمْ مِيثاقاً غَلِيظاً).

In this manner, He first mentions all the prophets regarding the issue of the covenant, then names the five Ulul-'Azm (Arch-Prophets), starting with the Prophet of Islam himself due to his honor and greatness, followed by the other four Ulul-'Azm prophets in chronological order of their appearance (Noah, Abraham, Moses, and Jesus عليه السلام).

This indicates that the aforementioned covenant was a universal one taken from all prophets, although the Ulul-'Azm prophets were more emphatically committed to this covenant—a covenant whose extraordinary emphasis is expressed by the phrase "We took from them a solemn (thick/firm) covenant" (أَخَذْنا مِنْهُمْ مِيثاقاً غَلِيظاً). [^1] It is important to know what this solemn covenant was that all prophets bore. Here, commentators have various views, all of which can be considered different branches of one general principle: fulfilling the responsibility of propagation, the message, leadership, and guiding the people in all fields and dimensions.

They were duty-bound to invite all human beings, above all else, toward Monotheism (Tawhid).

They were also duty-bound to confirm one another; the earlier prophets were to prepare their nations to accept the subsequent prophets, just as the later prophets were to testify to and emphasize the call of the preceding ones.

[^1]: Mithaq—as Raghib states in Al-Mufradat—means a firm covenant accompanied by an oath and a promise; therefore, the mention of ghaliza (thick/solemn) in the verse is an added emphasis beyond this meaning.

In short, the call of all should be toward one direction, all should propagate one truth, and they should gather the nations under a single banner.

Evidence for this can also be found in other verses of the Quran. In Surah Al-Imran, verse 81, we read: وَ إِذْ أَخَذَ اللَّهُ مِيثاقَ النَّبِيِّينَ لَما آتَيْتُكُمْ مِنْ كِتابٍ وَ حِكْمَةٍ ثُمَّ جاءَكُمْ رَسُولٌ مُصَدِّقٌ لِما مَعَكُمْ لَتُؤْمِنُنَّ بِهِ وَ لَتَنْصُرُنَّهُ قالَ أَ أَقْرَرْتُمْ وَ أَخَذْتُمْ عَلى‌ ذلِكُمْ إِصْرِي قالُوا أَقْرَرْنا قالَ فَاشْهَدُوا وَ أَنَا مَعَكُمْ مِنَ الشَّاهِدِينَ:

"(And remember) when Allah took the firm covenant of the prophets (and their followers): 'Whatever I give you of the Book and wisdom and then there comes to you a messenger confirming what is with you, you shall believe in him and you shall help him.' He said: 'Do you agree and take this My covenant as binding on you?' They said: 'We agree.' He said: 'Then bear witness, and I am with you among the witnesses!'" A similar meaning appears in Surah Al-Imran, verse 187, where it explicitly states that Allah took a covenant from the People of the Book to clarify the divine verses for the people and never to conceal them.

Thus, Allah has taken a solemn covenant from the prophets to invite people to the Monotheism of Allah and the unity of the true religion and heavenly faiths, and also from the scholars of the People of the Book to strive as much as they can in clarifying the divine religion and to refrain from concealment.

The next verse explains the purpose of the appointment of the prophets and the solemn covenant taken from them as follows: "That He may question the truthful about their truth, and He has prepared for the disbelievers a painful punishment" (لِيَسْئَلَ الصَّادِقِينَ عَنْ صِدْقِهِمْ وَ أَعَدَّ لِلْكافِرِينَ عَذاباً أَلِيماً).

Regarding who is meant by "the truthful" (al-sadiqin) here and what kind of questioning this is, commentators have provided many interpretations. However, what seems consistent with the verses of this Surah and other verses of the Quran is that it refers to the believers who have proven the truth of their claim through action—in other words, those who have emerged victorious from the field of divine trial and examination.

The evidence for this is:

First, "the truthful" here is placed in contrast to "the disbelievers," and this meaning is clearly understood from the context of opposition.

Second, in verse 23 of this very Surah (Al-Ahzab), we read: مِنَ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ رِجالٌ صَدَقُوا ما عاهَدُوا اللَّهَ عَلَيْهِ: "Among the believers are men who have been true to the covenant they made with Allah." Immediately in verse 24, He says: لِيَجْزِيَ اللَّهُ الصَّادِقِينَ بِصِدْقِهِمْ وَ يُعَذِّبَ الْمُنافِقِينَ إِنْ شاءَ أَوْ يَتُوبَ عَلَيْهِمْ: "That Allah may reward the truthful for their truth, and punish the hypocrites if He wills or accept their repentance."

Third, in Surah Al-Hujurat verse 15 and Surah Al-Hashr verse 8, "the truthful" are well-defined. In Al-Hujurat we read: إِنَّمَا الْمُؤْمِنُونَ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا بِاللَّهِ وَ رَسُولِهِ ثُمَّ لَمْ يَرْتابُوا وَ جاهَدُوا بِأَمْوالِهِمْ وَ أَنْفُسِهِمْ فِي سَبِيلِ اللَّهِ أُولئِكَ هُمُ الصَّادِقُونَ: "The true believers are only those who believe in Allah and His Messenger and then do not doubt, and strive with their wealth and their lives in the cause of Allah; it is they who are the truthful."

And in Surah Al-Hashr, He says: لِلْفُقَراءِ الْمُهاجِرِينَ الَّذِينَ أُخْرِجُوا مِنْ دِيارِهِمْ وَ أَمْوالِهِمْ يَبْتَغُونَ فَضْلًا مِنَ اللَّهِ وَ رِضْواناً وَ يَنْصُرُونَ اللَّهَ وَ رَسُولَهُ أُولئِكَ هُمُ الصَّادِقُونَ: "(The spoils are) for the poor emigrants who were expelled from their homes and their properties, seeking bounty from Allah and His pleasure, and helping Allah and His Messenger; it is they who are the truthful."

Thus, it is clear that "the truthful" refers to those who have proven their sincerity and truthfulness in the field of supporting God's religion, in Jihad, in standing firm against difficulties, and in sacrificing life and wealth. [^1] As for what is meant by "questioning the truthful about their truth," based on what we said above, it is clear: the purpose is whether they prove the sincerity of their intention and the truth of their claim in their actions or not—in charity, in Jihad, in patience against hardships, and especially the rigors of the battlefield.

Where does this questioning take place? The apparent meaning of the verse is that it occurs on the Day of Resurrection in the court of Divine Justice. Several verses of the Quran also report the occurrence of such questioning on the Day of Resurrection in general. However, there is also the possibility that the questioning has a practical aspect and takes place in this world, for with the appointment of the prophets, all claimants of faith are put under question, and their actions are the answer to this question of whether they are truthful in their claim!

[^1]: A group of commentators has suggested another possibility for the meaning of the verse: that "the truthful" here refers to the prophets themselves, who will be questioned on the Day of Resurrection as to how much they fulfilled their covenant. However, the three pieces of evidence mentioned above refute this interpretation. It has also been suggested that it refers to both "prophets" and "believers," but the interpretation mentioned above is more consistent with the verses of this Surah and other verses of the Quran.

Ayahs

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ ٱذْكُرُوا۟ نِعْمَةَ ٱللَّهِ عَلَيْكُمْ إِذْ جَآءَتْكُمْ جُنُودٌ فَأَرْسَلْنَا عَلَيْهِمْ رِيحًا وَجُنُودًا لَّمْ تَرَوْهَا ۚ وَكَانَ ٱللَّهُ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ بَصِيرًا (٩)
O you who have faith! Remember Allah’s blessing upon you when the hosts came at you, and We sent against them a gale and hosts whom you did not see, and Allah sees best what you do. (9)
إِذْ جَآءُوكُم مِّن فَوْقِكُمْ وَمِنْ أَسْفَلَ مِنكُمْ وَإِذْ زَاغَتِ ٱلْأَبْصَـٰرُ وَبَلَغَتِ ٱلْقُلُوبُ ٱلْحَنَاجِرَ وَتَظُنُّونَ بِٱللَّهِ ٱلظُّنُونَا۠ (١٠)
When they came at you from above and below you, and when the eyes rolled [with fear] and the hearts leapt to the throats, and you entertained misgivings about Allah, (10)
هُنَالِكَ ٱبْتُلِىَ ٱلْمُؤْمِنُونَ وَزُلْزِلُوا۟ زِلْزَالًا شَدِيدًا (١١)
it was there that the faithful were tested and jolted with a severe agitation. (11)

Commentary

The Great Divine Trial in the Field of al-Ahzab

These verses, and several subsequent ones totaling seventeen verses, speak about one of the greatest Divine trials concerning the "believers" and the "hypocrites," testing the truth of their words through action—a subject discussed in the preceding verses.

These verses discuss one of the most significant events in Islamic history, namely the Battle of al-Ahzab (the Confederates). This battle was, in truth, a turning point in the history of Islam, shifting the balance of power between Islam and disbelief in favor of the Muslims. Victory in this battle was the key to the great future conquests; indeed, the back of the enemy was broken in this expedition, and thereafter they were unable to achieve anything significant.

The Battle of "al-Ahzab," as its name suggests, was an all-out struggle by the collective enemies of Islam and various groups whose illegitimate interests were endangered by the progress of this faith.

The first spark of the war was lit by a group from the Jewish tribe of "Bani Nadir," who went to Mecca and encouraged the "Quraysh" tribe to wage war against the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, promising to stand by them until the last breath. Then they went to the "Ghatafan" tribe and prepared them for combat as well.

These tribes also invited their allies, such as the "Bani Asad" and "Bani Sulaym" tribes. Since they all sensed the danger, they joined hands to settle the matter of Islam once and for all: to kill the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, crush the Muslims, loot Medina, and extinguish the light of Islam.

The Muslims, finding themselves facing this massive group, held a council by the command of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله. Before anything else, following the suggestion of Salman al-Farsi, they dug a trench around Medina so the enemy could not easily cross it and raid the city (for this reason, one of the names of this war is the Battle of the Trench/Khandaq).

Very difficult and dangerous moments passed for the Muslims; souls had reached the throats. The hypocrites within the army of Islam were in a state of intense agitation. The vast multitude of the enemy and the small number of the Islamic forces in comparison (the army of disbelief is recorded as ten thousand, while the army of Islam was three thousand) and their readiness in terms of military equipment and necessary provisions, visualized a harsh and painful future before the eyes of the Muslims.

But Allah willed that the final blow be struck against the body of disbelief here, to distinguish the ranks of the hypocrites from the ranks of the Muslims, to expose the conspirators, and to place the true Muslims firmly in the crucible of trial.

Ultimately, this expedition—as will be detailed—ended in victory for the Muslims. A fierce storm blew by the command of Allah, overturning the tents, camps, and livelihoods of the disbelievers, casting intense terror into their hearts, and He sent the unseen forces of the angels to aid the Muslims.

Wondrous displays of power, such as that of the Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام, against Amr ibn Abd Wudd, were added to this, and the polytheists fled without being able to accomplish anything.

The aforementioned seventeen verses were revealed, utilizing this important event in the most excellent way through crushing and exposing analyses for the final victory of Islam and the defeat of the hypocrites.

This was a brief overview of the Battle of al-Ahzab, which took place in the fifth year of the Hijra. [1] From here, we proceed to the commentary of the verses and leave the other details of this expedition to the discussion of the "Key Points."

The Quran first summarizes this event in one verse and then proceeds to explain its specifics in the following 16 verses.

It says: "O you who have believed, remember Allah's great favor upon you when (vast) armies came against you" (يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا اذْكُرُوا نِعْمَةَ اللَّهِ عَلَيْكُمْ إِذْ جَاءَتْكُمْ جُنُودٌ).

"But We sent against them a wind and a storm, and armies you did not see, and thereby We crushed them" (فَأَرْسَلْنَا عَلَيْهِمْ رِيحًا وَجُنُودًا لَمْ تَرَوْهَا).

"And Allah is All-Seeing of all that you do (and the actions each group performed in this great field)" (وَكَانَ اللَّهُ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ بَصِيرًا).

Here, several points are noteworthy:

  1. The expression "Remember" (اذْكُرُوا) indicates that these verses were revealed after the end of the war and the passage of some time, allowing the Muslims to analyze what they had seen in their minds, thereby creating a deeper impact.

  2. The term "Armies" (جنود) refers to the various pagan confederates (such as Quraysh, Ghatafan, Bani Sulaym, Bani Asad, Bani Fazara, Bani Ashja', and Bani Murrah) and the Jewish tribes inside Medina.

  3. The meaning of "Armies you did not see" (جُنُودًا لَمْ تَرَوْهَا) who came to the aid of the Muslims is the same

[1]: What was mentioned above is a summary of the detailed discussion provided by Islamic historians, including "Ibn al-Athir" in "Al-Kamil."

angels whose assistance to the believers in the Battle of Badr is also explicitly mentioned in the Holy Quran. However, as we explained under verse 9 of Surah al-Anfal, there is no evidence that the angels—these unseen Divine armies—officially entered the field and engaged in fighting. Rather, there are indications showing they descended to strengthen the morale of the believers and to encourage them. [1]

The next verse, which is a depiction of the critical situation of the Battle of al-Ahzab, the immense military power of the enemies, and the intense anxiety of many Muslims, says: "Remember when they came at you from above you and from below you (and placed Medina under siege), and when eyes shifted [in fear], and hearts reached the throats, and you were harboring various [ill] thoughts about Allah!" (إِذْ جَاءُوكُمْ مِنْ فَوْقِكُمْ وَمِنْ أَسْفَلَ مِنْكُمْ وَإِذْ زَاغَتِ الْأَبْصَارُ وَبَلَغَتِ الْقُلُوبُ الْحَنَاجِرَ وَتَظُنُّونَ بِاللَّهِ الظُّنُونَا).

Many commentators consider the word "above" (فوق) in this verse to refer to the eastern side of Medina, from where the "Ghatafan" tribe entered, and "below" (اسفل) to refer to the west, from where the "Quraysh" tribe and their companions entered.

Of course, considering that Mecca is located exactly to the south of Medina, the polytheist tribes of Mecca should have come from the south, but perhaps the condition of the road and the entrance to Medina was such that they circled the city slightly and entered from the west. In any case, the above sentence refers to the siege of this city by the various enemies of Islam.

The phrase "eyes shifted" (زَاغَتِ الْأَبْصَارُ), considering that "zaaghat" is from the root "zaygh" meaning

[1]: For further explanation in this regard, refer to Volume 7 of Tafsir Nemouneh, page 103 onwards.

to lean to one side, refers to the state a person experiences during intense fear and terror, where their eyes deviate to one side and become fixed and staring at a certain point.

The phrase "hearts reached the throats" (بَلَغَتِ الْقُلُوبُ الْحَنَاجِرَ) is an interesting metaphor similar to what we have in the Persian language when we say "his soul reached his lips" (جانش به لب رسید); otherwise, the heart—in the sense of the specific organ that is the center of blood distribution—never moves from its place and never reaches the throat.

The phrase "and you were harboring [various] thoughts about Allah" (وَتَظُنُّونَ بِاللَّهِ الظُّنُونَا) indicates that in this state, improper thoughts had arisen for a group of Muslims, because they had not yet reached the stage of perfection in terms of the strength of faith. These are the same people who, as the next verse says, were severely shaken.

Perhaps some thought: "We will eventually be defeated, and the enemy army with this power and strength will prevail; the final days of Islam's life have arrived, and the Prophet's promises regarding victory will absolutely not be fulfilled!" Of course, these thoughts did not manifest as a firm belief but as a whisper in the depths of some hearts. This is similar to what the Holy Quran mentions regarding the Battle of Uhud, saying: "And another group was anxious only about themselves, harboring thoughts about Allah that were untrue—thoughts of the Days of Ignorance!" (Surah Al-Imran, Verse 154).

Of course, the addressees in the verse under discussion are certainly the believers, and the phrase "O you who have believed" in the previous verse is clear evidence of this. Those who considered the addressees to be the hypocrites have seemingly not paid attention to this point or have imagined that such thoughts

are inconsistent with faith and Islam, whereas the emergence of such thoughts at the level of a whisper—especially in very difficult and critical conditions—is a natural occurrence for individuals with weak faith or those new to Islam! [1]

It was here that the furnace of Divine trial became intensely hot, as the next verse says: "There the believers were tested and shaken with a severe shaking" (هُنَالِكَ ابْتُلِيَ الْمُؤْمِنُونَ وَزُلْزِلُوا زِلْزَالًا شَدِيدًا).

It is natural that when a person is caught in mental storms, his body does not remain exempt from this storm and falls into agitation and trembling. We have often seen individuals with mental distress who, while sitting in their place, constantly shake, rub their hands together, and clearly manifest their agitation in their movements.

One of the evidences of this intense terror was what has been narrated: five famous Arab heroes, of whom Amr ibn Abd Wudd was the foremost, donned battle attire and entered the field with a particular pride, calling out "Is there any challenger?" Specifically, Amr ibn Abd Wudd was reciting war poetry (rajaz) and mocking Paradise and the Hereafter, saying: "Do you not say that your slain will be in Paradise? Does none of you have the longing to see Paradise?! But in the face of his roars, silence reigned over the army, and no one dared to confront him, except Ali ibn Abi Talib عليه السلام, who rose to the challenge and achieved a great victory for the Muslims, which will be detailed in the discussion of the "Key Points."

[1]: A group of commentators have taken "thoughts" (ظنون) here in a general sense, including both good and bad thoughts, but the existing context in this verse and the next verse shows that bad thoughts are intended.

Indeed, steel is placed in a hot furnace to become tempered; the early Muslims also had to be placed in the furnace of difficult events, especially expeditions like the Battle of al-Ahzab, to become tempered and resilient.

Ayahs

وَإِذْ يَقُولُ ٱلْمُنَـٰفِقُونَ وَٱلَّذِينَ فِى قُلُوبِهِم مَّرَضٌ مَّا وَعَدَنَا ٱللَّهُ وَرَسُولُهُۥٓ إِلَّا غُرُورًا (١٢)
When the hypocrites, as well as those in whose hearts is a sickness, were saying, ‘Allah and His Apostle did not promise us [anything] except delusion.’ (12)
وَإِذْ قَالَت طَّآئِفَةٌ مِّنْهُمْ يَـٰٓأَهْلَ يَثْرِبَ لَا مُقَامَ لَكُمْ فَٱرْجِعُوا۟ ۚ وَيَسْتَـْٔذِنُ فَرِيقٌ مِّنْهُمُ ٱلنَّبِىَّ يَقُولُونَ إِنَّ بُيُوتَنَا عَوْرَةٌ وَمَا هِىَ بِعَوْرَةٍ ۖ إِن يُرِيدُونَ إِلَّا فِرَارًا (١٣)
And when a group of them said, ‘O people of Yathrib! [This is] not a place for you, so go back!’ A group of them sought the Prophet’s permission [to leave the scene of battle], saying, ‘Our homes lie exposed [to the enemy],’ although they were not exposed. They only sought to flee. (13)
وَلَوْ دُخِلَتْ عَلَيْهِم مِّنْ أَقْطَارِهَا ثُمَّ سُئِلُوا۟ ٱلْفِتْنَةَ لَـَٔاتَوْهَا وَمَا تَلَبَّثُوا۟ بِهَآ إِلَّا يَسِيرًا (١٤)
Had they been invaded from its flanks and had they been asked to apostatize, they would have done so with only a mild hesitation, (14)
وَلَقَدْ كَانُوا۟ عَـٰهَدُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ مِن قَبْلُ لَا يُوَلُّونَ ٱلْأَدْبَـٰرَ ۚ وَكَانَ عَهْدُ ٱللَّهِ مَسْـُٔولًا (١٥)
though they had already pledged to Allah before that they would not turn their backs [to flee], and pledges given to Allah are accountable. (15)
قُل لَّن يَنفَعَكُمُ ٱلْفِرَارُ إِن فَرَرْتُم مِّنَ ٱلْمَوْتِ أَوِ ٱلْقَتْلِ وَإِذًا لَّا تُمَتَّعُونَ إِلَّا قَلِيلًا (١٦)
Say, ‘Flight will not avail you, if you flee from death, or from being killed, and then you will be let to enjoy only for a little while.’ (16)
قُلْ مَن ذَا ٱلَّذِى يَعْصِمُكُم مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ إِنْ أَرَادَ بِكُمْ سُوٓءًا أَوْ أَرَادَ بِكُمْ رَحْمَةً ۚ وَلَا يَجِدُونَ لَهُم مِّن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ وَلِيًّا وَلَا نَصِيرًا (١٧)
Say, ‘Who is it that can protect you from Allah if He desires to cause you harm or desires to grant you His mercy?’ Besides Allah they will not find for themselves any protector or helper. (17)

Commentary

The Hypocrites and the Weak-Faithful in the Scene of Al-Ahzab

We have noted that the furnace of the trial in the Battle of Al-Ahzab grew intense, and everyone became entangled in this great test. It is clear that in such crisis situations, people who seemingly stand in one rank under normal conditions are divided into various groups. Here too, the Muslims were divided into several factions: a group of true believers, a group of the elite among the faithful, a group of those weak in faith, a group of hypocrites, a group of stubborn and persistent hypocrites, a group concerned only with their homes and lives and thinking of flight, a group attempting to dissuade others from Jihad, and a group striving to strengthen their ties of alliance with the hypocrites.

In short, everyone revealed their inner secrets in this extraordinary resurrection and "Day of Manifestation."

In the preceding verses, there was mention of the masses of Muslims with weak faith and the whispers and ill-suspicions they grappled with. In the first verses under discussion, the dialogue of the hypocrites and those with diseased hearts is reflected.

He says: "And remember when the hypocrites and those in whose hearts is a disease said: 'Allah and His Messenger promised us nothing but delusions!'" (إِذْ يَقُولُ الْمُنافِقُونَ وَ الَّذِينَ فِي قُلُوبِهِمْ مَرَضٌ ما وَعَدَنَا اللَّهُ وَ رَسُولُهُ إِلَّا غُرُوراً).

It is recorded in the history of the Battle of Al-Ahzab that during the digging of the trench, while the Muslims were each busy excavating a section, they one day encountered a large, hard rock that no pickaxe could penetrate. They informed the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله. The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله personally entered the trench, stood by the rock, took a pickaxe, and struck the first heavy blow upon the core of the stone. A portion of it shattered and a spark flew out. The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله shouted the Takbir of victory, and all the Muslims shouted the Takbir as well.

A second time, he struck another heavy blow upon the stone; another part broke, and a spark flew out. The Prophet shouted the Takbir, and the Muslims followed suit.

Finally, the Prophet struck the third blow upon the stone, a spark flew, and the remainder of the rock shattered. The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله again shouted the Takbir, and the Muslims raised their voices in Takbir. Salman asked about this occurrence. The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله said:

"In the first spark, I saw the land of 'Hira' and the palaces of the Persian kings, and Gabriel gave me the glad tidings that my Ummah would be victorious over them! In the second spark, the red palaces of 'Syria and Rome' appeared, and Gabriel gave me the glad tidings that my Ummah would be victorious over them as well! In the third spark, I saw the palaces of 'Sana'a and Yemen,' and Gabriel again informed me that my Ummah would prevail over them. Glad tidings to you, O Muslims!"

The hypocrites looked at one another and said: "What strange words? What vain and baseless talk? From Medina, he sees the land of Hira and the palaces of Khosrow, and he tells you of their conquest, while right now you are trapped in the clutches of a handful of Arabs (taking a defensive stance) and cannot even go to the place of relieving yourselves (what a vain fantasy and raw delusion!)."

The above verse was revealed, stating that these hypocrites and those with diseased hearts say that Allah and His Prophet have promised us nothing but deception and lies. (They are ignorant of the infinite power of the Lord [1]). Indeed, on that day, such news and glad tidings seemed like nothing but deception and delusion except to the enlightened believers. However, the celestial vision of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, amidst the fiery sparks flying from the impact of the pickaxes striking the ground to dig the trench, could see the opening of the gates of the palaces of the kings of Persia, Rome, and Yemen, giving glad tidings to his devoted Ummah and unveiling the secrets of the future.

It perhaps needs no reminder that "those in whose hearts is a disease" (الَّذِينَ فِي قُلُوبِهِمْ مَرَضٌ) refers to the same hypocrites, and the mention of this phrase is actually an explanation for the word "hypocrites" that preceded it. And what disease is worse than the disease of hypocrisy?! For a healthy person possessing a divine nature has only one face; two-faced and multi-faced individuals are sick people who are constantly trapped in anxiety, contradiction, and inconsistency.

Evidence for this is found at the beginning of Surah Al-Baqarah, which says in describing the hypocrites: "In their hearts is a disease, so Allah has increased their disease (because of their actions)!" (Al-Baqarah, Verse 10).

In the next verse, describing the state of a dangerous group of these same diseased-hearted hypocrites who possessed more malice and pollution than others, He says: "And remember when a group of them said: 'O people of Yathrib! (Medina) There is no place for you to stay here, so return to your homes!'" (وَ إِذْ قالَتْ طائِفَةٌ مِنْهُمْ يا أَهْلَ يَثْرِبَ لا مُقامَ لَكُمْ فَارْجِعُوا). In short, they claimed that against this massive enemy, you can achieve nothing; withdraw yourselves from the fray and do not surrender yourselves to destruction and your wives and children to captivity.

In this way, they wanted to separate the Ansar from the army of Islam—this was on one hand.

On the other hand, "a group of these same hypocrites who had houses in Medina sought permission from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله to return, making excuses for their return, saying: 'Our houses are exposed (lacking proper walls and gates),' while it was not so; they only wanted to desert the scene and flee." (وَ يَسْتَأْذِنُ فَرِيقٌ مِنْهُمُ النَّبِيَّ يَقُولُونَ إِنَّ بُيُوتَنا عَوْرَةٌ وَ ما هِيَ بِعَوْرَةٍ إِنْ يُرِيدُونَ إِلَّا فِراراً).

The word "Awrah" (عورة) originally comes from the root "Ar" (عار), referring to something whose exposure causes shame. It is also applied to cracks appearing in clothing or the walls of a house, as well as vulnerable points on borders, and anything a person fears or dreads. Here, it refers to houses that do not have secure walls and doors, fearing an enemy attack upon them.

The "hypocrites," by raising these excuses, wanted to leave the battlefield and take refuge in their homes.

In a narration, it is stated that the tribe of "Bani Harithah" sent someone to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله saying: "Our houses are without protection, and none of the houses of the Ansar are like ours; there is no barrier between us and the tribe of 'Ghatafan' who have attacked from the east of Medina. Permit us to return to our homes and defend our women and children." The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله granted them permission. [1] This reached the ears of "Sa'd ibn Mu'adh," the leader of the Ansar. He said to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله: "Do not permit them. By Allah, whenever a difficulty has arisen for us, this group has brought forward this same excuse. They are lying." The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله then ordered them to return.

"Yathrib" is the ancient name of Medina before the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله migrated there. After that, it gradually became known as "Madinat al-Rasul" (The City of the Messenger), which was shortened to "Medina."

This city has several names. Sayyid Murtada (may Allah have mercy on him), in addition to these two names (Yathrib and Medina), mentioned eleven other names for this city, including "Tayyibah," "Tabah," "Sakinah," "Mahbubah," "Marhumah," and "Qasimah" (some call Yathrib the name of the land of this city). [1] In some narrations, it is stated that the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله said: "Do not call this city Yathrib," perhaps because Yathrib originally comes from the root "Tharb" (rhyming with Harb), meaning to blame or reproach, and the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله did not like such a name for this blessed city.

In any case, the fact that the hypocrites addressed the people of Medina as "O people of Yathrib" (يا أَهْلَ يَثْرِبَ) is not without reason. Perhaps it was because they knew the Prophet disliked this name, or they wanted to declare the non-recognition of Islam and the title "Madinat al-Rasul," or to direct them back to the era of Ignorance (Jahiliyyah)!

The next verse points to the weakness of faith in this group, saying: "They are so weak and unstable in their profession of Islam that if the enemies were to enter Medina from its sides and occupy the city militarily, and propose to them to return to the religion of polytheism and disbelief, they would accept it quickly, and would hesitate but a little in choosing this path!" (وَ لَوْ دُخِلَتْ عَلَيْهِمْ مِنْ أَقْطارِها ثُمَّ سُئِلُوا الْفِتْنَةَ لَآتَوْها وَ ما تَلَبَّثُوا بِها إِلَّا يَسِيراً).

It is evident that people who are so weak and spineless are neither ready to fight the enemy nor prepared for martyrdom in the way of Allah; they surrender quickly and change their course.

Therefore, the meaning of the word "Fitnah" here is polytheism and disbelief (as it appears in other verses of the Quran, such as Verse 193 of Surah Al-Baqarah).

However, some commentators have suggested that "Fitnah" here refers to fighting against the Muslims—that if this group of hypocrites were invited to it, they would soon answer the call and cooperate with the mischief-makers! But this interpretation does not align with the literal meaning of the phrase "if they were entered upon from its sides" (وَ لَوْ دُخِلَتْ عَلَيْهِمْ مِنْ أَقْطارِها). Perhaps for this reason, most commentators have chosen the first meaning.

Then the Quran brings this group of hypocrites to trial, saying: "And they had already made a covenant with Allah before that they would not turn their backs (to the enemy), and they would stand by their covenant in defending Monotheism, Islam, and the Prophet. Do they not know that the covenant with Allah will be questioned?" and they are responsible for it (وَ لَقَدْ كانُوا عاهَدُوا اللَّهَ مِنْ قَبْلُ لا يُوَلُّونَ الْأَدْبارَ وَ كانَ عَهْدُ اللَّهِ مَسْؤُلًا).

Some have said: This covenant refers to the commitment that the tribe of "Bani Harithah" made with Allah and the Prophet on the day of the Battle of Uhud, when they initially decided to retreat from the field and then regretted it; they pledged never to return to such actions. Yet, those same people thought of breaking their pledge again in the field of Al-Ahzab. [1] Some also consider it an indication of the pledge they made at the Battle of Badr or at the Aqaba before the Prophet's migration. [1] However, it seems that the above verse has a broad and comprehensive meaning that includes both these specific pledges and all their other covenants.

Fundamentally, anyone who believes and pledges allegiance to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله has made this covenant with Him: to defend Islam and the Quran to the limit of their lives.

The emphasis on the covenant here is because even the pagan Arabs respected the matter of covenants and pledges; how is it possible for someone, after claiming Islam, to trample upon their own pledge?

After Allah exposed the intention of the hypocrites—that their purpose was not to protect their homes but to flee the battlefield—He responds to them with two arguments.

First, He says to the Prophet: "Say: 'Flight will never benefit you if you flee from death or slaughter; and even then, you will not be granted enjoyment of the life of this world but for a little while'" (قُلْ لَنْ يَنْفَعَكُمُ الْفِرارُ إِنْ فَرَرْتُمْ مِنَ الْمَوْتِ أَوِ الْقَتْلِ وَ إِذاً لا تُمَتَّعُونَ إِلَّا قَلِيلًا).

Suppose you succeeded in fleeing; it is one of two cases: either your appointed time (Ajal) has come and your certain death has arrived—wherever you are, death will seize you, even in your homes and beside your wives and children, and an incident from within or without will end your life—or if your time has not yet come, you will live for a few days in this world accompanied by humiliation and disgrace, becoming captives in the hands of enemies and then facing divine punishment.

In reality, this statement is similar to what was revealed during the Battle of Uhud, addressed to another group of weak-willed hypocrites: "Say: 'Even if you had been in your houses, those for whom slaughter was decreed would have gone forth to their deathbeds!'" (Al-Imran, 154).

The second point is: Do you not know that your entire destiny is in the hand of Allah, and you can never escape the domain of His power and will? "O Prophet! Say to them: 'Who is it that can protect you from Allah if He intends for you an ill, or intends for you a mercy?'" (قُلْ مَنْ ذَا الَّذِي يَعْصِمُكُمْ مِنَ اللَّهِ إِنْ أَرادَ بِكُمْ سُوءاً أَوْ أَرادَ بِكُمْ رَحْمَةً).

Indeed, "they will not find for themselves besides Allah any protector or helper" (وَ لا يَجِدُونَ لَهُمْ مِنْ دُونِ اللَّهِ وَلِيًّا وَ لا نَصِيراً).

Therefore, now that all your destinies are in His hand, accept His command regarding Jihad—which is the source of honor and pride in this world and in the presence of Allah—with all your soul. And even if martyrdom comes to you in this path, welcome it with open arms.

[1] Al-Kamil ibn al-Athir, Vol. 2, p. 179. In Sirat Ibn Hisham, this same incident is mentioned with a slight difference: that the Prophet said: In the first spark I saw the conquest of Yemen, in the second the conquest of Syria and the West, and in the third victory over the East (the land of Persia), which aligns with the historical order of the conquest of these three regions. [1] Tafsir Fi Zilal, Vol. 6, p. 554. [1] Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 8, p. 346. [1] Tafsir al-Qurtubi and Tafsir Fi Zilal under the verses discussed. [1] Alusi in Ruh al-Bayan has narrated this view.

Ayahs

۞ قَدْ يَعْلَمُ ٱللَّهُ ٱلْمُعَوِّقِينَ مِنكُمْ وَٱلْقَآئِلِينَ لِإِخْوَٰنِهِمْ هَلُمَّ إِلَيْنَا ۖ وَلَا يَأْتُونَ ٱلْبَأْسَ إِلَّا قَلِيلًا (١٨)
Allah knows those of you who discourage others, and those who say to their brethren, ‘Come to us!’ and take little part in the battle, (18)
أَشِحَّةً عَلَيْكُمْ ۖ فَإِذَا جَآءَ ٱلْخَوْفُ رَأَيْتَهُمْ يَنظُرُونَ إِلَيْكَ تَدُورُ أَعْيُنُهُمْ كَٱلَّذِى يُغْشَىٰ عَلَيْهِ مِنَ ٱلْمَوْتِ ۖ فَإِذَا ذَهَبَ ٱلْخَوْفُ سَلَقُوكُم بِأَلْسِنَةٍ حِدَادٍ أَشِحَّةً عَلَى ٱلْخَيْرِ ۚ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ لَمْ يُؤْمِنُوا۟ فَأَحْبَطَ ٱللَّهُ أَعْمَـٰلَهُمْ ۚ وَكَانَ ذَٰلِكَ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ يَسِيرًا (١٩)
grudging you [their help]. So when there is panic, you see them looking at you, with their eyes rolling like someone fainting at death. Then, when the panic is over, they scald you with [their] sharp tongues in their greed for the spoils. They never have had faith. So Allah has made their works fail, and that is easy for Allah. (19)
