يَحْسَبُونَ ٱلْأَحْزَابَ لَمْ يَذْهَبُوا۟ ۖ وَإِن يَأْتِ ٱلْأَحْزَابُ يَوَدُّوا۟ لَوْ أَنَّهُم بَادُونَ فِى ٱلْأَعْرَابِ يَسْـَٔلُونَ عَنْ أَنۢبَآئِكُمْ ۖ وَلَوْ كَانُوا۟ فِيكُم مَّا قَـٰتَلُوٓا۟ إِلَّا قَلِيلًا (٢٠)
They suppose the confederates have not left yet, and were the confederates to come [again], they would wish they were in the desert with the Bedouins asking about your news, and if they were with you they would fight but a little. (20)

Commentary

The Group of Hinderers

Next, the Quran refers to the situation of another group of hypocrites who withdrew from the battlefield of the Battle of the Trench (Ahzab) and also invited others to withdraw, stating: "Allah knows those among you who hinder others from the war" (قَدْ يَعْلَمُ اللَّهُ الْمُعَوِّقِينَ مِنْكُمْ).

"And also those who said to their brothers, 'Come to us,'" and abandon this dangerous struggle! (وَ الْقائِلِينَ لِإِخْوانِهِمْ هَلُمَّ إِلَيْنا).

"Those who are not people of war and combat, and do not go to battle except a little"—and even that is out of reluctance or ostentation (وَ لا يَأْتُونَ الْبَأْسَ إِلَّا قَلِيلًا).

The word mu’awwiqin is derived from the root ‘awq (rhyming with shawq), meaning to hinder or divert someone from something. The word ba’s originally means intensity, and here it refers to "war."

The above verse likely refers to two groups: one group of hypocrites who were within the ranks of the Muslims (and the expression "among you" [منكم] is evidence of this) and tried to hinder the weak-of-faith Muslims from the war; these were the "hinderers" (mu’awwiqin).

The other group consisted of those sitting outside the scene, either hypocrites or Jews, who, when they encountered the soldiers of the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family), would say, "Come to us and pull yourselves out of this fray" (these are the ones referred to in the second phrase).

It is also possible that this verse describes two different states of a single group: those who, when among the people, hinder them from war, and when they withdraw, invite others to join them.

In a narration, we read that one of the companions of the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family) had come from the field of "Ahzab" into the city for a necessity. He saw his brother with bread, roasted meat, and wine set before him. He said, "You are busy here with pleasure while the Messenger of Allah is engaged in battle amidst swords and spears?!" His brother replied, "O fool! You too come sit with us and be happy! By the God whom Muhammad swears by, he will never return from this field! This massive army that has gathered will not leave him or his companions alive!" His brother replied, "You lie! By Allah, I will go and inform the Messenger of Allah (peace be upon him and his family) of what you said." He came to the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family) and related the incident, and at this point, the above verse was revealed.

Therefore, regarding the occasion of revelation, the term "their brothers" (اخوانهم) might mean actual brothers or those of the same path, just as in verse 27 of Surah Al-Isra, the squanderers are called the brothers of the devils (إِنَّ الْمُبَذِّرِينَ كانُوا إِخْوانَ الشَّياطِينِ).

In the next verse, it adds: "The motive for all these obstructions is that they are miserly toward you in everything" (أَشِحَّةً عَلَيْكُمْ) [^1]. Not only in sacrificing their lives on the battlefield but also in financial aid for providing war equipment, in physical help for digging the trench, and even in intellectual assistance, they show stinginess—a stinginess coupled with greed, and an ever-increasing greed!

After describing their stinginess and refusal of any kind of self-sacrifice, it proceeds to describe other characteristics of theirs, which are almost universal among all hypocrites in all ages and centuries, saying: "When moments of fear and crisis arise, they are so cowardly that you see them looking at you while their eyes roll in their sockets uncontrollably, like one who is in the throes of death!" (فَإِذا جاءَ الْخَوْفُ رَأَيْتَهُمْ يَنْظُرُونَ إِلَيْكَ تَدُورُ أَعْيُنُهُمْ كَالَّذِي يُغْشى‌ عَلَيْهِ مِنَ الْمَوْتِ).

Because they do not possess sound faith and have no firm pillar in life, when they face a severe incident, they completely lose control, as if their souls are being seized.

Then it adds: "But as soon as the storm subsides and they regain their normal state, they come to you with such high expectations as if they were the primary victors of the war; they shout like creditors and, with harsh and sharp words, demand their share of the spoils, being stingy, greedy, and grasping in that as well!" (فَإِذا ذَهَبَ الْخَوْفُ سَلَقُوكُمْ بِأَلْسِنَةٍ حِدادٍ أَشِحَّةً عَلَى الْخَيْرِ).

Salaqukum (سَلَقُوكُمْ) is from the root salaq (rhyming with khalaq), which originally means opening

[^1]: Ashihhah (اشحه) is the plural of shahih (شحيح) from the root shuhh (شح), meaning stinginess coupled with greed. According to most commentators, this word functions grammatically as a "circumstantial adverb" (hal), but this does not contradict it being a hal that explains the cause (reflect on this).

something with anger and irritation, whether it be opening the hand or the tongue. This expression is used for those who shout in a commanding and demanding tone to claim something.

Bi-alsinatin hidadin (بِأَلْسِنَةٍ حِدادٍ) means sharp and stinging tongues, and here it is a metaphor for harshness in speech.

At the end of the verse, it points to their final description, which is actually the root of all their misfortunes, saying: "They have never believed" (أُولئِكَ لَمْ يُؤْمِنُوا).

"And for this reason, Allah has rendered their deeds null and void," because their actions were never coupled with divine motive and sincerity (فَأَحْبَطَ اللَّهُ أَعْمالَهُمْ).

"And this is easy for Allah" (وَ كانَ ذلِكَ عَلَى اللَّهِ يَسِيراً).

In a summary, we conclude that the hinderers (mu’awwiqin) were hypocrites with these characteristics:

  1. They were never people of war, except to a very small extent.
  2. They were never people of sacrifice and devotion in terms of life or wealth and could not tolerate the slightest discomfort.
  3. In stormy and critical moments, they completely lost themselves out of intense fear.
  4. Upon victory, they considered themselves the heirs to all the honors!
  5. They were faithless individuals, and their deeds were worthless in the sight of Allah.

And such is the way and custom of hypocrites in every age and time, and in every society and group. What a precise description the Quran has given of them, by which one can recognize those of the same mindset—and how many examples of them we see with our own eyes in our own time!

The next verse is an even more vivid depiction of the state of cowardice and fear of this group; it says: "They are so terrified that even after the confederates and the enemy armies have dispersed, they imagine they have not yet gone!" (يَحْسَبُونَ الْأَحْزابَ لَمْ يَذْهَبُوا).

A terrifying nightmare has cast a shadow over their minds; it is as if the soldiers of disbelief are constantly parading before their eyes, unsheathing swords and aiming spears at them! These cowardly warriors, these faint-hearted hypocrites, are afraid of their own shadows; every sound of a horse they hear, every bray of a camel that reaches their ears, they writhe in fear, imagining that the armies of the confederates have returned!

Then it adds: "If the confederates were to return again, they would wish to head for the desert and be scattered and hidden among the desert-dwelling Arabs" (وَ إِنْ يَأْتِ الْأَحْزابُ يَوَدُّوا لَوْ أَنَّهُمْ بادُونَ فِي الْأَعْرابِ).

Yes, they would go and stay there "and constantly inquire about your news" (يَسْئَلُونَ عَنْ أَنْبائِكُمْ).

Moment by moment, they would ask every traveler for the latest news, lest the confederates have approached their region and their shadow fall upon the walls of their houses! And they would hold this over you as a favor, saying we were always inquiring about your condition!

And in the final sentence, it adds: "Even if they did not flee and were among you, they would not fight except a little" (وَ لَوْ كانُوا فِيكُمْ ما قاتَلُوا إِلَّا قَلِيلًا).

Neither be worried about their departure nor happy about their presence, for they are worthless and useless individuals, and their absence is better than their presence! Even this small amount of fighting is not for Allah; it is out of fear of people's reproach and blame, and for show and hypocrisy. For if it were for Allah, it would have no limit, and they would have stood in this field until their last breath.

Ayahs

لَّقَدْ كَانَ لَكُمْ فِى رَسُولِ ٱللَّهِ أُسْوَةٌ حَسَنَةٌ لِّمَن كَانَ يَرْجُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ وَٱلْيَوْمَ ٱلْـَٔاخِرَ وَذَكَرَ ٱللَّهَ كَثِيرًا (٢١)
There is certainly a good exemplar for you in the Apostle of Allah—for those who look forward to Allah and the Last Day, and remember Allah much. (21)
وَلَمَّا رَءَا ٱلْمُؤْمِنُونَ ٱلْأَحْزَابَ قَالُوا۟ هَـٰذَا مَا وَعَدَنَا ٱللَّهُ وَرَسُولُهُۥ وَصَدَقَ ٱللَّهُ وَرَسُولُهُۥ ۚ وَمَا زَادَهُمْ إِلَّآ إِيمَـٰنًا وَتَسْلِيمًا (٢٢)
But when the faithful saw the confederates, they said, ‘This is what Allah and His Apostle had promised us, and Allah and His Apostle were true.’ And it only increased them in faith and submission. (22)
مِّنَ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ رِجَالٌ صَدَقُوا۟ مَا عَـٰهَدُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ عَلَيْهِ ۖ فَمِنْهُم مَّن قَضَىٰ نَحْبَهُۥ وَمِنْهُم مَّن يَنتَظِرُ ۖ وَمَا بَدَّلُوا۟ تَبْدِيلًا (٢٣)
Among the faithful are men who fulfill what they have pledged to Allah: there are some among them who have fulfilled their pledge, and some of them who still wait, and they have not changed in the least, (23)
لِّيَجْزِىَ ٱللَّهُ ٱلصَّـٰدِقِينَ بِصِدْقِهِمْ وَيُعَذِّبَ ٱلْمُنَـٰفِقِينَ إِن شَآءَ أَوْ يَتُوبَ عَلَيْهِمْ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ كَانَ غَفُورًا رَّحِيمًا (٢٤)
that Allah may reward the true for their truthfulness, and punish the hypocrites, if He wishes, or accept their repentance. Indeed Allah is all-forgiving, all-merciful. (24)
وَرَدَّ ٱللَّهُ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ بِغَيْظِهِمْ لَمْ يَنَالُوا۟ خَيْرًا ۚ وَكَفَى ٱللَّهُ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ ٱلْقِتَالَ ۚ وَكَانَ ٱللَّهُ قَوِيًّا عَزِيزًا (٢٥)
Allah sent back the faithless in their rage, without their attaining any advantage, and Allah spared the faithful of fighting, and Allah is all-strong, all-mighty. (25)

Commentary

The Role of True Believers in the Battle of al-Ahzab

Thus far, various groups and their agendas during the Battle of "al-Ahzab" (the Confederates) have been discussed, including those weak in faith, the hypocrites, the leaders of disbelief and hypocrisy, and those who discouraged others from Jihad.

At the conclusion of this discourse, the Holy Quran speaks of the "True Believers," their high morale, steadfastness, perseverance, and other characteristics in this great Jihad.

It begins the introduction to this discussion with the person of the Prophet of Islam, who was their leader, chief, and role model, stating: "Indeed, in the Messenger of Allah, there is for you an excellent example (in his conduct during the Battle of al-Ahzab) for him who hopes in Allah and the Last Day and remembers Allah much" (لَقَدْ كانَ لَكُمْ فِي رَسُولِ اللَّهِ أُسْوَةٌ حَسَنَةٌ لِمَنْ كانَ يَرْجُوا اللَّهَ وَ الْيَوْمَ الْآخِرَ وَ ذَكَرَ اللَّهَ كَثِيراً).

The best role model for you, not only in this battlefield but in all of life, is the person of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله. His sublime spirit, his endurance and patience, his alertness and sagacity, his sincerity and devotion to God, his mastery over events, and his refusal to kneel before hardships and problems—each of these can serve as a model and example for all Muslims.

When his vessel is caught in the severest of storms, this great Captain allows not the slightest weakness, frailty, or panic to reach him. He is both the Captain and the reliable anchor of this ship; He is both the lamp of guidance and the source of tranquility and comfort for the souls and hearts of the passengers.

Alongside the other believers, he takes a pickaxe in hand, digs the trench, gathers the earth with a shovel, and carries it out of the trench in a vessel. To maintain the morale and composure of his companions, he jokes with them, and to warm their hearts and souls, he encourages them to recite epic poetry. He constantly reminds them of Allah and gives them tidings of a brilliant future and great conquests.

He warns them against the conspiracies of the hypocrites and provides them with the necessary vigilance.

He does not for a moment neglect proper battle formations and the selection of the best military methods, while simultaneously never ceasing to employ various means to create a rift within the enemy ranks.

Indeed, he is the best leader and example for the believers in this field and in all fields.

"Uswah" (on the pattern of 'urwah) originally refers to the state a person adopts when following another; in other words, it means emulating and taking someone as a leader. Therefore, it carries a gerundive meaning rather than an adjectival one. The meaning of the verse (لَقَدْ كانَ لَكُمْ فِي رَسُولِ اللَّهِ أُسْوَةٌ حَسَنَةٌ) is that in the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, there is a good emulation and following for you; you can correct your path by following him and place yourselves upon the "Straight Path" (Sirat al-Mustaqim).

Interestingly, the Quran in the above verse considers this "excellent example" specifically for those who possess three characteristics: they have hope in Allah, hope in the Day of Resurrection, and they remember Allah much.

In reality, faith in the Origin (Allah) and the Return (Ma'ad) is the motivation for this movement, and the remembrance of Allah is what sustains it. For undoubtedly, one whose heart is not overflowing with such faith is incapable of stepping where the Prophet stepped. Furthermore, if one does not continuously remember Allah and repel the devils, they will be unable to continue this emulation and following.

It is also noteworthy that Ali عليه السلام, with all his bravery and courage in all battlefields—a living example of which is the Battle of al-Ahzab, as will be mentioned later—states in a saying narrated from him in Nahj al-Balagha:

كنا اذا احمر الباس اتقينا برسول اللَّه (ص) فلم يكن احد منا اقرب الى العدو منه:

"Whenever the fire of war blazed fiercely, we would take shelter behind the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله, and none of us was closer to the enemy than he." [1]

After mentioning this introduction, the text proceeds to describe the state of the true believers, saying: "When the believers saw the confederate armies, they did not allow any wavering into their hearts;

[1]: Nahj al-Balagha, Short Sayings, Section: Ghara'ib, Saying 9.

rather, they said: 'This is what Allah and His Messenger had promised us, and its harbingers have appeared; and Allah and His Messenger spoke the truth.' And this event did not increase them in anything except faith and submission" (وَ لَمَّا رَأَ الْمُؤْمِنُونَ الْأَحْزابَ قالُوا هذا ما وَعَدَنَا اللَّهُ وَ رَسُولُهُ وَ صَدَقَ اللَّهُ وَ رَسُولُهُ وَ ما زادَهُمْ إِلَّا إِيماناً وَ تسْلِيماً).

What was this promise that Allah and the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله had made?

Some have said this refers to the words the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله had spoken previously: that soon the Arab tribes and your various enemies would join hands and come for you, but know that ultimately, victory is yours.

When the believers witnessed the onslaught of the "Confederates," they became certain that this was indeed the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله promise. They said: "Now that the first part of the promise has occurred, the second part—namely victory—will surely follow." Thus, it increased their faith and submission.

Another explanation is that Allah had told the Muslims in Surah al-Baqarah, verse 214: "Do you think that you will enter Paradise without such [trials] as came to those who passed away before you? They were afflicted with severe poverty and ailments and were so shaken that [even] the Messenger and those who believed with him said, 'When will the help of Allah come?'"

In short, they had been told they would be tested in the crucible of severe trials, and upon seeing the Confederates, they realized the truth of the words of Allah and the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, which increased their faith.

Of course, these two interpretations are not contradictory, especially considering that one is fundamentally the promise of Allah and the other the promise of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, and both are mentioned together in the verse under discussion; combining the two seems entirely appropriate.

The next verse refers to a specific group of believers who were the foremost in emulating the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and stood by their covenant and pledge with Allah—namely, sacrifice until the last breath and the last drop of blood. It says:

"Among the believers are men who have been true to their covenant with Allah; of them, some have fulfilled their vow (and surrendered their soul to the Creator, drinking the draught of martyrdom on the field of Jihad), and some are still waiting" (مِنَ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ رِجالٌ صَدَقُوا ما عاهَدُوا اللَّهَ عَلَيْهِ فَمِنْهُمْ مَنْ قَضى‌ نَحْبَهُ وَ مِنْهُمْ مَنْ يَنْتَظِرُ).

"And they have not altered [their commitment] in the least," and not the slightest deviation or wavering was found in their conduct (وَ ما بَدَّلُوا تَبْدِيلًا).

Unlike the hypocrites or those weak in faith, whom the storm of events tossed this way and that, and who nurtured a new sinister thought in their weak brains every day, these stood firm and steadfast like mountains, proving that "the covenant they made with Him can never be broken!" The word "Nahb" (on the pattern of 'ahd) means a vow, a pledge, or a covenant, and it is sometimes used to mean death, danger, speed of travel, or loud weeping. [1] There is discussion among commentators as to which individuals this verse refers to.

The famous Sunni scholar, Hakim Abu al-Qasim al-Haskani, narrates with a chain of transmission from Ali عليه السلام that he said:

فينا نزلت "رِجالٌ صَدَقُوا ما عاهَدُوا اللَّهَ عَلَيْهِ‌"، فانا و اللَّه المنتظر و ما بدلت تبديلا!:

"The verse 'Among the believers are men who have been true to their covenant with Allah' was revealed concerning us. By Allah, I am the one who is waiting (for martyrdom), (and previously men from among us like Hamzah, the Master of Martyrs عليه السلام, drank the draught of martyrdom), and I have never changed my way; I stand by my covenant." [1]

[1]: Mufradat al-Ragheb, Majma' al-Bayan, and Lisan al-Arab (entry: Nahb).

Others have said: the phrase "some have fulfilled their vow" refers to the martyrs of Badr and Uhud, and the phrase "and some are still waiting" refers to other true Muslims who were waiting for victory or martyrdom.

It is also narrated from Anas bin Malik that his uncle, Anas bin Nadr, was not present on the day of the Battle of Badr. When he later found out, after the battle had ended, he regretted not participating in that Jihad. He made a covenant with Allah that if another battle occurred, he would participate and stand firm until death. Thus, he participated in the Battle of Uhud, and when a group fled, he did not flee. He resisted so much that he was wounded and then attained the honor of martyrdom. [2] It is narrated from Ibn Abbas that he said: the phrase "some have fulfilled their vow" refers to Hamzah bin Abd al-Muttalib and the rest of the martyrs of Uhud, as well as Anas bin Nadr and his companions. [3] There is no contradiction between these interpretations, as the verse has a broad meaning that includes all the martyrs of Islam who had attained martyrdom before the events of the Battle of "al-Ahzab," and the "waiters" were all those who lived in expectation of victory or martyrdom, with individuals like Hamzah, the Master of Martyrs عليه السلام, and Ali عليه السلام at the head of these two groups.

Therefore, it is stated in Tafsir al-Safi: "The companions of al-Husayn at Karbala, whenever one of them intended to go out [to the battlefield], he would bid farewell to al-Husayn عليه السلام and say: 'Peace be upon you, O son of the Messenger of Allah!' He would reply: 'And upon you be peace, and we are behind you,' and he would recite: 'of them, some have fulfilled their vow, and some are still waiting.'" [1] It is understood from the books of Maqatil (narratives of martyrdom) that Imam al-Husayn عليه السلام recited this verse by the bodies of other martyrs such as Muslim bin Awsajah, and also when the news of the martyrdom of Abdullah bin Yaqtar reached him. [2] From this, it becomes clear that the verse has such a broad meaning that it encompasses all true believers in every age and time—both those who donned the garment of martyrdom in the way of Allah and those who stood by their covenant with their Lord without any wavering, ready for Jihad and martyrdom.

The next verse recounts the result and ultimate goal of the actions of the believers and the hypocrites in one short sentence: "The goal is that Allah may reward the truthful for their truth, and punish the hypocrites if He wills, or (if they repent) forgive them and accept their repentance; for Allah is Ever-Forgiving, Most Merciful" (لِيَجْزِيَ اللَّهُ الصَّادِقِينَ بِصِدْقِهِمْ وَ يُعَذِّبَ الْمُنافِقِينَ إِنْ شاءَ أَوْ يَتُوبَ عَلَيْهِمْ إِنَّ اللَّهَ كانَ غَفُوراً رَحِيماً). Neither the truthfulness, sincerity, and loyalty of the sincere believers remains without reward, nor the weaknesses and sabotage of the hypocrites without punishment.

[1]: Majma' al-Bayan, under the verse discussed. [2]: Tafsir al-Qurtubi, Fi Zilal, and Majma' al-Bayan (with slight variations) have narrated this report. [3]: Majma' al-Bayan, under the verse discussed. [1]: Tafsir al-Safi, under the verse discussed. [2]: Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 4, p. 259.

However, so that the path of return is not closed even to these stubborn hypocrites, He opens the doors of repentance to them with the phrase "or forgive them" and describes Himself with the attributes "Ever-Forgiving and Most Merciful" to revive in them the motivation to move toward faith, truthfulness, sincerity, and the fulfillment of divine obligations.

Since this phrase is mentioned as a consequence of the evil deeds of the hypocrites, some great commentators have inferred that sometimes a great sin in receptive hearts can become the source of movement, revolution, and a return toward truth and reality—an evil that becomes the beginning of a good! [1]

The last verse under discussion, which speaks the final word regarding the Battle of al-Ahzab and concludes this discussion, provides a clear summary of this event in short phrases. In the first sentence, it says: "Allah turned back the disbelievers in their rage, while a great sorrow cast a shadow over their hearts, and they attained no good (none of the results they intended)" (وَ رَدَّ اللَّهُ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا بِغَيْظِهِمْ لَمْ يَنالُوا خَيْراً). "Ghayz" means "rage" and sometimes "grief," and here it is a mixture of both. The armies of the Confederates, who had exerted their final efforts to achieve victory over the army of Islam and failed, returned to their lands sorrowful and enraged.

The meaning of "Khayr" (good) here is victory in war. Of course, the victory of the army of disbelief would never have been "good"; rather, it would have been "evil." However, the Quran, speaking from the perspective of their own thoughts, refers to it as "good," indicating that they achieved no type of victory in this field.

Some have also said that "Khayr" here means "wealth," as this word is applied to wealth in some other instances (including the verse of the Will, verse 180 of Surah al-Baqarah: "if he leaves behind wealth [Khayr], the will is for parents...").

This is because one of the main motivations for the army of disbelief was to reach the spoils of Medina and plunder this land; fundamentally, in the Age of Ignorance, this was the most important motivation for war.

However, we have no reason to limit the concept of "Khayr" to "wealth" here; rather, it includes every type of victory they had in mind, wealth being one of them, all of which they were deprived of.

In the next sentence, it adds: "And Allah sufficed the believers in the fight" (وَ كَفَى اللَّهُ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ الْقِتالَ‌).

He provided such factors that the war ended without the need for a vast and extensive clash and without the believers incurring heavy losses and casualties. For on one hand, a severe and cold storm disrupted the affairs of the polytheists, and on the other hand, He cast awe, fear, and terror—which are also among the invisible armies of Allah—into their hearts. Thirdly, the blow that Ali bin Abi Talib عليه السلام struck upon the body of the enemy's greatest hero, Amr bin Abd Wudd, sending him to the realm of non-existence, caused the foundations of their hope to collapse. They gathered their belongings, broke the siege of Medina, and returned to their tribes unsuccessful.

And in the final sentence, it says: "And Allah is Powerful and All-Mighty" (وَ كانَ اللَّهُ قَوِيًّا عَزِيزاً).

It is possible for some to be "Powerful" but not "Mighty" (invincible)—meaning a more powerful person might defeat them. But the only "Invincible Powerful One" in the universe is Allah, whose power and strength are infinite. It was He who, in such a very difficult and dangerous field, granted the believers such a victory that they did not even need to engage in a full clash or suffer casualties!

[1]: Tafsir al-Mizan, under the verse discussed.

Key Points

1- Important Points from the Battle of al-Ahzab

A- The Battle of al-Ahzab, as its name suggests, was a conflict in which all the tribes and various groups of the enemies of Islam had united to crush the "young Islam."

The Battle of al-Ahzab was the final effort, the last arrow in the quiver of disbelief, and the final show of strength by polytheism. For this reason, when the enemy's greatest hero, Amr bin Abd Wudd, stood before the valiant officer of the world of Islam, the Commander of the Faithful, Ali bin Abi Talib عليه السلام, the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله said:

برز الايمان كله الى الشرك كله:

"All of Faith has stood against all of Polytheism." [1] This is because the victory of one of these two over the other was the victory of disbelief over faith or faith over disbelief. In other words, it was a decisive battle that would determine the future of Islam and polytheism. For this reason, after the failure of the enemies in this great struggle, they never stood tall again, and the initiative from then on was always in the hands of the Muslims.

The star of the enemy's fortune began to set, and the foundations of their power were shattered. Thus, we read in a Hadith that the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله said after the end of the Battle of al-Ahzab:

الان نغز و هم و لا يغزوننا:

"Now we shall raid them, and they will not have the power to raid us." [1]

B- Some historians have recorded the number of the "disbelieving" army as more than ten thousand. Al-Maqrizi in al-Imta' says that the Quraysh alone camped at the edge of the trench with four thousand soldiers, three hundred horses, and one thousand five hundred camels. The tribe of Bani Sulaym joined them with seven hundred men in the region of Marr al-Zahran. The tribe of Bani Fazara came with one thousand men, and the tribes of Bani Ashja' and Bani Murrah each with four hundred men, while other tribes sent various numbers, totaling more than ten thousand.

Meanwhile, the number of Muslims did not exceed three thousand. They had chosen the slopes of Mount Sal'—a high point (next to Medina)—as their main camp, which overlooked the trench, allowing them to control movement across the trench via their archers.

In any case, the army of disbelief besieged the Muslims from every side, and this siege lasted twenty days according to one narration, twenty-five days according to another, and about a month according to some reports. [2] Despite the enemy's superiority over the Muslims in various aspects, they ultimately returned to their lands unsuccessful, as we have mentioned.

C- The Issue of Digging the Trench

As we know, this was carried out upon the consultation of Salman al-Farsi. This matter, which was common as a defensive tool in the land of Iran at that time, had no precedent in the Arabian Peninsula until then and was considered a new phenomenon. Its creation around Medina was significant both from a military standpoint and in terms of weakening the enemy's morale and strengthening the psychological state of the Muslims.

Precise information regarding the specifications of the trench is not available. Historians have written that its width was such that the enemy's cavalry could not leap across it. Its depth must also have been such that if someone entered it, they could not easily climb out the other side.

Furthermore, the mastery of the archers of Islam over the trench area allowed them to target anyone attempting to cross while they were in the middle of the trench.

As for its length, some estimate the total to be twelve thousand cubits (6,000 meters), based on the famous narration that the Prophet assigned every ten people to dig forty cubits (about 20 meters) of the trench, and considering that the Muslim army famously numbered three thousand.

It must be admitted that with the very primitive tools of that day, digging such a trench was extremely grueling, especially since the Muslims were in severe straits regarding provisions and other means.

Undoubtedly, digging the trench took a significant amount of time, and this shows that the "Army of Islam," with full vigilance, had made the necessary preparations before the enemy attacked, such that the digging of the trench was completed three days before the disbelieving army reached Medina.

[1]: Bihar al-Anwar, Vol. 20, p. 215, narrates this Hadith from al-Karajiki. [1]: Tarikh al-Kamil by Ibn al-Athir, Vol. 2, p. 184. [2]: Bihar al-Anwar, Vol. 20, p. 228.

D- The Great Field of Trial

The Battle of al-Ahzab was an extraordinary touchstone of testing for all Muslims and those who claimed Islam, as well as those who sometimes claimed neutrality but secretly had dealings and cooperation with the enemies of Islam.

The positions of the three groups (true believers, weak believers, and hypocrites) became fully distinct through their actions, and Islamic values were clearly manifested.

Each of these three groups showed their true colors in the hot furnace of the Battle of al-Ahzab.

The storm of the event was so fierce that no one could hide what was in their heart, and matters that might have required years to discover under normal conditions were brought to light in less than a month! It is also noteworthy that the person of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, through his stubborn resistance and steadfastness, his maintenance of composure, his trust in Allah and self-confidence, as well as his solidarity and cooperation with the Muslims in digging the trench and enduring the hardships of war, proved in practice that he was fully a believer in and loyal to what he had previously brought in his teachings.

And what he said to the people, he practiced himself before anyone else.

E- The Historical Duel of Ali (عليه السلام) with Amr bin Abd Wudd

One of the sensitive and historical highlights of this war is the confrontation of Ali عليه السلام with the great hero of the enemy army, Amr bin Abd Wudd.

It is recorded in history that the Confederate army had invited the strongest Arab warriors to cooperate in this war. Among them, five were the most famous:

"Amr bin Abd Wudd," "Ikrimah bin Abi Jahl," "Hubayrah," "Nawfal," and "Dirar."

On one of the days of the war, they prepared for man-to-man combat, donned their battle gear, and from a narrow point of the trench that was relatively far from the reach of the Muslim archers, they leaped their horses to the other side of the trench and appeared before the army of Islam. Among them, Amr bin Abd Wudd was the most renowned.

His brain overflowing with a particular arrogance and having a long history in war, he came forward and challenged a duelist. He raised his voice and roared.

The echo of his cry "Is there any challenger?" (Hal min mubariz) rang through the field of al-Ahzab, and when none of the Muslims prepared to face him, he became more audacious and mocked the beliefs of the Muslims, saying: "You who say your slain are in Paradise and our killed are in Hell, is there not one among you whom I may send to Paradise or who may dispatch me to Hell?!" And here he recited his famous verses:

And I have become hoarse from calling Among your assembly: Is there any challenger! And I stood when the brave man cowers In the position of the dueling hero! Indeed, generosity and courage in A youth are the best of instincts!

At this point, the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله commanded someone to rise and rid the Muslims of the evil of this man, but no one except Ali bin Abi Talib عليه السلام prepared for this fight.

The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله said to him: "This is Amr bin Abd Wudd." Ali عليه السلام replied:

"I am ready, even if it be Amr."

The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله said to him: "Come closer." He wrapped a turban on his head, gave him his special sword, Dhu al-Fiqar, and prayed for him:

اللهم احفظه من بين يديه و من خلفه و عن يمينه و عن شماله و من فوقه و من تحته:

"O Allah! Protect him from before him and from behind him, from his right and his left, and from above him and from beneath him."

Ali عليه السلام quickly entered the middle of the field, while reciting these verses in response to Amr's verses:

Do not hasten, for there has come to you One who answers your voice, not being weak! Possessing intention, insight, And truthfulness, which saves every winner. I indeed hope to establish The wailing of funerals over you! From a piercing blow whose sound Remains after the tremors of battle:

It was here that the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله spoke the famous sentence:

"All of Faith has stood against all of Polytheism." [1]

The Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام, first invited him to Islam; he did not accept. Then he invited him to leave the field; he refused that as well, considering it a disgrace and shame. His third proposal was for him to dismount from his horse so that the duel could take place on foot.

[1]: Bihar al-Anwar, Vol. 20, p. 215; Ibn Abi al-Hadid in Sharh Nahj al-Balagha, Vol. 4, p. 344, as quoted in Ihqaq al-Haqq, Vol. 6, p. 9.

Amr became enraged and said: "I did not believe any Arab would make such a proposal to me." He dismounted from his horse and struck a blow at Ali's عليه السلام head with his sword. However, the Commander of the Faithful عليه السلام parried it with his shield with his characteristic agility, but the sword passed through the shield and injured Ali's عليه السلام head.

At this point, Ali عليه السلام employed a specific tactic. He said: "You are the hero of the Arabs and I am in a duel with you; why have these people behind you come?" As Amr turned to look behind him, Ali عليه السلام planted his sword into Amr's leg. It was then that the tall stature of "Amr" tumbled to the ground. A thick cloud of dust filled the air of the battlefield; a group of hypocrites thought Ali عليه السلام had been killed by Amr, but when they heard the cry of "Allahu Akbar," Ali's عليه السلام victory was confirmed. Suddenly, they saw Ali عليه السلام, with blood dripping from his head, slowly returning toward the camp with a smile of victory on his lips, while the headless body of "Amr" lay in a corner of the field.

The killing of the famous Arab hero dealt an irreparable blow to the Confederate army and their hopes and aspirations. It was a blow that severely weakened their morale and made them despair of victory. For this reason, the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله said regarding it:

لو وزن اليوم عملك بعمل جميع امة محمد لرجح عملك على عملهم و ذاك انه لم يبق بيت من المشركين الا و قد دخل ذل بقتل عمرو و لم يبق بيت من المسلمين الا و قد دخل عز بقتل عمرو!:

"If your deed today were weighed against the deeds of the entire Ummah of Muhammad, your deed would outweigh theirs; for with the killing of Amr, there remained no house of the polytheists but that humiliation entered it, and no house of the Muslims but that honor entered it!" [1]

[1]: Bihar al-Anwar, Vol. 20, p. 216.

The famous Sunni scholar, Hakim al-Nishapuri, has recorded the same statement, albeit with a different wording: "The duel of Ali bin Abi Talib with Amr bin Abd Wudd on the day of the Trench is superior to the deeds of my Ummah until the Day of Resurrection." [1] The philosophy of this statement is clear, for on that day, Islam and the Quran were apparently on the edge of a precipice and were passing through their most critical moments. The one who, through his sacrifice, showed the greatest devotion after the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله in this field, preserved Islam from danger and guaranteed its continuity until the Day of Resurrection. Islam took root through the blessing of his sacrifice and spread its branches over the world; therefore, the worship of everyone is indebted to him.

Some have written that the polytheists sent someone to the Prophet to purchase the body of "Amr" for ten thousand dirhams (perhaps they imagined the Muslims would do to Amr's body what the cold-hearted ones did to Hamzah's body in the Battle of Uhud). The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله said: "His body is for you; we never take a price for the dead!" It is also noteworthy that when Amr's sister reached her brother's body and saw his expensive armor, which Ali عليه السلام had not removed from him, she said:

ما قتله الا كفو كريم: "I confess that his peer and killer was a noble man!" [2]

F- The Prophet's Military and Political Actions in this Field

The factors of the victory of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and the Muslims in the field of al-Ahzab, in addition to divine support through the wind and the severe storm that disrupted the Confederate apparatus, and in addition to the invisible armies of the Lord, were a collection of various factors including military and political methods, and the important factor of belief and faith:

1- By accepting the proposal to dig the trench, the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله introduced a new factor into Arab warfare that had not existed until then, which was very effective in strengthening the morale of the army of Islam and weakening the army of disbelief.

2- The calculated positions of the army of Islam and appropriate military tactics were an effective factor in preventing the enemy from penetrating into Medina.

3- The killing of "Amr bin Abd Wudd" at the hands of the great hero of Islam, Ali bin Abi Talib عليه السلام, and the collapse of the hopes of the Confederate army with his death, was another factor.

4- Faith in the Lord and trust in His Pure Essence, the seeds of which had been sown in the hearts of the Muslims by the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and were constantly watered throughout the war by the recitation of Quranic verses and the sweet words of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, was also a major factor.

5- The Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله conduct, his great spirit, and his self-confidence gave heart and tranquility to the Muslims.

6- In addition to these, the story of "Nu'aym bin Mas'ud" became an effective factor for creating division within the Confederate army and weakening them.

[1]: Mustadrak al-Hakim, Vol. 3, p. 32. [2]: This section utilized the books Ihqaq al-Haqq Vol. 6, Bihar al-Anwar Vol. 20, Tafsir al-Mizan Vol. 16, Habib al-Siyar Vol. 1, and Furugh-e Abadiyyat Vol. 2.

G- The Story of Nu'aym bin Mas'ud and Sowing Discord in the Enemy Army!

"Nu'aym," who had recently become a Muslim and whose tribe, the "Ghatafan," were unaware of his Islam, came to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and said: "Give me any command you wish, and I will carry it out for the final victory."

He said: "You are but one man among us; if you can, sow discord among the enemy army, for 'war is a collection of secret plans.'"

Nu'aym bin Mas'ud devised an ingenious plan. He went to the Jews of "Bani Qurayzah," with whom he had been friends in the Age of Ignorance. He said: "O Bani Qurayzah, you know that I am fond of you!" They said: "You speak the truth; we never suspect you."

He said: "The tribes of 'Quraysh' and 'Ghatafan' are not like you. This city is your city; your wealth, children, and women are here, and you are never able to move from here.

'Quraysh' and the tribe of 'Ghatafan' have come to fight Muhammad and his companions, and you have supported them, while their city is elsewhere, and their wealth and women are outside this region. If an opportunity arises, they will plunder and take it with them, and if a problem occurs, they will return to their city, and you will remain in this city with Muhammad, and you are certainly not capable of facing him alone. Do not take up arms until you take a pledge from Quraysh and Ghatafan; let them hand over a group of their nobles to you as hostages so that they do not fall short in the war."

The Jews of "Bani Qurayzah" liked this proposal.

Nu'aym then secretly went to the Quraysh and said to "Abu Sufyan" and a group of the men of Quraysh:

"You well know the extent of my friendship toward you. Something has reached my ears that I feel obligated to convey, so that I may fulfill the duty of goodwill, but my request is that you do not quote me!"

They said: "Rest assured!" He said: "Do you know that the Jewish community has regretted their affair with you regarding Muhammad صلى الله عليه وآله, and they have sent a messenger to him saying: 'We regret what we did; is it enough if we take a group of the nobles of the tribes of Quraysh and Ghatafan as hostages for you, and hand them over to you bound so that you may strike their necks, and then we will be with you until we uproot them?' Muhammad has also agreed to this proposal. Therefore, if the Jews send to you asking for hostages, do not give them even one person, for the danger is serious!"

Then he went to the tribe of "Ghatafan," who were his own tribe, and said:

"You well know my lineage and origin. I love you and I do not think you have the slightest doubt in the sincerity of my intention."

They said: "You speak the truth; it is certainly so!" He said: "I have something to say to you, but do not let it be heard from me!" They said: "Rest assured, it will certainly be so; what is the news?"

"Nu'aym" explained to them word for word the same thing he had told the Quraysh regarding the Jews' regret and the decision to take hostages, and he warned them of the consequences of this action.

Coincidentally, it was a Saturday night (in the month of Shawwal, 5 AH) when Abu Sufyan and the leaders of Ghatafan sent a group to the Jews of Bani Qurayzah and said: "Our animals are perishing here, and this is no place for us to stay; tomorrow morning we must begin the attack to settle the matter."

The Jews replied: "Tomorrow is Saturday, and we do not do anything. Furthermore, we fear that if the war pressures you, you will return to your cities and leave us here alone. The condition for our cooperation is that you hand over a group to us as hostages."

When this news reached the tribes of Quraysh and Ghatafan, they said: "By Allah, it appears Nu'aym bin Mas'ud was telling the truth; something is afoot!"

They sent messengers to the Jews and said: "By Allah, we will not give you even one person, and if you wish to fight, then proceed!" When Bani Qurayzah learned of this news, they said: "Truly, what a truthful thing Nu'aym bin Mas'ud said! These people do not intend to fight; there is a trick involved. They want to plunder and return to their cities and leave you alone against Muhammad صلى الله عليه وآله." Then they sent a message saying: "The word is as we said; by Allah, until you hand over hostages, we will not fight." Quraysh and Ghatafan also insisted on their word, and discord fell between them. It was during those same days that a cold winter storm broke out at night, such that it overturned their tents and threw the pots from the hearths onto the ground.

These factors joined hands, and they all gathered their belongings and preferred flight over staying, such that not a single one of them remained on the battlefield. [1]

H- The Story of Hudhayfah

In many histories, it is recorded that "Hudhayfah al-Yamani" said: "We experienced such hunger, fatigue, and terror on the day of the Battle of the Trench as only Allah knows. One night (after discord had fallen among the Confederate army), the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله said:

'Is there anyone among you who will secretly go to the enemy camp and bring news of them, so that he may be my companion in Paradise?'"

Hudhayfah says: "By Allah, no one rose due to the intensity of terror, fatigue, and hunger.

When the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله saw this, he called me. I came to him and he said:

'Go, bring me news of this group, but do nothing else there until you return.'

I went while a severe storm was blowing, and this divine army was crushing them. Tents were collapsing before the gale, fires were being scattered in the desert, and food vessels were being overturned. Suddenly, I saw the silhouette of Abu Sufyan shouting in that darkness: 'O Quraysh, let each of you be careful to recognize the one next to him, so that no stranger is here.' I took the initiative and said to the one next to me: 'Who are you?' He said: 'I am so-and-so.' I said: 'Very well.'

Then Abu Sufyan said: 'By Allah, this is no place to stay; our camels and horses are lost, the Jews of Bani Qurayzah broke their covenant, and this wind and storm have left nothing for us.'

Then he quickly went to his mount and raised it from the ground to ride. He was so hurried that the mount stood on three legs while he had not yet untied the hobble from its other leg. I thought I would finish him with an arrow. I placed the arrow in the bowstring; just as I wanted to release it, I remembered the words of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله who said: 'Do not make a move and return, and only bring news for me.' I returned and recounted the event."

The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله said:

"اللهم انت منزل الكتاب، سريع الحساب، اهزم الاحزاب اللهم اهزمهم و زلزلهم"

"O Allah, You are the Revealer of the Book, Swift in reckoning; defeat the Confederates. O Allah, defeat them and shake them." [1]

I- The Aftermath of the Battle of al-Ahzab

The Battle of al-Ahzab was a turning point in the history of Islam and shifted the military and political balance forever in favor of the Muslims. Briefly, the fruitful consequences of this war can be stated in a few sentences:

A- The failure of the enemy's final effort and the breaking of their ultimate superior power. B- The exposure of the hypocrites and full disclosure regarding these dangerous internal enemies. C- Compensation for the painful memory of the defeat at Uhud. D- The seasoning of the Muslims and the increase of their awe in the hearts of the enemies. E- The raising of the morale and spirituality of the Muslims due to the great miracles they witnessed in that field. F- The consolidation of the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله position inside and outside Medina. G- The preparation of the ground for clearing Medina of the evil of the Jews of Bani Qurayzah.

[1]: Sirah Ibn Hisham, Vol. 3, p. 240 (with slight abridgment). [1]: Bihar al-Anwar, Vol. 20, p. 208.

2- The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) was an "Example" and "Leader"

We know that the selection of God's messengers from among humans is so that they can be practical role models for the nations, for the most important and effective part of the propagation and call of the prophets is their practical invitation. For this reason, Islamic scholars have considered being infallible (ma'sum) a definitive condition for the station of prophethood, and one of the proofs for it is that they must be the "Example for mankind" and the "Leader of creation."

It is noteworthy that the emulation of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله mentioned in the verses under discussion is stated in an absolute manner, which includes emulation in all fields, even though the occasion of its revelation is the Battle of al-Ahzab; and we know that occasions of revelation never limit the concepts of the verses to themselves.

Therefore, we see in Islamic Hadiths that in the matter of emulation, the "most important" and "simplest" issues are raised.

In a Hadith from the Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام, we read:

ان الصبر على ولاة الامر مقروض لقول اللَّه عز و جل لنبيه (ص) فَاصْبِرْ كَما صَبَرَ أُولُوا الْعَزْمِ مِنَ الرُّسُلِ‌، و ايجابه مثل ذلك على اوليائه و اهل طاعته، لقوله لَقَدْ كانَ لَكُمْ فِي رَسُولِ اللَّهِ أُسْوَةٌ حَسَنَةٌ:

"Patience and endurance regarding Islamic rulers is obligatory, for Allah commands His Prophet: 'So be patient as the Messengers of firm resolve were patient,' and He made the same obligatory upon His friends and the people of His obedience by the command to emulate the Prophet." [1] In another Hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, it is stated that he said: "When the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله performed the Isha prayer, he would place his water for wudu and his miswak (tooth-stick) above his head and cover it... then he described the quality of the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله night prayer..."

[1]: Ihtijaj al-Tabarsi, as quoted in Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 4, p. 255.

...He states, and at the end of it He says: لَقَدْ كانَ لَكُمْ فِي رَسُولِ اللَّهِ أُسْوَةٌ حَسَنَةٌ [^1] (Indeed, in the Messenger of Allah, there is a excellent pattern for you). Truly, if the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله becomes the role model in our lives—in his faith and trust in God, in his sincerity and courage, in his discipline and cleanliness, and in his asceticism and piety—the entire program of our lives will be transformed, and light and radiance will encompass our whole existence.

Today, it is an obligation upon all Muslims, especially the faithful and enthusiastic youth, to study the Sirah (biography) of the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله in meticulous detail, to commit it to memory, and to make him their guide and role model in all things; for this is the most important means of prosperity and the key to victory and success.

3- Remember Allah Frequently

The recommendation to remember Allah, and specifically "frequent remembrance" (dhikr kathir), has appeared repeatedly in the verses of the Quran, and great importance has been given to it in Islamic traditions as well. So much so that in a hadith from Abu Dharr, we read that he said: "I entered the mosque and came into the presence of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله... He said to me:

'You must recite the Book of Allah and remember Allah frequently, for indeed, it is a mention for you in the heavens and a light for you on the earth!'" [^2]

In another hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, it is stated:

"When a servant remembers his Lord one hundred times in a day, that is considered frequent remembrance." [^1]

Furthermore, in a hadith from the Noble Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, it is narrated that he said to his companions:

"Shall I inform you of the best of your deeds and the purest of them in the sight of your Sovereign, and the highest of them in your ranks, and what is better for you than gold and silver, and better for you than meeting your enemy and striking their necks and them striking yours?" They said: "Yes, O Messenger of Allah!" He said: "Remembering Allah frequently." [^2]

However, one must never imagine that the purpose of remembering the Lord with all this virtue is merely verbal remembrance. Rather, it is explicitly stated in Islamic narrations that the intention, in addition to this, is remembrance in the heart and in action; meaning that when a person is faced with a forbidden act, he remembers Allah and abandons it.

The goal is for Allah to be present in all of a person's life and for the light of the Lord to encompass his entire existence, that he always thinks of Him and keeps His command before his eyes.

Gatherings of remembrance (majalis al-dhikr) are not gatherings where a group of heedless people huddle together to indulge in pleasures while uttering a handful of invented litanies and promoting innovations. If we read in a hadith [^3] that the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله said:

"Hasten toward the gardens of Paradise!"

The companions asked: "And what are the gardens of Paradise?"

He said: "The circles of remembrance (hilaq al-dhikr)." [^4]

The intention is those sessions in which Islamic sciences are revived and educational and nurturing discussions are raised; where human beings are built, and sinners are purified and brought to the path of Allah. [^1]

[^1]: Wasa'il al-Shi'ah, Vol. 1, p. 356. [^2]: Al-Khisal (as quoted in Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 4, p. 257). [^1] and [^2]: Safinat al-Bihar, Vol. 1, p. 484. [^3]: Ibid, p. 486. [^4]: Safinat al-Bihar, Vol. 1, p. 486. [^1]: Regarding the importance of "Remembrance of Allah" and its meaning, we had another discussion in Vol. 10, p. 214 onwards.

Ayahs

وَأَنزَلَ ٱلَّذِينَ ظَـٰهَرُوهُم مِّنْ أَهْلِ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ مِن صَيَاصِيهِمْ وَقَذَفَ فِى قُلُوبِهِمُ ٱلرُّعْبَ فَرِيقًا تَقْتُلُونَ وَتَأْسِرُونَ فَرِيقًا (٢٦)
And He dragged down from their strongholds those who had backed them from among the People of the Book, and He cast terror into their hearts, [so that] you killed a part of them, and took captive [another] part. (26)
وَأَوْرَثَكُمْ أَرْضَهُمْ وَدِيَـٰرَهُمْ وَأَمْوَٰلَهُمْ وَأَرْضًا لَّمْ تَطَـُٔوهَا ۚ وَكَانَ ٱللَّهُ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ قَدِيرًا (٢٧)
He bequeathed you their land, their houses and their possessions, and a territory you had not trodden, and Allah has power over all things. (27)

Commentary

The Battle of Bani Qurayzah, Another Great Victory

Three famous Jewish tribes lived in Medina: "Bani Qurayzah," "Bani al-Nadir," and "Bani Qaynuqa."

All three groups had entered into a covenant with the Prophet of Islam صلي الله عليه وآله, pledging not to cooperate with his enemies, not to spy for them, and to maintain peaceful coexistence with the Muslims. However, the tribe of "Bani Qaynuqa" in the second year of the Hijrah, and the tribe of "Bani al-Nadir" in the fourth year of the Hijrah, each broke their covenant under various pretexts and engaged in direct confrontation with the Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله. Ultimately, their resistance was broken, and they were expelled from Medina.

Bani Qaynuqa went toward "Adhri'at" in Syria, while Bani al-Nadir were driven partly toward "Khaybar" and partly toward "Syria." [^1] Therefore, in the fifth year of the Hijrah, when the Battle of the "Ahzab" (the Confederates) took place, only the tribe of "Bani Qurayzah" remained in Medina. As we mentioned in the commentary on the seventeen verses regarding the Battle of Ahzab, they broke their covenant in that arena, joined the Arab polytheists, and drew their swords against the Muslims.

After the conclusion of the Battle of Ahzab and the scandalous retreat of the Quraysh, Ghatafan, and other Arab tribes from Medina, according to Islamic narrations, the Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله returned home, removed his armor, and began to wash himself. At this moment, Jibra'il (Gabriel) entered upon him by Allah's command and said: "Why have you laid down your weapon? The angels are ready for battle; you must move toward 'Bani Qurayzah' immediately, and their matter must be settled once and for all."

Truly, there was no better opportunity to settle accounts with Bani Qurayzah than this. The Muslims were emboldened by victory, while Bani Qurayzah were gripped by the intense terror of defeat. Their friends among the Arab tribes were exhausted, battered, and retreating to their own lands with very low morale, leaving no one to support them.

In any case, a herald on behalf of the Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله announced that everyone should move toward Bani Qurayzah before performing the Asr prayer. The Muslims quickly prepared for war, and the sun had just set when they brought the strong fortresses of Bani Qurayzah under siege.

[^1]: Al-Kamil fi al-Tarikh by Ibn al-Athir, Vol. 2, pp. 137-173.

This siege lasted for twenty-five days, and later, as will be mentioned in the Key Points, they all surrendered. A group was executed, and another great victory was added to the victories of the Muslims. The land of Medina was forever cleansed of the filth of these hypocritical tribes and stubborn, persistent enemies.

The verses under discussion provide a concise and precise reference to this event. As we mentioned, these verses were revealed after the victory was achieved, describing the memory of this event as a great divine blessing and favor.

First, He says: "And He brought down those of the People of the Book who supported them from their fortresses" (وَ أَنْزَلَ الَّذِينَ ظاهَرُوهُمْ مِنْ أَهْلِ الْكِتابِ مِنْ صَياصِيهِمْ‌).

"Sayasi" is the plural of "Sisyah," meaning strong fortresses. It was later applied to any means of defense, such as the horns of a bull or the spur on a rooster's leg.

It becomes clear here that the Jews had built their fortresses on elevated ground near Medina and would engage in defense from atop their towers. (The expression anzala "brought down" also refers to this meaning).

Then He adds: "And He cast terror into their hearts" (وَ قَذَفَ فِي قُلُوبِهِمُ الرُّعْبَ‌).

And finally, their situation reached a point where "a group you killed, and a group you took captive" (فَرِيقاً تَقْتُلُونَ وَ تَأْسِرُونَ فَرِيقاً).

And "He made you inherit their land, their dwellings, and their wealth" (وَ أَوْرَثَكُمْ أَرْضَهُمْ وَ دِيارَهُمْ‌).

These few sentences are a summary of all the outcomes of the Battle of Bani Qurayzah: a group of these traitors was killed at the hands of the Muslims, a group was taken captive, and abundant spoils, including lands, houses, and wealth, fell to the Muslims.

The use of the term "inheritance" (irth) for these spoils is because the Muslims did not undergo much hardship for them, and all those spoils—which were the result of long years of Jewish oppression, injustice, and exploitation in Medina—easily fell into the hands of the Muslims.

At the end of the verse, He says: "And a land you had never trodden" (وَ أَرْضاً لَمْ تَطَؤُها).

"And Allah is over all things Competent" (وَ كانَ اللَّهُ عَلى‌ كُلِّ شَيْ‌ءٍ قَدِيراً).

Regarding which land is meant by "a land you had never trodden," there is discussion among commentators:

Some consider it a reference to the land of "Khaybar," which was later conquered by the Muslims. Some refer it to the land of Mecca. Some consider it the lands of "Rome" and "Persia." And some consider it a reference to all the lands that came under Muslim dominion from that day until the Day of Resurrection.

However, none of these possibilities align with the apparent meaning of the verse, because the verse—by the evidence of the past tense verb used in it (awrathakum "He made you inherit")—testifies that this land came into the possession of the Muslims during this very event of the war with Bani Qurayzah. Furthermore, the land of Mecca, which is one of the interpretations, was not a land that the Muslims had never trodden, whereas the Quran says: "He gave you a land you had never trodden."

Apparently, this sentence refers to specific gardens and lands that were in the possession of "Bani Qurayzah" and which no one else had the right to enter, as the Jews were very strict in protecting and monopolizing their property.

If we set aside the past tense nature of this conquest and victory, it would have more compatibility with the land of "Khaybar," which was taken from the Jewish tribes not long after and placed at the disposal of the Muslims (the Battle of Khaybar took place in the seventh year of the Hijrah).

Key Points

1- The Root Cause of the Battle of Bani Qurayzah

The Holy Quran bears witness that the primary factor for this war was the support given by the Jews of Bani Qurayzah to the Arab polytheists during the Battle of Ahzab (as He says: "those who supported them...").

In addition, the Jews in Medina were essentially considered a "fifth column" for the enemies of Islam; they were diligent in anti-Islamic propaganda and seized every suitable opportunity that arose to strike at the Muslims.

As we mentioned, of the three Jewish tribes (Bani Qaynuqa, Bani al-Nadir, and Bani Qurayzah), only the third group remained at the time of the Battle of Ahzab. The first and second groups had been condemned and expelled from Medina in the second and fourth years of the Hijrah, respectively, due to their breach of covenant. It was necessary for this third group—who had most blatantly committed a breach of covenant and joined the enemies of Islam—to face the consequences of their treacherous actions and receive the punishment for their crimes.

2- The Story of the Battle of Bani Qurayzah

We mentioned that immediately after the end of the Battle of Ahzab, the Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله was commanded to settle the account of the Jews of Bani Qurayzah. It is written that the Muslims were in such a hurry to reach the area of the Bani Qurayzah fortresses that some even neglected their Asr prayer and were forced to perform it later as qada. The Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله ordered the siege of their fortresses. The siege lasted twenty-five days, and Allah cast intense terror and dread—as the Quran says—into their hearts.

"Ka'b ibn Asad," who was one of the Jewish leaders, said: "I am certain that Muhammad صلي الله عليه وآله will not leave us until he fights us. I offer you one of three proposals; choose whichever you wish:

My first proposal is that we join hands with this man, believe in him, and follow him, for it has been proven to you that he is the Prophet of Allah, and you find his signs in your own books. In this case, your lives, property, children, and women will be safe."

They said: "We will never abandon the law of the Torah and will not accept anything in its place."

He said: "Now that you have rejected this proposal, come and kill your children and women with your own hands so that our minds may be at ease regarding them! Then let us draw our swords and fight Muhammad and his companions to see what Allah wills. If we are killed, we will have no worry for our wives and children, and if we are victorious, there are many women and children!" They said: "Should we kill these poor souls with our own hands?! After them, life has no value for us."

Ka'b ibn Asad said: "Since you have not accepted this either, tonight is the night of the Sabbath. Muhammad صلي الله عليه وآله and his companions think we will not attack tonight. Let us go and take them by surprise; perhaps we will be victorious."

They said: "We will not do this either; we will never violate the sanctity of the Sabbath."

Ka'b said: "Not one of you, from the day you were born of your mother, has been a wise man for even a single night!" After this event, they requested the Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله to send "Abu Lubabah" to them so they could consult with him.

When Abu Lubabah came to them, the Jewish women and children began to cry in front of him. He was moved. The men said: "Do you think it is right for us to submit to the judgment of Muhammad صلي الله عليه وآله?" Abu Lubabah said: "Yes," but at the same time, he pointed to his throat, meaning he will kill you all! Abu Lubabah says: "As soon as I left that place, I realized my betrayal." He did not go to the Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله but went directly to the Mosque and tied himself to one of the pillars of the Mosque, saying: "I will not move from my place until Allah accepts my repentance."

Ultimately, Allah forgave his sin because of his sincerity, and the verse (وَ آخَرُونَ اعْتَرَفُوا بِذُنُوبِهِمْ‌ ...) was revealed in this regard (Surah At-Tawbah, Verse 102).

Finally, the Jews of Bani Qurayzah were forced to surrender unconditionally. The Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله asked: "Are you satisfied that I carry out whatever Sa'd ibn Mu'adh decrees regarding you?" (They agreed).

Sa'd ibn Mu'adh said: "Now the time has come for Sa'd to state the command of Allah without considering the blame of any blamer."

When Sa'd again obtained an admission from the Jews that they would accept whatever he decreed, he closed his eyes and turned toward the side where the Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله was standing and asked: "Do you also accept my judgment?" He replied: "Yes." He said: "I say that those who were prepared to fight the Muslims (the men of Bani Qurayzah) should be killed, and their children and women taken captive, and their property divided. However, a group of them accepted Islam and were saved." [^1]

3- The Consequences of the Battle of Bani Qurayzah

The victory over this oppressive and stubborn group had fruitful results for the Muslims, including:

A) Cleansing the internal front of Medina and putting the Muslims' minds at ease regarding Jewish spies. B) The collapse of the Arab polytheists' base in Medina and the cutting off of their hope for an internal uprising. C) Strengthening the financial base of the Muslims through the spoils of this war. D) Paving the way for future victories, especially the conquest of Khaybar! E) Consolidating the position of the Islamic government in the eyes of friend and foe, both inside and outside Medina.

[^1]: Sirah Ibn Hisham, Vol. 3, p. 244 onwards, and Al-Kamil fi al-Tarikh, Vol. 2, p. 185 onwards (summarized).

4- Meaningful Expressions in the Verses

Among the expressions found in the above verses is that regarding those killed in this war, it says "fariqan taqtulun" (a group you killed), placing "fariqan" before "taqtulun," whereas regarding the captives, "fariqan" is placed after its verb "tasirun." Some commentators have said this is because, in the matter of those killed, the emphasis is more on the individuals since their great leaders were in this group; but in the case of captivity, there were no prominent individuals to emphasize. Additionally, this word order allows "killing" and "capturing"—the two factors of victory over the enemy—to be placed side by side, maintaining proportion between them.

Also, in the first verse under discussion, the bringing down of the Jews from their fortresses is mentioned before the phrase "He cast terror into their hearts," whereas the natural order is the opposite—that is, first terror is created, then the descent from those strong fortresses occurs. This is because what was important and joyful for the Muslims, and constituted the main goal, was the breaking of their very strong fortresses.

The expression "He made you inherit their land, their dwellings, and their wealth" also expresses the reality that without you undergoing much hardship for this war, Allah placed their lands, houses, and wealth at your disposal.

Finally, the emphasis on Allah's power in the last verse (وَ كانَ اللَّهُ عَلى‌ كُلِّ شَيْ‌ءٍ قَدِيراً) is an indication that He, one day by means of wind, storm, and invisible armies, defeated the Confederates (Ahzab), and another day with the army of terror and dread, He crushed their supporters, namely the Jews of Bani Qurayzah.

Ayahs

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلنَّبِىُّ قُل لِّأَزْوَٰجِكَ إِن كُنتُنَّ تُرِدْنَ ٱلْحَيَوٰةَ ٱلدُّنْيَا وَزِينَتَهَا فَتَعَالَيْنَ أُمَتِّعْكُنَّ وَأُسَرِّحْكُنَّ سَرَاحًا جَمِيلًا (٢٨)
O Prophet! Say to your wives, ‘If you desire the life of the world and its glitter, come, I will provide for you and release you in a graceful manner. (28)
وَإِن كُنتُنَّ تُرِدْنَ ٱللَّهَ وَرَسُولَهُۥ وَٱلدَّارَ ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةَ فَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ أَعَدَّ لِلْمُحْسِنَـٰتِ مِنكُنَّ أَجْرًا عَظِيمًا (٢٩)
But if you desire Allah and His Apostle and the abode of the Hereafter, then Allah has indeed prepared a great reward for the virtuous among you.’ (29)
يَـٰنِسَآءَ ٱلنَّبِىِّ مَن يَأْتِ مِنكُنَّ بِفَـٰحِشَةٍ مُّبَيِّنَةٍ يُضَـٰعَفْ لَهَا ٱلْعَذَابُ ضِعْفَيْنِ ۚ وَكَانَ ذَٰلِكَ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ يَسِيرًا (٣٠)
O wives of the Prophet! Whoever of you commits a gross indecency, her punishment shall be doubled, and that is easy for Allah. (30)
۞ وَمَن يَقْنُتْ مِنكُنَّ لِلَّهِ وَرَسُولِهِۦ وَتَعْمَلْ صَـٰلِحًا نُّؤْتِهَآ أَجْرَهَا مَرَّتَيْنِ وَأَعْتَدْنَا لَهَا رِزْقًا كَرِيمًا (٣١)
But whoever of you is obedient to Allah and His Apostle and acts righteously, We shall give her a twofold reward, and We will have in store for her a noble provision. (31)

Occasion of Revelation

Commentators have mentioned several occasions of revelation for the above verses, which do not differ significantly in their ultimate conclusion.

From these accounts, it is understood that after certain battles where abundant spoils of war came into the possession of the Muslims, the wives of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) made various demands of him regarding an increase in their allowance or various household provisions. According to some commentaries, Umm Salama requested a servant girl, Maymunah asked for a fine robe, Zaynab bint Jahsh requested a specific Yemeni fabric, Hafsah an Egyptian garment, Juwayriyah a particular dress, and Sawdah a Khaybari rug! In short, each made a request.

The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله), knowing the consequences that surrendering to such endless demands would have for the "House of Prophethood," refused to grant these requests and withdrew from them for an entire month. Eventually, the above verses were revealed, warning them in a tone that was decisive yet coupled with kindness and mercy: if they desired the glittering life of this world, they could separate from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) and go wherever they wished; but if they had set their hearts on Allah, His Messenger, and the Day of Judgment, and were content with the simple and honorable life in the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله) home, they should remain and enjoy the great rewards of the Lord.

In this way, a firm and decisive response was given to the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله) wives who had expanded the scope of their expectations, giving them the choice between "staying" and "separating" from him.

Commentary

Eternal Happiness or Worldly Glitter!

You have not forgotten that in the opening verses of this Surah, Allah placed a crown of honor upon the heads of the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله) wives and introduced them as "Umm al-Mu'minin" (Mother of the Believers). Naturally, sensitive and honorable positions always carry heavy responsibilities. How can the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله) wives be the Mothers of the Believers while their thoughts and hearts are held captive by the glitz of the world? How could they imagine that if spoils were granted to the Muslims, they—like the wives of kings—should claim the best portions for themselves, taking what was obtained through the sacrifice and pure blood of martyrs, while in various corners, individuals were living in extreme hardship?

Furthermore, not only is the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) an "Exemplar" (Uswah) for the people according to previous verses, but his family must also be an exemplar for families, and his wives a model for believing women until the Day of Resurrection.

The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) is not a king to have a glittering harem, nor are his wives to be drowned in expensive jewelry and luxury items.

Perhaps at that time, a group of Meccan Muslims who had come to Medina as Muhajirun were still spending their nights on the Suffah (the specific platform next to the Prophet's Mosque), having no home or shelter in that city. Under such conditions, the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) would never allow his wives to have such expectations.

It is understood from some narrations that some of them even reached a level of harshness in their speech with the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) that they said: "Perhaps you think that if you divorce us, we will not find a husband among our people other than you?!" [^1] It was here that the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله), by Allah's command, was commissioned to deal with this issue with total decisiveness and clarify his situation with them forever.

In any case, the first of the above verses addresses the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله), saying:

"O Prophet! Say to your wives: 'If you desire the life of this world and its glitter, then come! I will provide for you and release you in a handsome manner, without any hostility or dispute.'" (يَا أَيُّهَا النَّبِيُّ قُلْ لِأَزْوَاجِكَ إِنْ كُنْتُنَّ تُرِدْنَ الْحَيَاةَ الدُّنْيَا وَزِينَتَهَا فَتَعَالَيْنَ أُمَتِّعْكُنَّ وَأُسَرِّحْكُنَّ سَرَاحاً جَمِيلاً).

"Umatti'kunna" (أُمَتِّعْكُنَّ) is derived from the root mut'ah. As we mentioned in the commentary of Surah Al-Baqarah, verse 236, it refers to a gift suitable to the woman's status. Here, it means adding an appropriate amount to the dower (mahr), or if no dower was specified, giving them a worthy gift so they are satisfied and pleased, and the separation occurs in a friendly atmosphere.

"Sarah" (سَرَاح) is originally from the root sarh, meaning a plant or tree that has leaves and fruit. Sarahtul-ibil means "I released the camel to graze on plants and leaves." Later, it took on a broader meaning, referring to any kind of releasing of a thing or person, and is sometimes used as a metaphor for divorce. Tasrih al-sha'r refers to combing hair, which also carries the sense of releasing or untangling.

In any case, "Sarah Jamil" (سَرَاحاً جَمِيلاً) in the verse under discussion means releasing the wives coupled with goodness and kindness, without conflict or resentment.

Here, commentators and Islamic jurists have a detailed discussion: did this statement mean the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) gave his wives the choice between staying and separating, such that if they chose separation, it would count as a divorce itself without needing a formal divorce formula? Or did it mean they should choose one of two paths: if they chose separation, the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) would then proceed with the divorce formula, otherwise they would remain as they were?

The verse itself does not explicitly indicate either of these two. The notion held by some that the verse is proof of the "choice" (takhyir) of the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله) wives and that this rule is exclusive to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) (as it does not apply to other people) does not seem correct.

Rather, reconciling this verse with the verses of divorce necessitates that the intended separation is through the process of divorce.

In any case, this issue is debated among Shi'a and Sunni jurists, although the second view—separation through divorce—is closer to the apparent meaning of the verses. Furthermore, the expression "Usarrihkunna" (أُسَرِّحْكُنَّ - I shall release you) suggests that the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) would take action to separate them, especially since the root tasrih is used elsewhere in the Holy Quran to mean divorce (Surah Al-Baqarah, verse 229). [^1]

[^1]: For more explanation in this regard, refer to books of jurisprudence, especially Jawahir al-Kalam, Vol. 29, p. 122 onwards.

The next verse adds: "But if you desire Allah and His Messenger and the Abode of the Hereafter, and are content with a simple life materially and occasional hardships, then Allah has prepared for the doers of good among you a great reward." (وَإِنْ كُنْتُنَّ تُرِدْنَ اللَّهَ وَرَسُولَهُ وَالدَّارَ الْآخِرَةَ فَإِنَّ اللَّهَ أَعَدَّ لِلْمُحْسِنَاتِ مِنْكُنَّ أَجْراً عَظِيماً).

In fact, in these few phrases, all the foundations of faith and the programs of a believer are gathered: on one hand, faith and belief in Allah, the Prophet, and the Day of Resurrection and seeking these principles; and on the other hand, being among the ranks of the "doers of good" (muhsinat) in practical programs. Therefore, merely professing love for Allah, the Hereafter, and the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) is not enough; practical actions must also be in harmony with it.

In this way, Allah clarified the duty of the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله) wives—who must be models for believing women—forever: to possess asceticism, piety, and indifference to the glitz and luxuries of the world, and to give special attention to faith, righteous deeds, and spirituality. If they are such, they may stay and be included in the great honor of being the wives of the Messenger of Allah; otherwise, they should go their own way and separate from him! Although the addressees in these words are the Prophet's wives, the content and result of the verses include everyone, especially those in positions of leadership and guidance. They always stand at a crossroads: either using their outward position to achieve a comfortable material life or submitting to hardships to attain Allah's pleasure and guide the people.

Then, in the following verse, it describes the position of the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله) wives regarding good and evil deeds, as well as their distinguished status and heavy responsibility, in clear terms: "O wives of the Prophet! Whoever of you commits an open indecency and a manifest sin, her punishment will be doubled, and that is easy for Allah!" (يَا نِسَاءَ النَّبِيِّ مَنْ يَأْتِ مِنْكُنَّ بِفَاحِشَةٍ مُبَيِّنَةٍ يُضَاعَفْ لَهَا الْعَذَابُ ضِعْفَيْنِ وَكَانَ ذَلِكَ عَلَى اللَّهِ يَسِيراً).

You live in the house of revelation and the center of prophethood. Your awareness of Islamic issues, given your constant contact with the Prophet of Allah, is greater than that of the masses. Furthermore, others look to you, and your actions are a model for them. Therefore, your sin is greater in the sight of Allah, because both reward and punishment are given according to the level of knowledge and awareness, as well as its impact on the environment. You have a greater share of knowledge and a more sensitive position in terms of influencing society.

[Tafsir Nemouneh, Vol. 17, p. 283]

Beyond all this, your wrongdoings, on one hand, grieve the Prophet, and on the other, damage his prestige; this itself is considered another sin and warrants another punishment.

The meaning of "fahishatin mubayyinah" (بِفَاحِشَةٍ مُبَيِّنَةٍ) is manifest sins, and we know that the corruption of sins committed by prominent individuals is greater when they are public.

Regarding the terms "di'f" (ضِعْف) and "muda'af" (مُضَاعَف), we will have a discussion in the "Key Points" section.

As for the statement "that is easy for Allah," it indicates that you should never imagine that punishing you is difficult for Allah, or that your connection to the Prophet of Islam will prevent it—as is common among people who overlook or downplay the sins of their friends and relatives. No, it is not so; this ruling will be executed with decisiveness regarding you.

But on the opposite side: "And whoever of you is humble and obedient to Allah and His Messenger (صلى الله عليه وآله) and does a righteous deed, We shall give her her reward twice over, and We have prepared for her a generous provision." (وَمَنْ يَقْنُتْ مِنْكُنَّ لِلَّهِ وَرَسُولِهِ وَتَعْمَلْ صَالِحاً نُؤْتِهَا أَجْرَهَا مَرَّتَيْنِ وَأَعْتَدْنَا لَهَا رِزْقاً كَرِيماً).

"Yaqnut" (يَقْنُتْ) is from the root qunut, meaning obedience coupled with humility and etiquette. [^1] With this expression, the Quran reminds them to be both obedient to the command of Allah and the Prophet and to fully observe the conditions of etiquette.

Here again, we encounter the point that the mere claim of faith and obedience is not enough; rather, according to "wa ta'mal salihan" (وَتَعْمَلْ صَالِحاً), its effects must also appear in action.
