"Rizqun Karim" (رِزْقاً كَرِيماً - generous provision) has a broad meaning that encompasses all spiritual and material blessings, and its interpretation as Paradise is because Paradise is the center of all these blessings.

Key Point: Why are the Sin and Reward of Prominent Individuals Doubled?

We said that although the above verses speak about the wives of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله)—that if they obey Allah, they have a doubled reward, and if they commit a manifest sin, they will have a doubled punishment—since the main criterion is having status, personality, and social position, this rule also applies to other individuals who have a position in society.

Such individuals do not belong only to themselves; rather, their existence has two dimensions: one dimension belongs to themselves, and another belongs to society. Their life program can guide a group or lead some astray. Therefore, their actions have two effects: an individual effect and a social effect, and from this perspective, each carries a reward and a punishment.

Thus, we read in a hadith from Imam al-Sadiq (عليه السلام) who said: "Seventy sins of an ignorant person are forgiven before a single sin of a scholar is forgiven!" [^1]

Furthermore, there is always a close relationship between the level of knowledge and awareness and the reward and punishment, as we read in some Islamic traditions: "The reward is in proportion to the intellect." [^2]

And in another hadith from Imam al-Baqir (عليه السلام) it is stated: "Allah will only be strict with His servants in the reckoning on the Day of Resurrection in proportion to the intellect He gave them in the world." [^3]

It is even mentioned in a narration from Imam al-Sadiq (عليه السلام) that the repentance of a scholar will not be accepted in certain stages; then he cited this noble verse: "Repentance is only with Allah for those who do evil out of ignorance" (Surah An-Nisa, verse 17). [^4]

From this, it becomes clear that the concept of "muda'af" (doubled) or "marratayn" (twice) here may mean an increase in reward and punishment—sometimes twofold and sometimes more—just like numbers that imply "multiplicity." Especially since Raghib in Al-Mufradat, regarding the meaning of di'f, says: "I doubled it, meaning I added its like to it and upwards." (Note this carefully).

The narration mentioned above regarding the difference between the sin of a scholar and an ignorant person being up to seventy times is another proof of this claim.

Fundamentally, the hierarchy of persons and their differences due to social position and being a model or exemplar also necessitates that divine reward and punishment be in the same proportion.

We end this discussion with a hadith from Imam al-Sajjad, Ali ibn al-Husayn (عليه السلام): Someone said to the Imam: "You are a household for whom Allah has granted forgiveness." The Imam became angry and said: "We are more worthy that what Allah applied to the wives of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) be applied to us, rather than being as you say. We believe that for the doer of good among us there are two rewards, and for the evildoer among us there are two punishments and torments." Then he recited the two above verses as evidence. [^1]

[^1]: Usul al-Kafi, Vol. 1, p. 37 (Chapter on the Necessity of Proof against the Scholar). [^2] & [^3]: Usul al-Kafi, Vol. 1, p. 9, Book of Intellect and Ignorance. [^4]: Usul al-Kafi, Vol. 1, p. 38 (Chapter on the Necessity of Proof against the Scholar). [^1]: Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 8, p. 354 (Under the verse discussed).

Ayahs

يَـٰنِسَآءَ ٱلنَّبِىِّ لَسْتُنَّ كَأَحَدٍ مِّنَ ٱلنِّسَآءِ ۚ إِنِ ٱتَّقَيْتُنَّ فَلَا تَخْضَعْنَ بِٱلْقَوْلِ فَيَطْمَعَ ٱلَّذِى فِى قَلْبِهِۦ مَرَضٌ وَقُلْنَ قَوْلًا مَّعْرُوفًا (٣٢)
O wives of the Prophet! You are not like other women: if you are wary [of Allah], do not be complaisant in your speech, lest he in whose heart is a sickness should aspire; speak honourable words. (32)
وَقَرْنَ فِى بُيُوتِكُنَّ وَلَا تَبَرَّجْنَ تَبَرُّجَ ٱلْجَـٰهِلِيَّةِ ٱلْأُولَىٰ ۖ وَأَقِمْنَ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ وَءَاتِينَ ٱلزَّكَوٰةَ وَأَطِعْنَ ٱللَّهَ وَرَسُولَهُۥٓ ۚ إِنَّمَا يُرِيدُ ٱللَّهُ لِيُذْهِبَ عَنكُمُ ٱلرِّجْسَ أَهْلَ ٱلْبَيْتِ وَيُطَهِّرَكُمْ تَطْهِيرًا (٣٣)
Stay in your houses and do not flaunt your finery like the former [days of pagan] ignorance. Maintain the prayer and pay the zakat, and obey Allah and His Apostle. Indeed Allah desires to repel all impurity from you, O People of the Household, and purify you with a thorough purification. (33)
وَٱذْكُرْنَ مَا يُتْلَىٰ فِى بُيُوتِكُنَّ مِنْ ءَايَـٰتِ ٱللَّهِ وَٱلْحِكْمَةِ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ كَانَ لَطِيفًا خَبِيرًا (٣٤)
And remember what is recited in your homes of the signs of Allah and wisdom. Indeed Allah is all-attentive, all-aware. (34)

Commentary

The Wives of the Prophet Must Be Like This!

In the preceding verses, the discussion centered on the status and heavy responsibility of the wives of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله. In the verses under discussion, this subject continues, outlining seven important instructions for the wives of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله.

First, in a brief introduction, He says: "O wives of the Prophet, you are not like any of the ordinary women if you fear Allah" (يَا نِسَاءَ النَّبِيِّ لَسْتُنَّ كَأَحَدٍ مِنَ النِّسَاءِ إِنِ اتَّقَيْتُنَّ).

Due to your affiliation with the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله on one hand, and your presence in the center of revelation—hearing the verses of the Quran and Islamic teachings—on the other, you possess a unique status. You can serve as a role model for all women, whether in the path of piety or the path of sin.

Therefore, realize your position and do not forget your heavy responsibility; know that if you practice piety, you will hold a highly distinguished rank in the presence of Allah.

Following this introduction, which prepares the audience to accept responsibilities and grants them a sense of character, the first instruction regarding chastity is issued. Specifically, it addresses a subtle point so that other related matters become clear by extension. He says: "Therefore, do not speak in an alluring tone, lest he in whose heart is a disease should be moved with desire" (فَلَا تَخْضَعْنَ بِالْقَوْلِ فَيَطْمَعَ الَّذِي فِي قَلْبِهِ مَرَضٌ).

Rather, when speaking, speak in a serious, firm, and normal manner. Do not speak like women of low character who try to use provocative expressions, sometimes accompanied by specific gestures that incite sinful thoughts in lustful individuals.

The expression "الَّذِي فِي قَلْبِهِ مَرَضٌ" (he in whose heart is a disease) is a very expressive and eloquent description of the fact that the sexual instinct, when balanced and legitimate, is the essence of health. However, when it exceeds this limit, it becomes a type of disease, sometimes reaching the point of madness, referred to as "sexual mania." Today, scientists have described various types of this mental illness in their books, caused by the rebellion of this instinct and indulgence in various sexual impurities and filthy environments.

At the end of the verse, He explains the second instruction as follows: "And speak in an appropriate manner [that is pleasing to Allah and the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and consistent with truth and justice]" (وَقُلْنَ قَوْلًا مَعْرُوفًا).

In fact, the phrase "do not speak in an alluring tone" refers to the manner of speaking, while the phrase "speak in an appropriate manner" refers to the content of the speech.

Of course, "appropriate speech" (Ma'ruf) has a broad meaning which, in addition to what has been mentioned, negates any false, vain, sinful, or untruthful speech.

Furthermore, the latter phrase can serve as an explanation for the first; lest anyone imagine that the interaction of the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله wives with unrelated men should be malicious or rude. Rather, it should be a proper and polite interaction, yet devoid of any provocative aspects.

Then, He states the third instruction regarding the observance of chastity: "And stay in your houses, and do not display yourselves as was the display of the former times of ignorance," and do not expose your bodies and ornaments to the gaze of others (وَقَرْنَ فِي بُيُوتِكُنَّ وَلَا تَبَرَّجْنَ تَبَرُّجَ الْجَاهِلِيَّةِ الْأُولَى).

"Qarna" is derived from the root "Waqar," meaning dignity or weightiness, and is a metaphor for staying settled in the homes. Some have also suggested it is from the root "Qarar" (to settle), which in terms of result does not differ much from the first meaning. [^1] "Tabarruj" means appearing openly before people and is derived from "Burj" (tower), which is visible to everyone's eyes.

But what is meant by the "former ignorance" (Jahiliyyah al-Ula)? Apparently, it refers to the ignorance contemporary to the era of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله. As recorded in histories, at that time, women did not observe proper hijab; they would throw the ends of their headscarves behind them so that their throats, parts of their chests, necklaces, and earrings were visible. Thus, the Quran forbids the wives of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله from such actions.

Undoubtedly, this is a general ruling, and the emphasis on the wives of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله is for greater stress. It is exactly like telling a scholar, "Since you are a scholar, do not lie." This does not mean that lying is permissible for others; rather,

[^1]: Of course, if it were from the root "Qarar," its imperative form would be "Iqrarna," where the first 'r' is omitted for ease of pronunciation, and its vowel is transferred to the 'q', making the helping hamza unnecessary, resulting in "Qarna" (reflect on this).

it means that a man of knowledge must avoid this act more emphatically and seriously.

In any case, this expression indicates that another "ignorance" is ahead, similar to the ignorance of the Arabs. Today, in our era, we see the signs of this Quranic prophecy in the civilized material world. However, earlier commentators, not foreseeing such a state, struggled to interpret this word. Thus, they interpreted "former ignorance" as the interval between Adam and Noah, or the interval between the eras of David and Solomon when women would come out in body-revealing shirts, so they could consider the pre-Islamic ignorance as the "second ignorance"!

But as we said, there is no need for such interpretations. Rather, it is apparent that "former ignorance" is the same pre-Islamic ignorance mentioned elsewhere in the Quran (Surah Al-Imran 3:143, Surah Al-Ma'idah 5:50, and Surah Al-Fath 48:26), and the "second ignorance" is the ignorance that would arise later (like our era). We will provide a more detailed explanation of this in the "Key Points" section.

Finally, He states the "fourth," "fifth," and "sixth" instructions as follows: "And establish prayer, and give zakat, and obey Allah and His Messenger" (وَأَقِمْنَ الصَّلَاةَ وَآتِينَ الزَّكَاةَ وَأَطِعْنَ اللَّهَ وَرَسُولَهُ).

If He emphasizes prayer and zakat among acts of worship, it is because prayer is the most important way of connecting with the Creator, and zakat, while being a great act of worship, is considered a strong bond with Allah's creation.

As for the phrase "obey Allah and His Messenger," it is a general ruling encompassing all divine programs.

These three instructions also show that the above rulings are not exclusive to the wives of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, but are for everyone, though they are more emphasized in their case.

At the end of the verse, He adds: "Allah only intends to remove from you the impurity [of sin], O people of the [Prophet's] household, and to purify you with a thorough purification" (إِنَّمَا يُرِيدُ اللَّهُ لِيُذْهِبَ عَنْكُمُ الرِّجْسَ أَهْلَ الْبَيْتِ وَيُطَهِّرَكُمْ تَطْهِيرًا).

The use of "Innama," which is usually for restriction (hasr), is evidence that this blessing is specific to the family of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله.

The phrase "Yuridu" (He intends) refers to the creative will (iradah takwini) of the Lord. Otherwise, the legislative will (iradah tashri'i)—or in other words, the necessity of keeping oneself pure—is not restricted to the family of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله; all people without exception are duty-bound by Sharia to be pure from all sin and filth.

It might be said that the creative will implies a type of compulsion (jabr), but considering the discussions we have had regarding the infallibility (ismah) of the Prophets and Imams, the answer to this becomes clear. Briefly, it can be said: the Infallibles possess a type of acquired merit through their own actions, and a type of inherent and gifted aptitude from the Lord, so they may serve as models and examples for the people.

In other words, the Infallibles, due to divine support and their own pure deeds, are such that while having the power and choice to sin, they do not go toward sin. Just as no rational person is willing to pick up a piece of fire and put it in their mouth, even though there is neither compulsion nor coercion in this; it is a state that wells up from within the human being due to awareness and natural, primordial principles, without any compulsion being involved.

The word "Rijs" means an impure thing, whether impure according to human nature, reason, Sharia, or all of these. [^1] The fact that in some words "Rijs" has been interpreted as "sin," "polytheism," "miserliness and envy," or "false belief" and the like, is actually an expression of its instances; otherwise, the concept of this word is general and inclusive, covering all types of impurities, as the "Alif and Lam" here is the "generic Alif and Lam."

"Tathir" means to make pure and is, in fact, an emphasis on the issue of "removing impurity" and negating filth. Its mention in the form of an "absolute object" (maf'ul mutlaq) here is considered another emphasis on this meaning.

As for the expression "Ahl al-Bayt," by the consensus of all Islamic scholars and commentators, it refers to the Household of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله. This is something understood from the apparent context of the verse itself, for although "Bayt" (House) is mentioned here in an absolute sense, by the context of the preceding and following verses, what is meant is the house and household of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله. [^2] But who are the individuals intended by "Ahl al-Bayt of the Prophet" صلى الله عليه وآله here?

There is discussion among commentators. Some have considered it specific to the wives of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, citing the preceding and following verses which speak about the wives of the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله as context for this meaning.

However, considering one point, this view is refuted: the pronouns used in the preceding and following verses are generally in the "feminine plural" form, whereas the pronouns in this part of the verse (إِنَّمَا يُرِيدُ اللَّهُ لِيُذْهِبَ عَنْكُمُ الرِّجْسَ أَهْلَ الْبَيْتِ وَيُطَهِّرَكُمْ تَطْهِيرًا) are all in the "masculine plural" form. This indicates that a different meaning was intended.

Therefore, other commentators have gone a step further and considered the verse to include the entire family of the Prophet, including both men and his wives.

On the other hand, a vast number of narrations found in both Sunni and Shi'a sources also negate the second meaning—the inclusion of the entire family—and state that:

The addressees in the above verse are exclusively five people: the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, Ali عليه السلام, Fatimah عليه السلام, Hasan عليه السلام, and Husayn عليه السلام.

Given these numerous texts, which are clear evidence for interpreting the concept of the verse, the only acceptable interpretation for this verse is the third meaning, namely its exclusivity to the "Five of the Cloak" (Khamsa Tayyiba).

The only question remaining here is how, in the midst of a discussion about the duties of the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله wives, something is said that does not include the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله wives.

The great commentator, the late Tabarsi, in Majma' al-Bayan, answers this question as follows: "This is not the first time we encounter verses in the Quran that are placed next to each other yet speak of different subjects. The Quran is full of such discussions. Similarly, in the speech of eloquent Arabs and their poetry, there are many examples of this."

[^1]: Raghib in al-Mufradat under the root "Rijs" has stated the above meaning and its four types of instances.

[^2]: The fact that some have interpreted "Bayt" here as referring to "Bayt Allah al-Haram" (the Kaaba) and considered its people to be the "pious" is very inconsistent with the context of the verses, as these verses speak of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and his house, not the House of Allah, and there is no evidence for what they have said.

The great commentator, the author of Al-Mizan, added another answer, summarized as follows: "We have no evidence that the phrase 'Allah only intends to remove from you the impurity...' was revealed along with these verses. Rather, it is well-understood from narrations that this part was revealed separately, but was placed next to these verses during the compilation of the Quranic verses in the era of the Prophet or after him."

A third answer that can be given to the question is that the Quran wants to say to the wives of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله: You are within a family where a group of them are infallible. One who resides under the shadow of the tree of infallibility and in the center of the Infallibles deserves to be more watchful of themselves than others. Do not forget that affiliation with a family containing five pure Infallibles creates heavy responsibilities for them, and Allah and His creation have high expectations of them.

In the "Key Points" section, God willing, we will speak in detail about the Sunni and Shi'a narrations regarding the interpretation of this verse.

In the last verse under discussion, the "seventh" and final duty of the Prophet's wives is stated. it is a warning to all of them to utilize the best opportunity provided to them to become aware of the truths of Islam. He says: "And remember what is recited in your houses of the verses of Allah and wisdom" and build yourselves in its light, for you have the best opportunity at your disposal (وَاذْكُرْنَ مَا يُتْلَى فِي بُيُوتِكُنَّ مِنْ آيَاتِ اللَّهِ وَالْحِكْمَةِ).

You are at the source of revelation and in the center and hub of the light of the Quran. Even if you are sitting at home, you can properly benefit from the teachings of Islam and the words of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله through the verses echoing in the atmosphere of your home from his blessed tongue—for every breath of his is a lesson and every word a program!

Regarding the difference between "the verses of Allah" and "wisdom" (Hikmah), some commentators have said both refer to the Quran, but the expression "verses" describes its miraculous aspect, while "wisdom" describes the deep content and knowledge hidden within it.

Others have said: "the verses of Allah" refers to the verses of the Quran, and "wisdom" refers to the Sunnah of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and his wise counsels.

Although both interpretations suit the status and wording of the verse, the first interpretation seems closer, as the expression "recitation" (tilawah) is more appropriate for divine verses. Additionally, in several verses of the Quran, the term "revelation" (nuzul) is used for both the Book and Wisdom, such as Surah Al-Baqarah 2:231: "and what He has revealed to you of the Book and wisdom." A similar expression appears in Surah An-Nisa 4:113.

Finally, at the end of the verse, He says: "Indeed, Allah is Ever-Subtle, All-Aware" (إِنَّ اللَّهَ كَانَ لَطِيفًا خَبِيرًا).

This indicates that He is aware of the most precise and subtle matters, knows your intentions well, and is aware of the secrets within your chests.

This is if we interpret "Latif" as one who is aware of subtleties. If interpreted as "the Bestower of Grace," it indicates that Allah has grace and mercy toward you, the wives of the Prophet, and is also "All-Aware" of your deeds.

It is also possible that the emphasis on the title "Latif" is due to the miraculous nature of the Quranic verses, and the emphasis on "Khabir" is due to its wise content. At the same time, these meanings are not contradictory and can be combined.

Key Points

1- The Verse of Purification is a Clear Proof of Infallibility

Some commentators have considered "Rijs" in the above verse to refer only to polytheism or "vile major sins" such as "adultery," while there is no evidence for this limitation. Rather, the absolute use of "al-Rijs" (considering the Alif and Lam is generic) includes every type of filth and sin, for all sins are "rijs." Thus, this word is applied in the Quran to "polytheism," "alcoholic drinks," "gambling," "hypocrisy," "forbidden and impure meats," and the like (Al-Hajj: 30, Al-Ma'idah: 90, At-Tawbah: 125, Al-An'am: 145).

Given that the divine will is infallible, and the phrase "Allah only intends to remove from you the impurity" is evidence of His certain will—especially considering the word "Innama" which is for restriction and emphasis—it becomes clear that Allah's definitive will is for the Ahl al-Bayt to be pure from every kind of filth, impurity, and sin. This is the very station of Infallibility (Ismah).

It is also noteworthy that the divine will intended in this verse does not refer to His commands and rulings regarding the permissible and forbidden, as these commands include everyone and are not exclusive to the Ahl al-Bayt; therefore, it would not be consistent with the concept of the word "Innama."

Thus, this continuous will refers to a type of divine assistance that helps the Ahl al-Bayt toward infallibility and its continuation, while not contradicting freedom of will and choice (as we explained previously).

In fact, the concept of the verse is the same as what appears in the "Ziyarat al-Jamiah": "Allah protected you from slips, secured you from trials, purified you from stains, removed impurity from you, and purified you with a thorough purification."

With this explanation, there should be no doubt about the indication of the above verse regarding the station of infallibility of the Ahl al-Bayt.

2- Regarding Whom was the Verse of Purification Revealed?

We said that although this verse appears in the midst of verses related to the wives of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, the change in its style (the shift from "feminine plural" to "masculine plural" pronouns) is evidence that this verse has a content separate from those verses.

For this reason, even those who did not consider the verse exclusive to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, Ali, Fatimah, Hasan, and Husayn عليه السلام, have assigned a broad meaning to it that includes both these noble figures and the wives of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله.

However, we have numerous narrations showing that the verse is specific to these noble figures, and the wives are not included in this meaning, although they enjoy appropriate respect. Here we review a portion of those narrations:

A: Narrations narrated from the wives of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله themselves, which say: "When the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله spoke of this noble verse, we asked him if we were part of it. He said: 'You are on goodness, but you are not included in this verse!'" Among them is a narration that Tha'labi recorded in his commentary from Umm Salama, that the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله was in her house when Fatimah عليه السلام brought a silk cloth to him. The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله said: "Call your husband and your two sons, Hasan and Husayn." She brought them. Then they ate food, after which the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله cast a cloak over them and said:

"O Allah, these are my household and my kin, so remove impurity from them and purify them with a thorough purification."

And here the verse "إِنَّمَا يُرِيدُ اللَّهُ" was revealed... I said, "Am I with you, O Messenger of Allah?" He said: "Indeed, you are upon goodness" (but you are not among this group). [^1] Similarly, Tha'labi himself narrates from Aisha: "When she was asked about the Battle of the Camel and her involvement in that destructive war, she said (with regret):

'This was a divine decree!' And when she was asked about Ali عليه السلام, she said:

'Do you ask me about the person who was the most beloved of people to the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله, and the husband of the most beloved of people to the Messenger of Allah? I saw with my own eyes Ali, Fatimah, Hasan, and Husayn عليهم السلام, and the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله gathered them under a garment, then said: O Allah, these are my household and my protectors, so remove impurity from them and purify them with a thorough purification.' She said: 'I asked, O Messenger of Allah! Am I from your household?' He said: 'Stay back, for you are upon goodness!'" (but you are not part of this gathering). [^2]

[^1]: Tabarsi in Majma' al-Bayan under the verse—this hadith has been narrated through other paths from Umm Salama with the same meaning (refer to Shawahid al-Tanzil by Hakim Haskani, Vol. 2, p. 56 onwards).

[^2]: Majma' al-Bayan (under the verse).

These types of narrations explicitly state that the wives of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله are not part of the title "Ahl al-Bayt" in this verse.

B: A vast number of narrations regarding the "Hadith of the Cloak" (Kisa) have been recorded in summary, all of which indicate that the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله called Ali, Fatimah, Hasan, and Husayn—or they came to him—and the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله cast a cloak over them and said: "O Allah! These are my household; remove impurity and filth from them." At this moment, the verse "إِنَّمَا يُرِيدُ اللَّهُ لِيُذْهِبَ عَنْكُمُ الرِّجْسَ" was revealed.

The famous scholar Hakim Haskani Neyshaburi in Shawahid al-Tanzil has collected these narrations through numerous paths from various narrators. [^1] Here, a question draws attention: what was the purpose of gathering them under the cloak?

It seems the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله wanted to clearly identify them and say that the above verse is only about this group, lest anyone consider the addressees in this verse to be all the households of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and everyone who is part of his family.

Even in some narrations, it is mentioned that the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله repeated this phrase three times: "O Allah, these are my household and my elite, so remove impurity from them and purify them with a thorough purification." [^2]

C: In many other narrations, we read: After the revelation of the above verse, for a period of six months, whenever the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله passed by the house of Fatimah (peace be upon her) for the morning prayer, he would call out:

"Prayer, O Ahl al-Bayt! Allah only intends to remove from you the impurity, O people of the household, and to purify you with a thorough purification."

This hadith was narrated by Hakim Haskani from Anas ibn Malik. [^1] In another narration from Abu Sa'id al-Khudri from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, we read: "The Prophet continued this practice for eight or nine months!" [^2] This hadith was also narrated by Ibn Abbas from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله. [^3] It is noteworthy that the repetition of this matter for six, eight, or nine months continuously next to the house of Fatimah عليه السلام was to clearly define the matter so that no doubt would remain for anyone in the future that this verse was revealed only regarding this group. Especially since the only house whose entrance opened into the Prophet's mosque—after he ordered the doors of others' houses to the mosque to be closed—was the house of Fatimah, and naturally, a group of people would always hear this statement from the Prophet there at the time of prayer (reflect on this).

Despite this, it is surprising that some commentators insist the verse has a general concept and that the wives of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله are included in it, even though the majority of Islamic scholars, both Shi'a and Sunni, limit it to these five individuals.

It is noteworthy that Aisha, the wife of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, who according to the testimony of Islamic narrations did not omit anything in recounting her own virtues and the details of her relationship with the Prophet, would certainly have spoken of it on various occasions if this verse included her, yet no such thing has ever been narrated from her.

[^1]: Shawahid al-Tanzil, Vol. 2, p. 31 onwards. [^2]: Durr al-Manthur under the verse. [^1]: Shawahid al-Tanzil, Vol. 2, p. 11. [^2]: Shawahid al-Tanzil, Vol. 2, pp. 28-29. [^3]: Durr al-Manthur under the verse.

D: Numerous narrations are recorded from Abu Sa'id al-Khudri, the famous companion, which explicitly testify: "This verse was revealed only regarding those five" (نزلت في خمسة: في رسول الله و علي و فاطمة و الحسن و الحسين ع). [^1] These narrations are so numerous that some researchers consider them mutawatir (frequently narrated).

From the sum of what we have said, we conclude that the sources and narrators of the hadiths indicating the restriction of the verse to the five individuals are so numerous that they leave no room for doubt. So much so that in the commentary of Ihqaq al-Haq, more than seventy sources from famous Sunni sources have been collected, and Shi'a sources in this regard exceed a thousand. [^2] The author of the book Shawahid al-Tanzil, who is among the famous scholars of our Sunni brothers, has narrated more than 130 hadiths in this regard. [^3] Beyond all this, some of the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله wives engaged in actions during their lives that are never consistent with the station of being infallible, such as the event of the Battle of the Camel, which was an uprising against the Imam of the time and caused extensive bloodshed; according to some historians, the number of those killed in this war reached seventeen thousand.

Undoubtedly, this event is in no way justifiable, and we even see that Aisha herself expressed remorse after this incident, an example of which was mentioned in previous discussions.

Aisha's fault-finding of Khadijah—who is among the greatest, most self-sacrificing, and most virtuous women of Islam—is famous in Islamic history. This statement was so unpleasant to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله that his hair stood on end from the intensity of anger, and he said:

"By Allah, I never had a wife better than her; she believed when the people were disbelievers, and she placed her wealth at my disposal when the people had all cut me off!" [^1]

3- Is the Divine Will Here Creative or Legislative?

In the course of interpreting the verse, we alluded to the fact that the "will" (iradah) in the phrase "Allah only intends to remove from you the impurity" is a creative will (takwini), not a legislative one (tashri'i).

For further explanation, we must recall that legislative will refers to divine commands and prohibitions; for example, Allah has asked us for prayer, fasting, Hajj, and Jihad—this is legislative will.

It is clear that legislative will pertains to our actions, not Allah's actions, whereas in the above verse, the object of the will is Allah's action; it says: Allah has intended to remove impurity from you. Therefore, such a will must be creative, relating to Allah's intent in the realm of creation.

Furthermore, the issue of "legislative will" regarding purity and piety is not restricted to the Ahl al-Bayt عليه السلام, as Allah has commanded everyone to be pure and pious, and this would not be a merit for them, as all those obligated (mukallaf) are included in this command.

[^1]: Shawahid al-Tanzil, Vol. 2, p. 25. [^2]: Refer to Vol. 2 of Ihqaq al-Haq and its footnotes. [^3]: Refer to Vol. 2 of Shawahid al-Tanzil, pp. 10-92. [^1]: Isti'ab, Sahih Bukhari, and Muslim (as cited in al-Muraja'at, p. 229, Letter 72).

In any case, this subject—namely legislative will—is not only inconsistent with the apparent meaning of the verse but also in no way fits with the preceding hadiths, for all these hadiths speak of a high merit and a special, important value exclusive to the Ahl al-Bayt عليه السلام.

It is also certain that "Rijs" here does not mean outward impurity, but refers to inward impurities. The absolute use of this word negates any restriction or limitation to polytheism, disbelief, acts contrary to chastity, and the like, and includes all sins and ideological, moral, and practical pollutions.

Another point that must be carefully noted is that the creative will, which means creation and origination, here means the "requisite" (muqtadi) and not the "complete cause" (illat tammah), so as not to result in compulsion and the deprivation of choice.

To explain: the station of infallibility means a state of divine piety created in the Prophets and Imams with the assistance of the Lord. However, despite this state, it is not such that they cannot sin; rather, they have the power to do so, but they refrain from sin by their own choice.

Just like a highly knowledgeable physician who never consumes a highly toxic substance whose serious dangers he knows, even though he has the power to do so; however, his awareness and intellectual and spiritual principles cause him to refrain from this act by his own desire and will.

It is also necessary to remember that this divine piety is a special gift given to the Prophets and not to others. But it must be noted that Allah gave them this privilege because of the heavy responsibility of leadership; therefore, it is a privilege whose benefit reaches everyone, and this is the essence of justice. It is exactly like the special privilege Allah gave to the delicate and highly sensitive membranes of the eye, from which the entire body benefits.

Beyond this, to the same extent that the Prophets have privileges and are included in divine gifts, their responsibility is also heavy, and a "leaving of the better" (tark al-awla) by them is equivalent to a major sin of ordinary individuals. This defines the line of justice.

The result is: this will is a creative will at the level of a requisite (not a complete cause) and, at the same time, it neither causes compulsion nor deprives one of merit and honor.

4- The Ignorance of the Twentieth Century!

As mentioned, a group of commentators fell into doubt and hesitation in interpreting "the former ignorance" (al-Jahiliyyah al-Ula) in the verses under discussion, as if they could not believe that after the appearance of Islam, another type of ignorance would emerge in the world compared to which the pre-Islamic Arab ignorance would be a minor matter.

But today, this matter is completely resolved for us who witness the manifestations of the horrific ignorance of the twentieth century, and it must be counted as one of the miraculous prophecies of the Holy Quran.

If the Arabs in the era of the first ignorance had war and plunder, and for example, the Ukaz market several times became the scene of foolish bloodshed where a few people were killed, in the ignorance of our era, world wars occur in which sometimes twenty million people are sacrificed and more than that are wounded and disabled! If in the Arab ignorance women practiced "displaying ornaments" and set aside their headscarves such that a portion of their chest, throat, necklace, and earrings were visible, in our era, clubs are formed called nudist clubs (a famous example of which is in England) where, with utmost apology, individuals become naked as the day they were born. The scandals of beaches, pools, and even public thoroughfares are unspeakable.

If in the Arab ignorance there were "polluted women of the flags" who would place a flag at the door of their house to invite individuals to themselves, in the ignorance of our century, there are individuals who raise matters in special newspapers in this regard that the pen is truly ashamed to mention, and the Arab ignorance holds a hundred honors over it.

In short, what can we say of the state of corruption existing in the material machine civilization devoid of faith in our era, which is better left unsaid, and this commentary should not be polluted with it.

What we have said is only a handful from a heap to show the life of those who distance themselves from God and, despite having thousands of universities, scientific centers, and famous scientists, drown in the quagmire of corruption, and sometimes even these scientific centers and their scientists are placed at the service of those same atrocities and corruptions.

Ayah

إِنَّ ٱلْمُسْلِمِينَ وَٱلْمُسْلِمَـٰتِ وَٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ وَٱلْمُؤْمِنَـٰتِ وَٱلْقَـٰنِتِينَ وَٱلْقَـٰنِتَـٰتِ وَٱلصَّـٰدِقِينَ وَٱلصَّـٰدِقَـٰتِ وَٱلصَّـٰبِرِينَ وَٱلصَّـٰبِرَٰتِ وَٱلْخَـٰشِعِينَ وَٱلْخَـٰشِعَـٰتِ وَٱلْمُتَصَدِّقِينَ وَٱلْمُتَصَدِّقَـٰتِ وَٱلصَّـٰٓئِمِينَ وَٱلصَّـٰٓئِمَـٰتِ وَٱلْحَـٰفِظِينَ فُرُوجَهُمْ وَٱلْحَـٰفِظَـٰتِ وَٱلذَّٰكِرِينَ ٱللَّهَ كَثِيرًا وَٱلذَّٰكِرَٰتِ أَعَدَّ ٱللَّهُ لَهُم مَّغْفِرَةً وَأَجْرًا عَظِيمًا (٣٥)
Indeed the muslim men and the muslim women, the faithful men and the faithful women, the obedient men and the obedient women, the truthful men and the truthful women, the patient men and the patient women, the humble men and the humble women, the charitable men and the charitable women, the men who fast and the women who fast, the men who guard their private parts and the women who guard, the men who remember Allah greatly and the women who remember [Allah greatly]—Allah holds in store for them forgiveness and a great reward. (35)

Occasion of Revelation

A group of commentators have stated that when "Asma bint Umays," the wife of "Ja'far ibn Abi Talib," returned from "Abyssinia" with her husband, she came to visit the wives of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله. One of the first questions she raised was: "Has anything from the verses of the Quran been revealed regarding women?" They replied: "No!"

Asma came to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and said: "O Messenger of Allah, the female gender is in a state of loss and detriment!" The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله asked: "Why?" She replied: "Because in Islam and the Quran, no virtue has been mentioned regarding them as it has been for men."

It was at this point that the above verse was revealed (assuring them that men and women are equal in the sight of Allah in terms of proximity and status; what matters is that they possess virtue in terms of belief, action, and Islamic ethics).

Commentary

The Personality and Value of Woman's Status in Islam

Following the discussions regarding the duties of the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله wives mentioned in the previous verses, the verse under discussion presents a comprehensive and content-rich discourse regarding all men and women and their prominent qualities. While enumerating ten attributes from their creedal, ethical, and practical characteristics, it lists their great reward at the end of the verse.

A portion of these ten attributes speaks of the stages of faith (verbal profession, confirmation by the heart and soul, and action by the limbs).

Another part discusses the control of the tongue, the stomach, and sexual desire, which are three fate-determining factors in the life and ethics of human beings.

In another section, it speaks of the issue of supporting the deprived and standing firm against harsh and heavy events—namely patience, which is the root of faith—and finally, it mentions the factor that sustains these qualities: the remembrance of the Lord.

It says: "Indeed, the Muslim men and Muslim women" (إِنَّ الْمُسْلِمِينَ وَ الْمُسْلِماتِ).

"And the believing men and believing women" (وَ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ وَ الْمُؤْمِناتِ).

"And the obedient men and obedient women" (وَ الْقَانِتِينَ وَ الْقَانِتَاتِ).

Although some commentators have taken "Islam" and "Faith" (Iman) in the above verse to mean the same thing, it is evident that this repetition indicates they refer to two different things, pointing to the same matter mentioned in verse 14 of Surah Al-Hujurat: "The bedouins say, 'We have believed.' Say, 'You have not [yet] believed; but say [instead], "We have submitted," for faith has not yet entered your hearts.'"

This indicates that Islam is the verbal profession that places a person in the ranks of Muslims and subjects them to their rulings, but Faith is the confirmation within the heart and soul.

Islamic narrations also point to this distinction. In a narration, we read: "One of the companions of Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام asked him about 'Islam' and 'Faith,' inquiring if they are different. The Imam replied: 'Yes, Faith is accompanied by Islam, but Islam might not be accompanied by Faith.'"

He asked for further clarification, and the Imam عليه السلام said: "Islam is the testimony that there is no god but Allah and the confirmation of the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله. By it, blood is protected, and through it, marriages and inheritances are conducted, and the generality of people fall under its outward form. But 'Faith' is guidance and that which is established in the hearts from the description of Islam, and the actions that manifest from it." [^1]

"Qanit" is derived from "Qunut," which, as we have previously stated, means obedience coupled with humility—an obedience that springs from faith and conviction. This refers to the practical aspects and the effects of faith.

Then, it addresses another of the most important attributes of true believers, namely the preservation of the tongue, saying: "And the truthful men and truthful women" (وَ الصَّادِقِينَ وَ الصَّادِقاتِ).

It is understood from Islamic narrations that the steadfastness and correctness of a person's faith depend on the steadfastness and correctness of their tongue: "A person's faith does not become upright until his heart becomes upright, and his heart does not become upright until his tongue becomes upright!" [^2]

And since the root of faith is patience and endurance in the face of difficulties—and its role in human spirituality is like the role of the "head" to the "body"—it describes their fifth attribute as: "And the patient men and patient women" (وَ الصَّابِرِينَ وَ الصَّابِراتِ).

On the other hand, we know that one of the worst ethical afflictions is pride, arrogance, and the love of status. Its opposite is "humility" (khushu'); therefore, in the sixth description, He says: "And the humble men and humble women" (وَ الْخاشِعِينَ وَ الْخاشِعاتِ).

Besides the love of status, the love of wealth is also a great affliction, and being enslaved by it is a painful captivity. Its opposite is spending and helping the needy; thus, in the seventh description, it says: "And the charitable men and charitable women" (وَ الْمُتَصَدِّقِينَ وَ الْمُتَصَدِّقاتِ).

We said there are three things that, if a person remains safe from their evil, they are safe from many ethical evils and afflictions: the tongue, the stomach, and sexual desire. The first part was mentioned in the fourth description, but the second and third parts are addressed in the eighth and ninth attributes of true believers: "And the fasting men and fasting women" (وَ الصَّائِمِينَ وَ الصَّائِماتِ).

"And the men who guard their private parts and the women who do so" (وَ الْحافِظِينَ فُرُوجَهُمْ وَ الْحافِظاتِ).

Finally, it addresses the tenth and last attribute, upon which the continuity of all previous attributes depends: "And the men who remember Allah often and the women who do so" (وَ الذَّاكِرِينَ اللَّهَ كَثِيراً وَ الذَّاكِراتِ).

Yes, by remembering Allah in every state and under all conditions, they remove the veils of negligence and ignorance from their hearts, drive away the whispers and promptings of the devils, and if a slip occurs from them, they immediately set out to compensate so as not to distance themselves from the Straight Path of Allah.

Regarding what is meant by "much remembrance" (dhikr kathir), various interpretations have been mentioned in Islamic narrations and the words of commentators, all of which seemingly serve as examples, and the broad concept of this word encompasses all of them.

Among them, in a hadith from the noble Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, we read: "When a man wakes his wife at night and they both perform ablution and pray, they will be recorded among the men and women who remember Allah much." [^1]

In a hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, we read: "Whoever recites the Tasbih of Fatima Zahra عليها السلام at night is included in this verse." [^2]

Some commentators have said: "Much remembrance" is that one remembers Allah while standing, sitting, and when going to bed.

In any case, remembrance is a sign of thought, and thought is a prelude to action; the goal is never remembrance devoid of thought and action.

At the end of the verse, He states the great reward for this group of men and women who possess the ten aforementioned characteristics: "Allah has prepared for them forgiveness and a great reward" (أَعَدَّ اللَّهُ لَهُمْ مَغْفِرَةً وَ أَجْراً عَظِيماً).

First, He washes away their sins—which cause the pollution of their soul and spirit—with the water of forgiveness, then He places at their disposal a great reward whose magnitude no one knows but Him. In fact, one of these two aspects involves the negation of unpleasantries and the other involves the acquisition of pleasantries.

The expression "a reward" (ajran) is itself evidence of its greatness, and describing it with the attribute "great" (adhima) is an emphasis on this magnitude. The absolute nature of this greatness is another proof of its vast scope; obviously, something that the Great Allah considers "great" is extraordinarily magnificent.

It is also noteworthy that the phrase "prepared" (a'adda) in the past tense is an expression of the certainty of this reward and the absence of any failure in its fulfillment, or an indication that Paradise and its blessings are already prepared for the believers.

Key Point: Equality of Men and Women before Allah

Sometimes some imagine that Islam has placed the heavy weight of personality on the side of men, and that women do not have much of a place in the program of Islam. Perhaps the source of their error is some of the legal differences, each of which has its own specific reason and philosophy.

However, undoubtedly, regardless of these types of differences which are related to their social positions and natural conditions, there is no difference in terms of human aspects and spiritual stations between man and woman in the programs of Islam.

The above verse is clear evidence for this reality, because when stating the characteristics of believers and the most fundamental creedal, ethical, and practical issues, it places man and woman side by side like two scales of a balance, and grants both an identical reward without the slightest difference.

In other words, the physical difference between man and woman, like their psychological difference, cannot be denied, and it is obvious that this difference is necessary for the continuation of the system of human society and creates effects and consequences in some of the legal codes for men and women. However, Islam never calls into question the human personality of woman—as some Christian clerics did in previous centuries, questioning whether a woman is truly human and whether she has a human soul or not! Not only does it not question it, but it does not consider any difference in terms of the human soul between the two. Therefore, in Surah An-Nahl, verse 97, we read: "Whoever does righteousness, whether male or female, while he is a believer - We will surely cause him to live a good life, and We will surely give them their reward [in the Hereafter] according to the best of what they used to do."

Islam has granted woman the same economic independence as man (unlike many laws of the past world and even today, which do not grant woman absolute economic independence).

For this reason, in Islamic "Biographical Evaluation" (Ilm al-Rijal), we encounter a specific section related to women scholars who were in the ranks of narrators and jurists, mentioned as unforgettable personalities.

If we return to the history of the Arabs before Islam and examine the status of women in that society—how they were deprived of the most basic human rights, and sometimes even the right to life was not granted to them, and they were buried alive after birth—and if we also look at the status of woman in today's world, where she has become a will-less doll in the hands of a group of human-like claimants of civilization, we will testify to what a great service Islam has rendered to the female gender and what a great right it has over them! [^1]

[^1]: Usul al-Kafi, Vol. 2, p. 21, Chapter: Faith shares with Islam. [^2]: Mahajjat al-Bayda, Vol. 5, p. 193. [^1]: Tafsir Majma' al-Bayan and Tafsir al-Qurtubi, under the verse discussed. [^2]: Majma' al-Bayan, under the verse discussed. [^1]: We had another discussion in this regard in Vol. 2, p. 112, under verse 228 of Al-Baqarah, and also in Vol. 11, p. 391, under verse 97 of Surah An-Nahl.

Ayahs

وَمَا كَانَ لِمُؤْمِنٍ وَلَا مُؤْمِنَةٍ إِذَا قَضَى ٱللَّهُ وَرَسُولُهُۥٓ أَمْرًا أَن يَكُونَ لَهُمُ ٱلْخِيَرَةُ مِنْ أَمْرِهِمْ ۗ وَمَن يَعْصِ ٱللَّهَ وَرَسُولَهُۥ فَقَدْ ضَلَّ ضَلَـٰلًا مُّبِينًا (٣٦)
A faithful man or woman may not have any option in their matter, when Allah and His Apostle have decided on a matter, and whoever disobeys Allah and His Apostle has certainly strayed into manifest error. (36)
وَإِذْ تَقُولُ لِلَّذِىٓ أَنْعَمَ ٱللَّهُ عَلَيْهِ وَأَنْعَمْتَ عَلَيْهِ أَمْسِكْ عَلَيْكَ زَوْجَكَ وَٱتَّقِ ٱللَّهَ وَتُخْفِى فِى نَفْسِكَ مَا ٱللَّهُ مُبْدِيهِ وَتَخْشَى ٱلنَّاسَ وَٱللَّهُ أَحَقُّ أَن تَخْشَىٰهُ ۖ فَلَمَّا قَضَىٰ زَيْدٌ مِّنْهَا وَطَرًا زَوَّجْنَـٰكَهَا لِكَىْ لَا يَكُونَ عَلَى ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ حَرَجٌ فِىٓ أَزْوَٰجِ أَدْعِيَآئِهِمْ إِذَا قَضَوْا۟ مِنْهُنَّ وَطَرًا ۚ وَكَانَ أَمْرُ ٱللَّهِ مَفْعُولًا (٣٧)
When you said to him whom Allah had blessed, and whom you [too] had blessed, ‘Retain your wife for yourself, and be wary of Allah,’ and you had hidden in your heart what Allah was to divulge, and you feared the people though Allah is worthier that you should fear Him, so when Zayd had got through with her, We wedded her to you, so that there may be no blame on the faithful in respect of the wives of their adopted sons, when the latter have got through with them, and Allah’s command is bound to be fulfilled. (37)
مَّا كَانَ عَلَى ٱلنَّبِىِّ مِنْ حَرَجٍ فِيمَا فَرَضَ ٱللَّهُ لَهُۥ ۖ سُنَّةَ ٱللَّهِ فِى ٱلَّذِينَ خَلَوْا۟ مِن قَبْلُ ۚ وَكَانَ أَمْرُ ٱللَّهِ قَدَرًا مَّقْدُورًا (٣٨)
There is no blame on the Prophet in respect of that which Allah has made lawful for him: Allah’s precedent with those who passed away earlier (and Allah’s commands are ordained by a precise ordaining), (38)

Circumstances of Revelation

According to the majority of Islamic commentators and historians, the above verses were revealed regarding the story of the marriage of Zaynab bint Jahsh (the cousin of the Noble Prophet of Islam) to Zayd ibn Harithah, the Prophet’s (صلى الله عليه وآله) freed slave.

The story was as follows: Before the commencement of the Prophetic mission (Bi'thah) and after Khadijah married the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله), she purchased a slave named "Zayd," whom she later gifted to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله). The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) subsequently set him free. When Zayd's tribe disowned him, the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) called him "his son" and, so to speak, "adopted" him (tabanni).

After the emergence of Islam, Zayd became a sincere and pioneering Muslim, attaining a distinguished position in Islam. As we know, he eventually became one of the commanders of the Muslim army in the Battle of Mu'tah, where he attained the drink of martyrdom.

When the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) decided to choose a wife for Zayd, He proposed to Zaynab bint Jahsh, who was the daughter of Umaymah, the daughter of Abd al-Muttalib (the Prophet's paternal aunt). Initially, Zaynab imagined that the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) wanted to choose her for Himself; she was pleased and gave her consent. However, when she later realized that the proposal was on behalf of Zayd, she became deeply upset and refused. Her brother, named Abdullah, also strongly opposed the matter.

It was at this point that the first of the verses under discussion was revealed, warning the likes of Zaynab and Abdullah that they cannot oppose a matter when Allah and His Prophet deem it necessary. Upon hearing this, they submitted to Allah's command. (Of course, as we shall see, this marriage was no ordinary marriage; it was a prelude to breaking a false pre-Islamic custom, for in the Age of Ignorance (Jahiliyyah), no woman of status and nobility was willing to marry a slave, regardless of his high human values).

However, this marriage did not last long and, due to moral incompatibilities between the two parties, resulted in divorce, even though the Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله) insisted that this divorce should not take place; yet it did.

Subsequently, to compensate for Zaynab's setback in marriage, the Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله), by Allah's command, chose her as His own wife. The matter concluded there, but other rumors and talk arose among the people, which the Quran dismantled through some of the verses under discussion, the details of which will come, Allah willing. [1]

Commentary

A Great Breaking of Custom

We know that the spirit of Islam is "submission," specifically "unconditional submission to the command of Allah." This meaning is reflected in various verses of the Quran through different expressions, including the above verse which states: "It is not for a believing man or a believing woman, when Allah and His Messenger have decided a matter, that they should [thereafter] have any choice about their affair" (وَ ما كانَ لِمُؤْمِنٍ وَ لا مُؤْمِنَةٍ إِذا قَضَى اللَّهُ وَ رَسُولُهُ أَمْراً أَنْ يَكُونَ لَهُمُ الْخِيَرَةُ مِنْ أَمْرِهِمْ).

They must make their will subordinate to the Will of the Truth, just as their entire existence is dependent upon Him.

"Qada" (قضى) here means "legislative decree," law, command, and judgment. It is obvious that neither does Allah need the obedience and submission of the people, nor does the Prophet have any personal expectations; in reality, it is for their own benefit, which they sometimes remain unaware of due to their limited knowledge, but Allah knows and commands His Prophet accordingly.

This is exactly like a skilled physician telling a patient: "I will treat you only if you show absolute submission to my instructions and have no will of your own." This demonstrates the physician's ultimate compassion for the patient, and Allah is far greater and higher than such a physician.

[1] Adapted from the commentaries of Majma' al-Bayan, Qurtubi, Al-Mizan, Fakhr Razi, Fi Zilal, and other tafsirs under the verses discussed, as well as Sirat Ibn Hisham, Vol. 1, p. 264, and Al-Kamil fi al-Tarikh by Ibn al-Athir, Vol. 2, p. 177.

Therefore, at the end of the verse, it points to this very fact, saying: "And whoever disobeys Allah and His Messenger has certainly strayed into manifest error" (وَ مَنْ يَعْصِ اللَّهَ وَ رَسُولَهُ فَقَدْ ضَلَّ ضَلالًا مُبِيناً).

Such a person loses the path of prosperity and is drawn toward deviation and misery, for he has ignored the command of the All-Knowing, Merciful Allah and His Messenger, who is the guarantor of his good and happiness. What deviation could be more manifest than this?!

Then, it addresses the famous story of Zayd and his wife Zaynab, which is one of the sensitive issues in the life of the Noble Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله) and is related to the matter of the Prophet's wives mentioned in previous verses, saying: "And [remember, O Muhammad], when you said to the one on whom Allah bestowed favor and you bestowed favor, 'Keep your wife...'" and fear Allah (وَ إِذْ تَقُولُ لِلَّذِي أَنْعَمَ اللَّهُ عَلَيْهِ وَ أَنْعَمْتَ عَلَيْهِ أَمْسِكْ عَلَيْكَ زَوْجَكَ وَ اتَّقِ اللَّهَ).

The "favor of Allah" refers to the blessing of guidance and faith He bestowed upon Zayd ibn Harithah, and the "favor of the Prophet" (صلى الله عليه وآله) was that He set him free and honored him like His own son.

It is inferred from this verse that a dispute had arisen between Zayd and Zaynab, and this dispute continued until it reached the brink of separation and divorce. Considering the word "taqulu" (تَقُولُ), which is a present-tense verb (denoting continuity), the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) repeatedly and continuously advised him and discouraged him from separation and divorce.

Was this dispute due to the incompatibility of Zaynab's social status with Zayd—she being from a prominent tribe and he a freed slave? Or was it due to some of Zayd's harsh temperament? Or neither? Rather, there was no spiritual or moral harmony between the two, for it is possible for two people to be good individuals yet have differences in thought and taste such that they cannot continue a shared life.

In any case, up to this point, there is no complex issue. Then it adds: "And you concealed within yourself that which Allah is to disclose, and you feared the people, while Allah has more right that you should fear Him" (وَ تُخْفِي فِي نَفْسِكَ مَا اللَّهُ مُبْدِيهِ وَ تَخْشَى النَّاسَ وَ اللَّهُ أَحَقُّ أَنْ تَخْشاهُ).

Commentators have said much here, and the lack of tact in the expressions of some has provided excuses for enemies. However, based on the evidence within the verse itself, the circumstances of revelation, and history, the meaning of this verse is not a complex matter, because:

The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) intended that if reconciliation between the spouses failed and their situation led to divorce, He would choose Zaynab as His wife to compensate for the setback suffered by His cousin—who had been divorced even by a freed slave. However, He feared that people would criticize Him from two perspectives and that opponents would create an uproar.

First: Zayd was the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله) adopted son, and according to a Jahiliyyah custom, an adopted son held all the rulings of a biological son, including the belief that marrying the divorced wife of an adopted son was forbidden.

Second: How could a person of the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله) stature and status marry the divorced wife of a freed slave?

It is understood from some Islamic narrations that the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) had, in any case, made this decision by Allah's command, and there is evidence for this in the subsequent part of the verse.

Therefore, this was an ethical and humanitarian issue, as well as an effective means to break two false Jahiliyyah customs (marrying the divorced wife of an adopted son, and marrying the divorced wife of a former slave).

It is certain that the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) should not fear the people in such matters or allow himself to be apprehensive of propaganda and smear campaigns. However, it is natural for a human in such cases—especially when it involves choosing a spouse—to experience a sense of apprehension, particularly since these rumors and uproars could affect the progress of His sacred goal and the spread of Islam, influencing those weak in faith and creating doubt in their hearts.

Thus, the verse continues: "So when Zayd had no longer any need for her, We married her to you in order that there not be upon the believers any discomfort concerning the wives of their adopted sons when they no longer have need of them" (فَلَمَّا قَضى‌ زَيْدٌ مِنْها وَطَراً زَوَّجْناكَها لِكَيْ لا يَكُونَ عَلَى الْمُؤْمِنِينَ حَرَجٌ فِي أَزْواجِ أَدْعِيائِهِمْ إِذا قَضَوْا مِنْهُنَّ وَطَراً).

And this was a task that had to be done, "and the command of Allah is ever implemented" (وَ كانَ أَمْرُ اللَّهِ مَفْعُولًا).

"Ad'iya" (أدعیه) is the plural of "da'i" (دعی), meaning adopted son, and "watar" (وطر) means an important need or requirement. The choice of this expression regarding the divorce and release of Zaynab is, in fact, due to the elegance of the language, so as not to explicitly use the term "divorce," which is considered a flaw for women and even men. It is as if these two had a need for one another to live together for a while, and their separation was due to the end of this need.

The expression "zawwajnakaha" (زَوَّجْناكَها - We married her to you) is proof that this marriage was a divine marriage. Hence, it is recorded in history that Zaynab used to boast to the other wives of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) about this, saying: "Your families married you [to the Prophet], but Allah married me [to Him] from above the heavens." [1] It is noteworthy that to remove any ambiguity, the Quran explicitly states the primary goal of this marriage: breaking a Jahiliyyah custom regarding the refusal to marry the divorced wives of adopted sons. This itself is an indication of a general principle: the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله) multiple marriages were not simple matters but pursued goals that affected the destiny of His school of thought.

The phrase "the command of Allah is ever implemented" indicates that in such matters, one must exercise decisiveness, and what must be done should be done, for submitting to uproars in matters related to fundamental and general goals is meaningless.

With the clear commentary we have provided regarding the above verse, it becomes evident that the embellishments that enemies or ignorant friends have tried to attach to this verse are completely baseless. We will provide more explanation in this regard in the "Key Points" section, Allah willing.

The last verse under discussion, completing the previous points, says: "There is not upon the Prophet any discomfort concerning that which Allah has imposed upon him" (ما كانَ عَلَى النَّبِيِّ مِنْ حَرَجٍ فِيما فَرَضَ اللَّهُ لَهُ‌). When Allah gives him a command, no consideration is permissible against it, and it must be implemented without any "ifs" or "buts."

Heavenly leaders must never listen to the talk of this person or that in implementing divine commands, nor should they consider political propaganda or the false customs and traditions prevailing in the environment. Often, that command is specifically for breaking those incorrect conditions and crushing ugly and scandalous innovations (bid'ah).

They must, as an embodiment of "and do not fear the blame of a critic" (Al-Ma'idah: 54), carry out Allah's command without fear of reproaches or uproars.

Fundamentally, if we were to wait to gain everyone's satisfaction and pleasure to implement the command of Truth, such a thing would be impossible. There are groups who will only be satisfied when we submit to their desires or follow their school of thought, as the Quran says: "And never will the Jews or the Christians be pleased with you until you follow their religion" (Al-Baqarah: 120).

Regarding the verse under discussion, the situation was such because the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله) marriage to Zaynab—as we said—faced two objections in the public opinion of that environment: one was marrying the "divorced wife of an adopted son," which in their view was like marrying the wife of a biological son, and this was an innovation that had to be crushed.

The other was that it was considered a flaw and a disgrace for a person of status like the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) to marry the divorced wife of a freed slave, as it placed the Prophet on the same level as a slave. This false culture also had to be abolished, and human values had to take its place, establishing that "compatibility" (kufu') between spouses is based solely on faith, Islam, and piety.

In principle, breaking customs and abolishing superstitious and inhumane traditions is always accompanied by noise, and prophets must never pay heed to such noise.

[1] Al-Kamil fi al-Tarikh by Ibn al-Athir, Vol. 2, p. 177. It is noteworthy that the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله) marriage to Zaynab took place in the fifth year of the Hijra (ibid).

Therefore, in the next sentence, it says: "[This is] the established way of Allah with those who passed on before" (سُنَّةَ اللَّهِ فِي الَّذِينَ خَلَوْا مِنْ قَبْلُ‌). You are not the only one facing this problem; rather, all prophets faced these difficulties when breaking false customs.

The great challenge in this case was not limited to breaking these two Jahiliyyah customs; rather, because it involved the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله) marriage, it could provide another pretext for enemies to find fault, the details of which will follow.

At the end of the verse, to establish decisiveness in such fundamental matters, it says: "And the command of Allah is a destiny decreed" (وَ كانَ أَمْرُ اللَّهِ قَدَراً مَقْدُوراً).

The expression "qadaran maqdurah" (قَدَراً مَقْدُوراً) may refer to the inevitability of the divine command, or it may point to the observance of wisdom and benefit within it. However, it is more appropriate for the context of the verse that both meanings be intended—that is, Allah's command is both based on precise calculation and must be implemented without question.

Interestingly, we read in history: regarding the marriage with Zaynab, the Prophet of Islam extended such a general invitation for a meal to the people that had no precedent for any of His other wives! [1] It seems that by this act, He wanted to show that He was in no way intimidated by the superstitious customs of the environment; rather, He took pride in implementing this divine instruction. Furthermore, He intended that the news of breaking this Jahiliyyah custom should reach everyone throughout the Arabian Peninsula.

Key Points

1- False Legends

Despite all the clarity the Quran has shown regarding the story of the Prophet of Islam's (صلى الله عليه وآله) marriage to Zaynab and the goal of this marriage—identifying it as the breaking of a Jahiliyyah custom regarding marriage with the divorced wife of an adopted son—it has still been exploited by a group of enemies of Islam. They have tried to turn it into a love story to tarnish the Prophet's holy status, relying on suspicious or fabricated hadiths in this regard.

Among these, they have written: When the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) came to Zayd's house to check on him, as soon as He opened the door, His eyes fell upon Zaynab's beauty, and He said: "Glory be to Allah, the Creator of light! Blessed be Allah, the best of creators!" They have taken this sentence as evidence of the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله) attraction to Zaynab.

However, clear evidence—regardless of the issue of Prophethood and Infallibility ('Ismah)—is available to refute these legends.

First: Zaynab was the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله) cousin and had grown up with Him in the family environment. The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) personally proposed to her on behalf of Zayd. If Zaynab possessed extraordinary beauty and, hypothetically, her beauty had attracted the Prophet, neither was her beauty a hidden matter, nor was marrying her before this incident a problem. On the contrary, Zaynab showed no inclination to marry Zayd and expressed her opposition explicitly, clearly preferring to become the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله) wife. So much so that when the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) proposed to her for Zayd, she was happy because she imagined the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) was proposing for Himself. But later, with the revelation of the Quranic verse and the command to submit to the decree of Allah and the Prophet, she consented to marry Zayd.

With these preliminaries, what room is there for the delusion that He was unaware of Zaynab's appearance? And what room is there for the delusion that He desired to marry her but could not take action?

Second: When Zayd approached the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) to divorce his wife Zaynab, the Prophet advised him many times and prevented this divorce. This itself is another witness to the negation of those legends.

Third: The Quran explicitly stated the goal of this marriage so that there would be no room for other talk.

Fourth: In the above verses, we read that Allah says to the Prophet: In the matter of the marriage with Zayd's divorced wife, there was a situation where the Prophet feared the people, while He should fear Allah.

The issue of "fearing Allah" shows that this marriage was performed as a duty where personal considerations had to be set aside for the sake of the Lord to fulfill a sacred divine goal, even at the cost of the taunts of the blind-hearted and the legend-weaving of the hypocrites in accusing the Prophet. This was a heavy price the Prophet paid—and still pays—for obeying Allah's command and breaking a false custom! But moments arise in the lives of true leaders where they must sacrifice and expose themselves to the accusations of such individuals so that their goal may be implemented.

Yes, if the Prophet had never seen or known Zaynab, and if Zaynab had never desired to marry Him, and if Zayd had not been willing to divorce her (regardless of the issue of Prophethood and Infallibility), there might have been room for such talk and delusions. But given the negation of all these conditions, the fabricated nature of these legends becomes clear.

Furthermore, the history of the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله) life in no way shows that He had a special affection or inclination toward Zaynab; rather, she was like His other wives, and perhaps in some respects less than some of them. This is another historical witness refuting those legends. The final point we deem necessary to mention here is that someone might say: breaking such a false custom was necessary, but what was the necessity for the Prophet Himself to take action? He could have stated the matter as a law and encouraged others to marry the divorced wives of their adopted sons.

However, it must be noted that sometimes a false Jahiliyyah custom—especially one related to marrying individuals who are considered beneath one's status—cannot be changed by words alone. People would say: "If this was good, why did He not do it Himself? Why did He not marry the wife of a freed slave? Why did He not enter into marriage with the divorced wife of His adopted son?"

In such cases, a practical example puts an end to all these "whys," and that false custom is decisively broken. Moreover, the act itself was a form of altruism and sacrifice.

[1] The great commentator, the late Tabarsi, narrates in Majma' al-Bayan: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله) married her... and He did not give a wedding feast (walimah) for any of His wives as He did for her; He slaughtered a sheep and fed the people bread and meat until the day was well advanced" (Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 8, p. 361).

2- Submission to the Truth is the Spirit of Islam

Undoubtedly, the intellectual and spiritual independence of a human does not allow him to submit unconditionally to anyone, for that person is a human like himself and may make mistakes.

However, when the matter reaches the All-Knowing and All-Wise Allah and the Prophet who speaks from Him and moves by His command, not being absolutely submissive is evidence of deviation, for His command contains not the slightest error or mistake.

Furthermore, His command protects the interests of the human himself and is not something that returns to the Holy Essence of Allah. Is it possible for any rational person, upon recognizing this truth, to trample upon his own interests?

Beyond all this, we belong to Him, and whatever we have is from Him, and we can have no role but submission before Him.

Therefore, throughout the Quran, many verses are seen pointing to this issue:

Sometimes it says: The true followers of the prophets are those who, in the face of the judgment of Allah and His Messenger, say: "We hear and we obey" (إِنَّما كانَ قَوْلَ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ إِذا دُعُوا إِلَى اللَّهِ وَ رَسُولِهِ لِيَحْكُمَ بَيْنَهُمْ أَنْ يَقُولُوا سَمِعْنا وَ أَطَعْنا وَ أُولئِكَ هُمُ الْمُفْلِحُونَ - An-Nur: 51).

And sometimes it says: "But no, by your Lord, they will not [truly] believe until they make you, [O Muhammad], judge concerning that over which they dispute among themselves and then find within themselves no discomfort from what you have judged and submit in [full, willing] submission" (فَلا وَ رَبِّكَ لا يُؤْمِنُونَ حَتَّى يُحَكِّمُوكَ فِيما شَجَرَ بَيْنَهُمْ ثُمَّ لا يَجِدُوا فِي أَنْفُسِهِمْ حَرَجاً مِمَّا قَضَيْتَ وَ يُسَلِّمُوا تَسْلِيماً - An-Nisa: 65).

And elsewhere it says: "And who is better in religion than one who submits himself to Allah while being a doer of good?" (وَ مَنْ أَحْسَنُ دِيناً مِمَّنْ أَسْلَمَ وَجْهَهُ لِلَّهِ وَ هُوَ مُحْسِنٌ - An-Nisa: 125).

Fundamentally, "Islam" is derived from the root "taslim" (submission) and points to this very reality. Therefore, every human possesses the spirit of Islam to the extent of his submission to the Truth.

People in this regard fall into several groups: One group submits to the command of Truth only in cases that align with their interests. These are, in reality, polytheists who have named themselves "Muslims," and their work is the fragmentation of divine rulings, as per "we believe in some and disbelieve in some." Even where they believe, they have in reality believed in their own interests, not in Allah's command!

Another group consists of those whose will and desire are overshadowed by the Will and Desire of Allah. When their fleeting interests conflict with the command of Truth, they renounce them and submit to Allah's command. These are the true believers and Muslims.

A third group is even higher than this; they fundamentally have no will except what Allah wills, and no desire in their hearts except what He desires. They have reached a point where they only love what He loves and detest what He does not want.

These are the elite, the sincere, and those brought near to His presence, whose entire existence has taken on the color of Monotheism (Tawhid) and who are drowned in His love and lost in His Beauty. [1]

[1] We have provided another discussion in this regard in Volume 3, page 454 (under verse 65 of Surah An-Nisa).

Ayah

ٱلَّذِينَ يُبَلِّغُونَ رِسَـٰلَـٰتِ ٱللَّهِ وَيَخْشَوْنَهُۥ وَلَا يَخْشَوْنَ أَحَدًا إِلَّا ٱللَّهَ ۗ وَكَفَىٰ بِٱللَّهِ حَسِيبًا (٣٩)
such as deliver the messages of Allah and fear Him, and fear no one except Allah, and Allah suffices as reckoner. (39)

Commentary

Who are the True Conveyors?

The first verse under discussion—in alignment with the discussion in the final verse of the preceding passage regarding past Prophets—refers to one of the most important general programs of the Prophets, stating: "The past Prophets were those who conveyed the messages of Allah and feared Him, and feared no one except Allah" (الَّذِينَ يُبَلِّغُونَ رِسالاتِ اللَّهِ وَ يَخْشَوْنَهُ وَ لا يَخْشَوْنَ أَحَداً إِلَّا اللَّهَ‌).

You too, in conveying the messages of your Lord, must not have the slightest dread of anyone when He commands you to break a false pagan tradition regarding marriage with the divorced wife of an adopted son, and to marry Zaynab, the divorced wife of Zayd. You must never allow the slightest concern regarding the gossip of this person or that to enter your heart in fulfilling this duty, for this is the tradition of all the Prophets.

Fundamentally, the work of the Prophets in many stages is to break such traditions, and if they were to allow the slightest fear or dread into themselves, they would not succeed in fulfilling their mission. They must proceed decisively, embrace the discordant words of detractors, and continue their programs indifferent to the atmosphere-making and clamor of the masses or the conspiracies of the corrupt and the mischief-makers, for all accounts are in the hands of Allah.

Therefore, at the end of the verse, He says: "And sufficient is Allah as a Reckoner [of the deeds of His servants and their rewarder]" (وَ كَفى‌ بِاللَّهِ حَسِيباً).

He both keeps the account of the altruism and sacrifice of the Prophets in this path and rewards them, and He also calculates and punishes the discordant and nonsensical words of the enemies.

In reality, the phrase "وَ كَفى‌ بِاللَّهِ حَسِيباً" is a reason for the fact that divine leaders must not have any dread in communicating Their messages, because the one who calculates Their efforts and rewards Them is Allah.

Key Points

  1. The meaning of "Tabligh" (تبليغ) here is "Iblagh" (ابلاغ) and conveying. When it is associated with "the messages of Allah" (رسالات اللَّه), its meaning becomes that they should teach the people what Allah has taught the Prophets as revelation, and penetrate hearts through reasoning, warning, glad tidings, and exhortation.

  2. "Khashyat" (خشيت) means fear accompanied by veneration and respect; for this reason, it differs from "Khawf" (خوف), which lacks this characteristic, though it is sometimes used to mean absolute fear.

In some of the works of Muhaqqiq Tusi, there is a discussion regarding the difference between these two terms which actually pertains to their mystical (gnostic) meaning rather than the linguistic one. He says:

"Although 'Khashyat' and 'Khawf' are linguistically the same in meaning (or close in meaning), in the convention of the 'people of heart' (the gnostics), there is a difference between the two, which is: 'Khawf' means an internal unease regarding a punishment that a person expects due to committing sins or falling short in acts of obedience. This state occurs for most people, although its degrees vary greatly, and its highest degree is achieved only by a small group.

However, 'Khashyat' is a state that occurs for a human being upon perceiving the greatness of Allah and His awe, and the fear of remaining deprived of the lights of His grace. This is a state that is not achieved except by those who are aware of the greatness of His Pure Essence and His station of Majesty, and who have tasted the pleasure of proximity to Him. Therefore, in the Quran, this state is considered exclusive to the learned and aware servants, as He says: 'Only those of His servants who possess knowledge fear Allah' (إِنَّما يَخْشَى اللَّهَ مِنْ عِبادِهِ الْعُلَماءُ) [1]."

Answer to a Question

It might be said that this verse contradicts a sentence that appeared in the previous verses; for here it says: the divine Prophets fear only Allah and have no fear or dread of anyone else, but in the previous verses it was said: "And you were hiding in your heart that which Allah was to disclose," and "you feared the people, while Allah is more worthy that you should fear Him" (وَ تَخْشَى النَّاسَ وَ اللَّهُ أَحَقُّ أَنْ تَخْشاهُ‌).

However, by considering two points, the answer to this question becomes clear:

First: If the Prophet had any fear or dread, it was because he feared that breaking this tradition might not be digestible or tolerable for a large group, and consequently, they might become wavering in their faith regarding the foundations of Islam. Such a "Khashyat" in reality returns to "Khashyat of Allah."

Second: The Prophets, in conveying the divine message, never fall victim to fear or dread of anyone. However, in matters of personal and private life, there is no obstacle to them fearing a dangerous matter, such as the stinging tongues of the people, or like Musa عليه السلام who, when he threw the staff and it became a serpent, feared according to human nature. This kind of fear and dread, if not excessive, is not a flaw or deficiency; even the bravest individuals sometimes face it in their lives. The flaw and deficiency is to fear in social life while performing a divine duty.

Do the Prophets Practice Taqiyyah?
