A group has inferred from the above verse that it is never permissible for the Prophets to practice Taqiyyah (dissimulation) in conveying the message, because the Quran says: "And they fear no one except Allah" (وَ لا يَخْشَوْنَ أَحَداً إِلَّا اللَّهَ‌).

However, it must be noted that Taqiyyah has various types. Only one type of it is "fearful Taqiyyah" (Taqiyyah Khawfi), which, according to the above verse, is ruled out regarding the invitation of the Prophets and the conveying of the message.

But Taqiyyah has other types as well, including "conciliatory Taqiyyah" (Taqiyyah Tahbibi) and "protective Taqiyyah" (Taqiyyah Pushishi).

The meaning of "conciliatory Taqiyyah" is that sometimes a person conceals his belief to gain the affection of the opposite party so that he can attract their view for cooperation in shared goals.

The meaning of "protective Taqiyyah" is that sometimes, to reach a goal, one must conceal plans and preliminaries, because if they are revealed and enemies become aware of them, they might neutralize them.

The lives of the Prophets, especially the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, are full of these types of Taqiyyah. For we know that on many occasions, when he moved toward the battlefield, he would hide his destination; his war plans were drawn in complete secrecy, and camouflage—which is a type of Taqiyyah—was implemented in all stages.

Sometimes, to express a ruling, he used a staged method, which is a type of Taqiyyah. For example, the issue of "the prohibition of usury" or "drinking wine" was not expressed in one stage; rather, by the command of Allah, it took place in several stages—meaning it started from lighter stages until it reached the final and original ruling.

In any case, Taqiyyah has a broad meaning, which is "concealing realities to avoid and prevent goals from being endangered," and this is something that exists among all the wise people of the world. Divine leaders also perform it in some stages to reach their sacred goals, just as we read in the story of Prophet Ibrahim عليه السلام, the hero of monotheism, that he concealed his purpose for staying in the city on the day the idolaters went outside the city for festival ceremonies, so that he could use a suitable opportunity to smash the idols.

Likewise, the "Believer of the Family of Pharaoh" concealed his faith so that he could help Musa عليه السلام at critical moments and save him from being killed. For this reason, the Quran mentions him with greatness. In any case, it is only "fearful Taqiyyah" that is not permitted for the Prophets, not the other types of Taqiyyah.

Although there is much to say in this regard, we conclude this discussion with a meaningful and comprehensive Hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام. The Imam عليه السلام said:

"Taqiyyah is my religion and the religion of my fathers; he who has no Taqiyyah has no religion. Taqiyyah is the shield of Allah on earth, for if the Believer of the Family of Pharaoh had disclosed his Islam, he would surely have been killed (and his mission in preserving the religion of Musa at the time of danger would not have been fulfilled)" [1]. Regarding Taqiyyah, we had a detailed discussion in Volume 11, page 423 (under verse 106 of Surah al-Nahl).

The Condition for Success in Propagation

The above verse is a clear proof that the essential condition for progress in matters of propagation is decisiveness, sincerity, and the absence of dread of anyone except Allah.

Those who, in the face of divine commands, take into account the desires of this person or that and the excessive inclinations of groups and societies, and overshadow truth and justice with justifications, will never achieve a fundamental result. No blessing is greater than the blessing of guidance, and no service is greater than granting this blessing to a human being. For this reason, the reward for this work is the greatest of rewards. Thus, we read in a Hadith from the Commander of the Faithful عليه السلام that he said: "When the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله sent me toward Yemen, he said: 'Do not fight anyone until you have first invited him toward the Truth.

And by Allah, if Allah guides one man through your hands, it is better for you than everything upon which the sun rises and sets'" [1]. And for this same reason, true conveyors must have no need for the people and no fear of any authority, for that "need" and this "fear" will inevitably affect their thoughts and will.

A divine conveyor, by virtue of "وَ كَفى‌ بِاللَّهِ حَسِيباً" (And sufficient is Allah as a Reckoner), thinks only that the one who calculates his deeds is Allah, and his reward is in His hand, and this very awareness and gnosis helps him on this path full of ups and downs.

[1]: Majma' al-Bahrayn, root kh-sh-y. [1]: Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 8, p. 521, under verse 28 of Surah al-Mu'min. [1]: Al-Kafi (as narrated in Bihar al-Anwar, Vol. 21, p. 361).

Ayah

مَّا كَانَ مُحَمَّدٌ أَبَآ أَحَدٍ مِّن رِّجَالِكُمْ وَلَـٰكِن رَّسُولَ ٱللَّهِ وَخَاتَمَ ٱلنَّبِيِّـۧنَ ۗ وَكَانَ ٱللَّهُ بِكُلِّ شَىْءٍ عَلِيمًا (٤٠)
Muhammad is not the father of any man among you, but he is the Apostle of Allah and the Seal of the Prophets, and Allah has knowledge of all things. (40)

Commentary

The Issue of Finality (Khatamiyat)

This verse is the final word that Allah expresses regarding the issue of the Prophet’s (صلى الله عليه وآله) marriage to the "divorced wife of Zayd" to break a false Jahiliyyah (pre-Islamic) custom. It serves as a concise and condensed final response, while simultaneously articulating another significant reality—the issue of Finality (Khatamiyat)—within a specific context at its conclusion.

First, He says: "Muhammad was not the father of any of your men" (مَّا كَانَ مُحَمَّدٌ أَبَا أَحَدٍ مِّن رِّجَالِكُمْ).

Neither Zayd nor anyone else. If one day they called him "the son of Muhammad," it was merely a habit and custom that was abolished with the arrival of Islam and the revelation of the Quran, not a natural or kinship relationship.

Of course, the Prophet had biological sons named "Qasim," "Tayyib," "Tahir," and "Ibrahim," but according to historians, all of them passed away before reaching puberty; therefore, the term "men" (rijal) did not apply to them [1].

[1]: Tafsir al-Qurtubi and Tafsir al-Mizan under the verse in question.

Imam Hasan and Imam Husayn (عليه السلام), whom people called the sons of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله), although they reached adulthood, were still children at the time this verse was revealed. Therefore, the phrase مَّا كَانَ مُحَمَّدٌ أَبَا أَحَدٍ مِّن رِّجَالِكُمْ, which is in the past tense, was decisively true regarding everyone at that moment.

And if we read in some of the Prophet’s (صلى الله عليه وآله) own expressions: "I and Ali are the two fathers of this Ummah" (أنا وعلي أبوا هذه الأمة), the intention was certainly not biological fatherhood, but rather the fatherhood arising from teaching, upbringing, and leadership.

Thus, the marriage to the divorced wife of Zayd—the philosophy of which the Quran explicitly states was to break incorrect customs—was not something that should cause gossip among people or be used as a pretext for malicious intentions.

Then it adds: The Prophet’s (صلى الله عليه وآله) connection to you is solely through the aspect of Messengership and Finality: "He is the Messenger of Allah and the Seal of the Prophets" (وَلَكِن رَّسُولَ اللَّهِ وَخَاتَمَ النَّبِيِّينَ).

Therefore, the beginning of the verse completely severs the biological connection, and the end of the verse establishes the spiritual connection arising from Messengership and Finality; from here, the link between the beginning and the end of the verse becomes clear.

Furthermore, it points to the reality that his affection is beyond that of a father for a child, for his affection is that of a Messenger for his Ummah—a Messenger who knows that no other Prophet will come after him and must, therefore, anticipate with precision and utmost compassion everything the Ummah needs until the Day of Resurrection.

And certainly, the All-Knowing and Aware God placed everything necessary in this regard at his disposal—from principles and branches to generalities and details in all fields. Thus, at the end of the verse, He says: "And Allah has always been All-Knowing of all things" (وَكَانَ اللَّهُ بِكُلِّ شَيْءٍ عَلِيماً).

It is also noteworthy that being the "Seal of the Prophets" (Khatam al-Anbiya) also means being the "Seal of the Messengers" (Khatam al-Mursalin). The fact that some modern cult-founders have clung to the idea that the Quran calls the Prophet of Islam the "Seal of the Prophets" and not the "Seal of the Messengers" to undermine the issue of Finality is a major error. If someone is the Seal of the Prophets, they are, by greater priority (a fortiori), the Seal of the Messengers as well, because the stage of "Messengership" (Risalat) is a stage beyond the stage of "Prophethood" (Nubuwwat) (Observe carefully).

This argument is exactly like saying: "So-and-so is not in the land of Hijaz"; such a person will certainly not be in Mecca. But if we say "He is not in Mecca," he might be in another part of Hijaz. Therefore, if He had called the Prophet the "Seal of the Messengers," it might have been possible that he was not the "Seal of the Prophets." But when He says he is the "Seal of the Prophets," he will certainly be the "Seal of the Messengers" as well. In technical terms, the relationship between "Prophet" (Nabi) and "Messenger" (Rasul) is one of "absolute generality and specificity" (Again, observe carefully).

Key Points

1- What is "Khatam"?

"Khatam" (rhyming with Hatam), as lexicographers have stated, means that by which something is brought to an end, and it also means that with which papers and the like are sealed.

In the past and today, it has been customary that when people want to close a letter, a container, or a house so that no one opens it, they place a sticky substance on the door or the lock and press a seal onto it, which today is referred to as "wax and seal."

This is done such that to open it, the seal and that sticky substance must be broken. The seal pressed onto such objects is called a "Khatam." Since in the past, hard and sticky clay was sometimes used for this purpose, we read in some famous lexicons that the meaning of Khatam is "that which is placed on clay" (ما يوضع على الطينة) [1]. All of this is because the word is derived from the root Khatm, meaning "end," and since this act (sealing) occurs at the conclusion and end, the tool used for it is named "Khatam."

And if we see that one of the meanings of "Khatam" is a ring, it is also because the designs of seals were usually engraved on rings, and letters were sealed using rings. Therefore, in the biographies of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله), the Imams of Guidance (عليه السلام), and other personalities, one of the issues discussed is the design of their Khatam (seal-ring).

The late Kulayni in al-Kafi narrates from Imam al-Sadiq (عليه السلام): "The seal-ring of the Messenger of Allah was made of silver, and its engraving was 'Muhammad is the Messenger of Allah'" (ان خاتم رسول اللَّه كان من فضة نقشه محمد رسول اللَّه) [2]. In some histories, it is mentioned that one of the events of the sixth year of Hijrah was that the Prophet chose an engraved ring for himself, because he was told that kings do not read letters that are not sealed [3]. In the book al-Tabaqat, it is also stated that when the noble Prophet of Islam decided to expand his invitation and write letters to the kings and monarchs of the earth, he ordered a ring to be made for him on which "Muhammad is the Messenger of Allah" was engraved, and he would seal his letters with it [1].

[1]: Lisan al-Arab and Qamus al-Lughah, root kh-t-m (The Khatam is what is placed on clay). [2]: This report is also cited by Bayhaqi in al-Sunan, Vol. 10, p. 128. [3]: Safinat al-Bihar, Vol. 1, p. 386. [1]: al-Tabaqat al-Kubra, Vol. 1, p. 258.

With this explanation, it becomes clear that although "Khatam" is applied today to decorative rings, its original root is derived from Khatm meaning "end," and in those days, it referred to rings used to seal letters.

Furthermore, this root is used in the Holy Quran in several instances, and in all of them, it means ending and placing a seal, such as: "الْيَوْمَ نَخْتِمُ عَلى‌ أَفْواهِهِمْ وَ تُكَلِّمُنا أَيْدِيهِمْ" ("Today—the Day of Resurrection—We set a seal upon their mouths, and their hands speak to Us") (Ya-Sin: 65).

"خَتَمَ اللَّهُ عَلى‌ قُلُوبِهِمْ وَ عَلى‌ سَمْعِهِمْ وَ عَلى‌ أَبْصارِهِمْ غِشاوَةٌ" ("Allah has set a seal upon their hearts and upon their hearing [such that no truth penetrates them], and over their vision is a veil") (Al-Baqarah: 7).

From this, it is evident that those who have raised doubts about the indication of the verse in question regarding the Finality of the Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله) and the conclusion of the chain of Prophets through him were either completely ignorant of the meaning of this word or feigned ignorance. Otherwise, anyone with the slightest knowledge of Arabic literature knows that the phrase "خَاتَمَ النَّبِيِّينَ" clearly indicates the meaning of Finality.

Moreover, if any interpretation other than this were given for the verse, it would take on a trivial and childish meaning—such as saying the Prophet of Islam was the "ring" of the Prophets, meaning he was considered their ornament. We know that a ring is a simple tool for a human and can never be in the same rank as the human himself. If we were to interpret the verse thus, we would have extraordinarily diminished the status of the Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله), besides the fact that it does not align with the linguistic meaning. Therefore, in all 8 instances where this root is used in the Quran, it consistently means "ending and placing a seal."

2- Proofs for the Finality of the Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله)

Although the above verse is sufficient to prove this matter, the evidence for the Finality of the Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله) is not limited to it. Other verses in the Holy Quran point to this meaning, and numerous narrations have been recorded in this regard.

Among them, in Surah Al-An'am, verse 19, we read: "وَ أُوحِيَ إِلَيَّ هذَا الْقُرْآنُ لِأُنْذِرَكُمْ بِهِ وَ مَنْ بَلَغَ" ("And this Quran has been revealed to me that I may warn you thereby and whomever it reaches").

The breadth of the expression "whomever it reaches" (wa man balagh) clarifies the universal mission of the Quran and the Prophet of Islam on one hand, and the issue of Finality on the other.

Other verses that prove the universality of the Prophet of Islam’s (صلى الله عليه وآله) invitation to all the worlds include: "تَبارَكَ الَّذِي نَزَّلَ الْفُرْقانَ عَلى‌ عَبْدِهِ لِيَكُونَ لِلْعالَمِينَ نَذِيراً" ("Blessed is He who sent down the Criterion upon His Servant that he may be a warner to the worlds") (Al-Furqan: 1).

And: "وَ ما أَرْسَلْناكَ إِلَّا كَافَّةً لِلنَّاسِ بَشِيراً وَ نَذِيراً" ("And We have not sent you except comprehensively to mankind as a bringer of good tidings and a warner") (Saba: 28).

And the verse: "قُلْ يا أَيُّهَا النَّاسُ إِنِّي رَسُولُ اللَّهِ إِلَيْكُمْ جَمِيعاً" ("Say, 'O mankind, indeed I am the Messenger of Allah to you all'") (Al-A'raf: 158).

Considering the vastness of the terms "worlds" ('alamin), "mankind" (nas), and "comprehensively" (kaffatan), these also support this meaning. Beyond this, the consensus of Muslim scholars on one hand, the fact that this issue is a "necessity of religion" (dharuri) among Muslims on the other, and the numerous narrations from the Prophet and other leaders of Islam on a third hand, make the matter even clearer. As an example, we suffice with mentioning a few narrations:

1- In a famous Hadith from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله), we read that he said: "My lawful (halal) is lawful until the Day of Resurrection, and my unlawful (haram) is unlawful until the Day of Resurrection" (حلالى حلال الى يوم القيامة و حرامى حرام الى يوم القيامة) [1]. This expression indicates the continuation of this Sharia until the end of the world.

Sometimes the above Hadith is narrated as: "The lawful of Muhammad is lawful forever until the Day of Resurrection, and his unlawful is unlawful forever until the Day of Resurrection; there shall be nothing else, and no one else shall come" [2].

2- The famous "Hadith of Manzila," which appears in various Shia and Sunni books regarding Ali (عليه السلام) and the story of him staying in Medina in place of the Prophet when the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله) went to the Battle of Tabuk, also completely clarifies the issue of Finality. In this Hadith, we read that the Prophet said to Ali (عليه السلام): "You are to me as Aaron was to Moses, except that there is no Prophet after me" (انت منى بمنزلة هارون من موسى الا انه لا نبى بعدى) (Therefore, you hold all the positions of Aaron relative to Moses except for Prophethood) [3].

[1]: Bihar al-Anwar, Vol. 2, p. 260, Chapter 31, Hadith 17. [2]: Usul al-Kafi, Vol. 1, "Chapter on Innovations, Opinion, and Analogies," Hadith 19. [3]: This Hadith is narrated by Muhibb al-Din al-Tabari in Dhakha'ir al-Uqba, p. 79; Ibn Hajar in al-Sawa'iq al-Muhriqah, p. 177; Tarikh Baghdad, Vol. 7, p. 452; and other books like Kanz al-Ummal and Yanabi' al-Mawaddah.

3- This Hadith is also famous and narrated in many Sunni sources, where he said: "My likeness and the likeness of the Prophets is like a man who built a building and made it beautiful and graceful. People began to walk around it saying, 'We have not seen a building more beautiful than this, except for [the place of] this brick.' I am that [final] brick!" This Hadith is narrated in Sahih Muslim with various wordings and from multiple narrators; in one instance, the following sentence appears at the end: "And I am the Seal of the Prophets" (و انا خاتم النبيين). In another Hadith, we read at its conclusion: "I came and I ended the Prophets" (جئت فختمت الانبياء) [1]. It is also narrated in Sahih al-Bukhari, Musnad Ahmad ibn Hanbal, Sahih al-Tirmidhi, al-Nasa'i, and other books. It is among the very well-known and famous Hadiths, and Shia and Sunni commentators like Tabarsi in Majma' al-Bayan and Qurtubi in his Tafsir have cited it under the verse in question.

4- In many sermons of Nahj al-Balagha, the Finality of the Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله) is explicitly mentioned. For instance, in Sermon 173, describing the Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله), we read: "The trustee of His revelation, the Seal of His Messengers, the bringer of glad tidings of His mercy, and the warner of His punishment" (امين وحيه و خاتم رسله و بشير رحمته و نذير نقمته).

In Sermon 133, it is stated: "He sent him after a gap (fatra) between the Messengers, and when there was contention among tongues; so He followed the Messengers with him and sealed the revelation with him" (ارسله على حين فترة من الرسل... و ختم به الوحى).

In the first sermon of Nahj al-Balagha, after listing the programs of previous Prophets, he says: "Until Allah the Sublime sent Muhammad, the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله), to fulfill His promise and to complete His Prophethood" (الى ان بعث اللَّه سبحانه محمدا رسول اللَّه (ص) لانجاز عدته و اتمام نبوته).

5- At the end of the "Farewell Pilgrimage" sermon—the same sermon the Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله) delivered during his last Hajj and the last year of his blessed life as a comprehensive testament to the people—the issue of Finality is explicitly mentioned: "Lo! Let those present convey to those absent that there is no Prophet after me, and no Ummah after you." Then he raised his hands toward the sky until the whiteness of his armpits became visible and said: "O Allah, bear witness that I have conveyed" (اللهم اشهد انى قد بلغت) [1].

6- In another Hadith in the book al-Kafi from Imam al-Sadiq (عليه السلام), we read: "Indeed, Allah sealed the Prophets with your Prophet, so there is no Prophet after him forever; and He sealed the Books with your Book, so there is no Book after it forever" [2]. The Hadiths in this regard in Islamic sources are so numerous that in the book Ma'alim al-Nubuwwah, 135 Hadiths from the books of Islamic scholars have been collected from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) himself and the great leaders of Islam [1].

[1]: Sahih Muslim, Vol. 4, pp. 1790-1791. [1]: Bihar al-Anwar, Vol. 21, p. 381. [2]: Usul al-Kafi, Vol. 1. [1]: Ma'alim al-Nubuwwah, section on texts regarding Finality.

3- Answering Several Questions

1- How is Finality compatible with the evolutionary path of man?

The first question raised in this discussion is: Is it possible for human society to come to a halt? Does the evolutionary path of man have a limit? Do we not see with our own eyes that today's humans are at a higher stage of science, knowledge, and culture compared to the past?

Given this, how is it possible for the book of Prophethood to be closed entirely, and for man to be deprived of the leadership of new Prophets in this evolutionary journey?

Answer: This question is clarified by noting one point: sometimes man reaches a stage of intellectual and cultural maturity where he can continue the path by continuously utilizing the comprehensive principles and teachings that the Final Prophet placed at his disposal, without needing a new Sharia.

This is exactly like a person who, at different educational levels, needs a new teacher and mentor to pass through various stages. However, once he reaches the doctoral level and becomes a Mujtahid and an expert in a science or various sciences, he no longer continues his studies under a new master. Instead, relying on what he has learned from previous masters—especially the final master—he engages in research, study, and investigation, continuing his evolutionary path. In other words, he solves the needs and problems of the way using those general principles he holds from the last master.

Therefore, it is not necessary that with the passage of time, a new religion and creed must always emerge (Observe carefully).

To put it another way, the previous Prophets, so that man could advance in this path full of ups and downs toward evolution, each provided him with a part of the map of this route, until he gained the merit for the Great God to place the general and comprehensive map of the entire way at his disposal through the final Prophet.

Clearly, upon receiving the general and comprehensive map, there will be no need for another map. This is, in fact, the explanation of the expression found in the narrations of Finality, which counts the Prophet of Islam as the last brick or the one who placed the final brick of the beautiful and sturdy palace of Messengership.

All of this pertains to the lack of need for a new religion. However, the issue of leadership and Imamate—which is the general supervision over the implementation of these principles and laws and assisting those who falter on the path—is another matter that man will never be without. For this reason, the ending of the chain of Prophethood never means the ending of the chain of Imamate, because the "explanation" and "clarification of these principles" and "giving them objectivity and external realization" is impossible without utilizing the existence of a divinely protected (Ma'sum) leader.

2- How do fixed laws reconcile with changing needs?

Beyond the issue of man's evolutionary path mentioned in the first question, another question is raised: We know that the requirements of times and places differ; in other words, human needs are constantly changing, while the Final Sharia has fixed laws. Can these fixed laws respond to the changing needs of man over time?

This question can also be well-answered by noting the following:

If all Islamic laws were specific and had determined a completely distinct and specific ruling for every single subject, this question would be valid. However, considering that in Islamic instructions there is a series of general, very vast, and broad principles that can be applied to changing needs and respond to them, there is no room for this objection.

For example, with the passage of time, a series of new contracts and legal relationships emerge among humans that never existed at the time of the Quran's revelation. For instance, at that time, something called "insurance" with its various branches did not exist at all [1]. Similarly, the various types of companies that have emerged in our era according to modern needs. Yet, we have a general principle in Islam mentioned at the beginning of Surah Al-Ma'idah as the "necessity of fulfilling covenants and contracts" (يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا أَوْفُوا بِالْعُقُودِ—"O you who have believed, fulfill the contracts"), which can cover all these agreements. Of course, there are also general limitations and conditions for this general principle in Islam that must also be considered.

Therefore, the general law in this field is fixed, even though its instances (masadiq) change, and every day a new instance may be found for it.

Another example is that we have a definitive law in Islam called the "Law of No Harm" (La Dharar), through which any ruling that becomes a source of loss or harm in Islamic society can be limited, and many needs can be addressed through this path.

Beyond this, the issues of the "necessity of maintaining social order," the "necessity of the preliminary of an obligation," and the "principle of prioritizing the more important over the important" can also be problem-solvers in very broad cases.

In addition to all this, the authorities granted to the Islamic government through "Wilayat al-Faqih" provide it with vast possibilities to solve problems within the framework of the general principles of Islam.

Of course, explaining each of these matters—especially considering that the door of Ijtihad (in the sense of deducing divine rulings from Islamic sources) is open—requires extensive discussion that would take us away from our goal. Nevertheless, what we have pointed out here can answer the above objection.

3- How are humans deprived of the grace of connection with the Unseen world?

Another question is that the descent of revelation and connection with the world of the Unseen and the supernatural, besides being a blessing and an honor for humanity, is considered a window of hope for all true believers. Does the cutting off of this path of communication and the closing of this window of hope not constitute a great deprivation for humans living after the passing of the Final Prophet?

The answer to this question also becomes clear by noting the following:

Firstly: Revelation and connection with the world of the Unseen is a means for perceiving realities. When what needed to be said has been said, and all requirements until the Day of Resurrection have been expressed in the general principles and comprehensive teachings of the Final Prophet, the cutting off of this communication path no longer creates a problem.

Secondly: What is cut off forever after the end of Prophethood is the issue of revelation for a new Sharia or for completing a previous Sharia, not every kind of connection with the world beyond nature. For both the Imams have a connection with the Unseen world, and so do the true believers who, through self-purification, have removed the veils from their hearts and attained the station of "unveiling and witnessing" (kashf wa shuhud).

The famous philosopher Sadr al-Muta'allihin Shirazi says in Mafatih al-Ghayb: "Revelation—meaning the descent of an angel upon the ear and heart for the purpose of mission and Prophethood—has indeed ceased, and no angel descends upon anyone to command them to execute an order, because according to the verse 'أَكْمَلْتُ لَكُمْ دِينَكُمْ' (I have perfected for you your religion), whatever was to reach man through this path has reached him. However, the door of inspiration (ilham) and illumination (ishraq) has never been closed and will never be; it is impossible for this path to be blocked" [1].

Fundamentally, this connection is the result of the elevation of the soul, the refinement of the spirit, and the purity of the inner self, and it is not related to the issue of Messengership and Prophethood. Therefore, at any time the preliminaries and conditions are met, this spiritual relationship will be established, and mankind has never been and will never be deprived of this great grace (Observe carefully).

[1]: Mafatih al-Ghayb, p. 13.

Ayahs

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ ٱذْكُرُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ ذِكْرًا كَثِيرًا (٤١)
O you who have faith! Remember Allah with frequent remembrance, (41)
وَسَبِّحُوهُ بُكْرَةً وَأَصِيلًا (٤٢)
and glorify Him morning and evening. (42)
هُوَ ٱلَّذِى يُصَلِّى عَلَيْكُمْ وَمَلَـٰٓئِكَتُهُۥ لِيُخْرِجَكُم مِّنَ ٱلظُّلُمَـٰتِ إِلَى ٱلنُّورِ ۚ وَكَانَ بِٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ رَحِيمًا (٤٣)
It is He who blesses you—and so do His angels—that He may bring you out from darkness into light, and He is most merciful to the faithful. (43)
تَحِيَّتُهُمْ يَوْمَ يَلْقَوْنَهُۥ سَلَـٰمٌ ۚ وَأَعَدَّ لَهُمْ أَجْرًا كَرِيمًا (٤٤)
The day they encounter Him, their greeting will be, ‘Peace,’ and He holds in store for them a noble reward. (44)

Commentary

The Mercy and Blessings of Allah and the Angels: A Path for the Believers

Since the preceding verses discussed the heavy responsibilities of the Prophet of Islam صلي الله عليه وآله in conveying the Message, the verses under discussion outline a portion of the duties of the believers to prepare the ground for this mission and expand its reach across all environments.

Addressing all of them, He says: "O you who have believed, remember Allah with much remembrance" (يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا اذْكُرُوا اللَّهَ ذِكْراً كَثِيراً).

"And exalt Him morning and afternoon" (وَ سَبِّحُوهُ بُكْرَةً وَ أَصِيلًا). Indeed, because the factors of negligence in material life are so numerous, and the arrows of the devils' temptations are launched at man from every direction, there is no way to combat them except through "much remembrance" (dhikr kathir). "Much remembrance," in the true sense of the word, means "turning toward God with one's entire being," not merely with the tongue and empty lip service.

It is a "much remembrance" that radiates upon all of a person's actions and sheds light and brilliance upon them. In this way, the Quran mandates all believers in this verse to be mindful of Allah at all times.

At the time of worship, remember Him and maintain presence of heart and sincerity. When facing scenes of sin, remember Him and avert your gaze; or if a slip occurs, repent and return to the path of truth. At the time of blessing, remember Him and be grateful. And at the time of calamity and hardship, remember Him and be patient and steadfast.

In short, do not forget His remembrance, which is the motive for an appropriate and divine reaction in every scene of life.

In a hadith narrated in Sahih al-Tirmidhi and Musnad Ahmad from Abu Sa'id al-Khudri, from the Noble Prophet of Islam صلي الله عليه وآله, we read that he was asked: "Which of the servants has the highest rank before Allah on the Day of Resurrection?" He replied: "Those who remember Allah much" (الذاكرون اللَّه كثيرا).

Abu Sa'id says: I asked, "O Messenger of Allah! Even more than the warrior in the way of Allah?" He replied: "Even if he strikes with his sword among the disbelievers and polytheists until it breaks and is dyed with blood, those who remember Allah much are superior in rank to him!" [^1] This is because even sincere Jihad is not possible without the "much remembrance" of Allah.

From this, it becomes clear that "much remembrance" has a broad meaning. If in some narrations it is interpreted as the Tasbih of Lady Fatima Zahra عليها السلام (34 times Allahu Akbar, 33 times Alhamdulillah, and 33 times Subhan Allah), or in the words of some commentators as the mention of the Supreme Attributes and the Beautiful Names, and the sanctification of the Lord from what is not worthy of Him, these are all by way of mentioning the clearest examples, not limiting the concept of the verse to these specific instances.

Just as it is clearly understood from the context of the verses, the purpose of "exalting Allah every morning and afternoon" is the continuity of glorification, and the mention of these two specific times is as the beginning and end of the day. Some have interpreted it as the morning and afternoon prayers, which is again an example of mentioning a specific instance.

Thus, "much remembrance of Allah and His glorification every morning and afternoon" is achieved only through continuous attention to the Lord and constant sanctification and hallowing of Him from every defect and deficiency. We know that the remembrance of Allah is for the soul and spirit what food and water are for the body.

[^1]: Ad-Durr al-Manthur, as quoted in Al-Mizan, Vol. 16, p. 353.

In Surah Ar-Ra'd, verse 28, it is stated: "Unquestionably, by the remembrance of Allah hearts are assured" (أَلا بِذِكْرِ اللَّهِ تَطْمَئِنُّ الْقُلُوبُ). The result of this tranquility and assurance of the heart is what appears in Surah Al-Fajr, verses 27-30: "O reassured soul, return to your Lord, well-pleased and pleasing [to Him], and enter among My [righteous] servants, and enter My Paradise" (يَا أَيَّتُهَا النَّفْسُ الْمُطْمَئِنَّةُ ارْجِعِي إِلى‌ رَبِّكِ راضِيَةً مَرْضِيَّةً فَادْخُلِي فِي عِبادِي وَ ادْخُلِي جَنَّتِي).

The next verse is, in fact, the result and the ultimate cause of constant remembrance and glorification. It says: "It is He who confers blessing upon you, and His angels [ask Him to do so] that He may bring you out from darknesses into the light. And ever is He, to the believers, Merciful" (هُوَ الَّذِي يُصَلِّي عَلَيْكُمْ وَ مَلائِكَتُهُ لِيُخْرِجَكُمْ مِنَ الظُّلُماتِ إِلَى النُّورِ).

"For He is Merciful to the believers," and for this reason, He has taken upon Himself their guidance and leadership and has commissioned His angels to assist them (وَ كانَ بِالْمُؤْمِنِينَ رَحِيماً).

"Yusalli" (يصلى) is derived from the root "Salat," which here means special attention and grace. This grace, in the case of Allah, is the sending down of mercy, and in the case of the angels, it is seeking forgiveness and requesting mercy, as we read in Surah Ghafir, verse 7: "And they ask forgiveness for those who have believed" (وَ يَسْتَغْفِرُونَ لِلَّذِينَ آمَنُوا).

In any case, this verse contains a great glad tiding and a magnificent promise for the believers who are constantly mindful of Allah. It explicitly states that they are not alone in their journey toward Allah; rather, according to the word "Yusalli"—which is a present continuous verb indicating persistence—they are always under the cover of the mercy of Allah and His angels. In the shadow of this mercy, the veils of darkness are torn asunder, and He sprinkles the light of knowledge, wisdom, faith, and piety upon their hearts and souls.

Indeed, this verse is a great glad tiding for all wayfarers on the path of truth, promising them that there is a powerful attraction from the "Beloved" so that the effort of the "wretched lover" may reach its destination! This verse is a guarantee for all strivers in the way of "Allah" that Satan's oath regarding the seduction of the children of Adam will not affect them, for they are among the sincere and purified ones. Satan, from the very first day, expressed his inability to mislead them, saying: "By Your might, I will surely mislead them all, except, among them, Your chosen servants" (فَبِعِزَّتِكَ لَأُغْوِيَنَّهُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ إِلَّا عِبادَكَ مِنْهُمُ الْمُخْلَصِينَ) (Surah Sad, 82-83).

The phrase "And ever is He, to the believers, Merciful" (وَ كانَ بِالْمُؤْمِنِينَ رَحِيماً), considering that "kana" (كان) is a past tense verb indicating that Allah has always had a special mercy toward the believers, is a renewed emphasis on what appeared at the beginning of this verse. Yes, it is this special mercy of God that brings the believers out of the darkness of illusions, desires, and satanic whispers, and guides them toward the light of certainty, assurance, and self-mastery. If it were not for His mercy, this winding path would never be traversed.

In the final verse under discussion, the station of the believers and their reward is depicted in the most sublime manner and the shortest phrase: "Their greeting on the Day they meet Him will be, 'Peace'" (تَحِيَّتُهُمْ يَوْمَ يَلْقَوْنَهُ سَلامٌ). "Tahiyyah" (greeting) is from the root "hayat" (life), meaning to pray for the health and life of another.

This "Salam" is a sign of safety from punishment and from every kind of pain, suffering, and discomfort; it is a peace accompanied by tranquility and assurance.

Although some commentators believe that "tahiyyatuhum" refers to the greeting of the believers to one another, given the previous verses which spoke of the Salat and mercy of Allah and the angels in this world, it is apparent that this greeting is also from the angels in the Hereafter. As we read in Surah Ar-Ra'd, verse 23: "And the angels will enter upon them from every gate, [saying], 'Peace be upon you for what you patiently endured'" (وَ الْمَلائِكَةُ يَدْخُلُونَ عَلَيْهِمْ مِنْ كُلِّ بابٍ سَلامٌ عَلَيْكُمْ بِما صَبَرْتُمْ).

From what we have said, it also becomes clear that the phrase "the Day they meet Him" (يَوْمَ يَلْقَوْنَهُ) refers to the Day of Resurrection, which is called the "Day of Meeting Allah" (Liqa' Allah), and this expression is usually used in the verses of the Quran with this meaning.

After this greeting, which in fact pertains to the beginning of the matter, He points to their end, saying: "And He has prepared for them a noble reward" (وَ أَعَدَّ لَهُمْ أَجْراً كَرِيماً). A phrase that, despite its brevity, encompasses everything, and all blessings and favors are hidden within it.

Key Points

1. Remembrance of Allah in All Circumstances

When the name of Allah is mentioned, a world of greatness, power, knowledge, and wisdom manifests in the human heart, for He possesses the Beautiful Names and the Supreme Attributes, is the owner of all perfections, and is free from any defect or deficiency.

Constant attention to such a Reality, who possesses such attributes, draws the human soul toward goodness and purity and cleanses it from evil and ugliness. In other words, the reflection of His attributes manifests in the human soul. Attention to such a Great Deity results in a sense of permanent presence before Him, and with this feeling, the distance between man and sin or pollution becomes very great.

Remembering Him is remembering His watchfulness; it is remembering His reckoning and reward; it is remembering His court of justice, His Paradise, and His Hell. Such remembrance brings clarity to the soul and light and life to the heart. For this reason, it is stated in Islamic narrations that everything has a measure except the remembrance of Allah, for which there is no limit or boundary! Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, according to a narration in Usul al-Kafi, says: "There is nothing but that it has a limit where it ends, except for dhikr (remembrance), for it has no limit where it ends!"

He then adds: "Allah, the Almighty and Majestic, has ordained the obligatory duties; whoever performs them has fulfilled their limit. The month of Ramadan—whoever fasts it has fulfilled its limit. Hajj—whoever performs it (once) has fulfilled its limit. Except for the remembrance of Allah, for Allah, the Almighty and Majestic, is not satisfied with a small amount of it and has not set a limit for its abundance." Then he recited: "O you who have believed, remember Allah with much remembrance and exalt Him morning and afternoon..." [^1]

Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, in the continuation of this same narration, quotes from his father, Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام, that he was "much in remembrance" (kathir al-dhikr). Whenever I walked with him, he would remember Allah, and while eating, he was also occupied with the remembrance of Allah. Even when he spoke with people, he was not heedless of the remembrance of Allah...

Finally, the above hadith ends with this meaningful sentence: "The house in which the Quran is recited and Allah, the Almighty and Majestic, is remembered, its blessings will increase, the angels will be present in it, the devils will desert it, and it will shine for the inhabitants of heaven just as the brilliant star shines for the inhabitants of the earth." (Conversely, the house in which the Quran is not recited and Allah is not remembered will have few blessings, the angels will migrate from it, and the devils will be permanently present in it). [^2]

This subject is so important that in one hadith, the remembrance of Allah is considered equivalent to all the good of this world and the Hereafter, as narrated from the Messenger of Allah صلي الله عليه وآله: "He who has been given a remembering tongue has been given the good of this world and the Hereafter." [^3]

The narrations regarding the importance of remembering Allah are so numerous that if we were to include them all here, we would exceed the scope of this book. We conclude this discussion with another short and meaningful hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, where he said: "Whoever remembers Allah, the Almighty and Majestic, much, Allah will shade him in His Paradise under His grace!" [^1] (Those who wish to gain more knowledge in this field may refer to the second volume of Usul al-Kafi, the chapters concerning the remembrance of Allah, especially the chapter stating that many afflictions, calamities, and misfortunes do not affect those who remember Allah).

We do not find it necessary to emphasize that all these blessings and benefits certainly do not pertain to a mere verbal remembrance and movement of the tongue devoid of thought, reflection, and action. Rather, the goal is a remembrance that becomes a source of thought—the very thought whose widespread reflection becomes apparent in human actions. As stated explicitly in narrations to this effect. [^2]

2. An Explanation Regarding "Meeting Allah" (Liqa' Allah)

We said that this expression in the Holy Quran usually refers to the Resurrection. Since a sensory meeting is meaningless regarding the Lord—as He is neither a body nor subject to physical qualities—some commentators have found it necessary to assume a "hidden word" (mudaf) here and say the meaning is "meeting the reward of Allah" or "meeting the angels of Allah."

However, "meeting" (liqa') can be taken here in the sense of a true meeting with the "eye of the heart," for on the Day of Resurrection, the veils are removed, and the greatness of Allah and His signs manifest more clearly than ever. Man reaches the station of inner witnessing and heart-vision, and everyone, according to the extent of their knowledge and righteous deeds, attains a higher level of this witnessing.

[^1]: Kafi, Vol. 2, Book of Supplication, "Chapter on the Remembrance of Allah." [^2] and [^3]: Ibid.

Fakhr al-Razi, in his commentary here, has another interesting explanation that can be reconciled with what we have said. He says: "In this world, man is often heedless of God due to being drowned in material affairs and the struggle for livelihood. But in the Resurrection, when all these mental preoccupations are removed, man turns toward the Lord of the worlds with his entire being, and this is the meaning of 'meeting Allah'." [^1]

Furthermore, from what we have said, it becomes clear that the statement of some commentators who considered this expression to refer to the moment of death and meeting the Angel of Death is neither suitable for the verses under discussion nor for similar expressions in other verses of the Quran. Especially since the object pronoun in the phrase "yalqawnahu" (meeting Him) is singular, referring to the Pure Essence of Allah, while the angels of death are plural, and the word "mala'ikah" (angels) in the previous verse also appeared in the plural form (unless a word is assumed to be hidden).

3. The Rewards of the Believers are Already Prepared!

The phrase "He has prepared for them a noble reward" (أعد لهم اجرا كريما), which shows that Paradise and its blessings are already created and awaiting the believers, might raise the question: "Preparation" is appropriate for those whose power is limited and who might sometimes be unable to provide what they want when needed. But before the unlimited power of the Lord, who, whenever He wills something, commands "Be!" and it immediately is, such a need is not felt. So, what is the purpose of emphasizing "preparation" in this verse and other verses of the Quran?

However, attention to one point solves the problem: preparing something is not always due to limited power; rather, it is sometimes for greater encouragement and peace of mind, and sometimes for greater respect and honor. Therefore, if we invite a guest and have been busy preparing the means of hospitality for some time beforehand, we have shown him more respect and importance. Conversely, if we start preparing the means of hospitality on the very day and hour of his arrival, this is considered a kind of indifference and lack of respect.

At the same time, this does not prevent the prepared divine rewards from becoming more perfect and advancing toward completion in parallel with the efforts of the people of faith in self-building, knowledge, and purity of action.

[^1]: Tafsir al-Kabir by Fakhr al-Razi, under the verse discussed. [^2]: Al-Khisal by al-Saduq, as quoted in Tafsir al-Mizan, Vol. 16, p. 353.

Ayahs

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلنَّبِىُّ إِنَّآ أَرْسَلْنَـٰكَ شَـٰهِدًا وَمُبَشِّرًا وَنَذِيرًا (٤٥)
O Prophet! Indeed We have sent you as a witness, as a bearer of good news and as a warner (45)
وَدَاعِيًا إِلَى ٱللَّهِ بِإِذْنِهِۦ وَسِرَاجًا مُّنِيرًا (٤٦)
and as a summoner to Allah by His permission, and as a radiant lamp. (46)
وَبَشِّرِ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ بِأَنَّ لَهُم مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ فَضْلًا كَبِيرًا (٤٧)
Announce to the faithful the good news that there will be for them a great grace from Allah. (47)
وَلَا تُطِعِ ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ وَٱلْمُنَـٰفِقِينَ وَدَعْ أَذَىٰهُمْ وَتَوَكَّلْ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ ۚ وَكَفَىٰ بِٱللَّهِ وَكِيلًا (٤٨)
Do not obey the faithless and the hypocrites, and disregard their torments, and put your trust in Allah, and Allah suffices as trustee. (48)

Commentary

You Are a Radiant Lamp!

In these verses, the address is directed toward the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, but the result is for the believers; thus, it completes the previous verses which discussed a portion of the duties of the believers.

In the first two verses of these four, five descriptions are mentioned for the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, and in the other two verses, five duties are stated, all of which are interrelated and complementary to one another.

First, He says: "O Prophet! Truly We have sent you as a witness" (يَا أَيُّهَا النَّبِيُّ إِنَّا أَرْسَلْنَاكَ شَاهِدًا).

On one hand, he is a witness over the deeds of the Ummah, for he sees their actions, as we read elsewhere: "And say: 'Work; for Allah will see your work, and His Messenger, and the believers'" (At-Tawbah: 105). This awareness is realized through the presentation of the Ummah's deeds to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and the Imams عليه السلام, the explanation of which was provided under that same verse (Volume 8, page 124).

On the other hand, he is a witness over the previous prophets, who were themselves witnesses over their own nations: "How then shall it be when We bring from every nation a witness, and We bring you as a witness against these?" (An-Nisa: 41).

And from a third perspective, your very existence—with your attributes and character, your constructive programs, your brilliant background, and your conduct—is a witness and testimony to the truth of your school of thought, and a witness to the greatness and power of the Lord.

Then, moving to the second and third descriptions, He says: "And as a bearer of glad tidings and a warner" (وَ مُبَشِّراً وَ نَذِيراً).

A bearer of glad tidings to the righteous of the Lord's endless reward, of eternal health and felicity, and of proud victory and success.

And a warner to the disbelievers and hypocrites of the painful divine punishment, of the loss of all existential capital, and of falling into the lap of misery in this world and the Hereafter.

As we have stated before, glad tidings and warning must always be coupled and balanced with one another, because half of human existence is formed by the desire to gain benefit and the other half by the repulsion of harm. "Glad tidings" provides the motive for the first part, and "warning" provides the motive for the second. Those who emphasize only one part in their programs have not truly recognized man nor considered his motives for movement. [^1]

The next verse points to the fourth and fifth descriptions of the Prophet, saying: "And as one who invites to Allah by His permission, and as a lamp spreading light" (وَ دَاعِيًا إِلَى اللَّهِ بِإِذْنِهِ وَ سِرَاجًا مُنِيرًا).

Here, several points should be noted

  1. The status of "witnessing" (shuhud) and being a witness for the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله is mentioned before all his other attributes, because this status requires no prerequisite other than the existence of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and his mission. As soon as he is appointed to this station, his being a witness from all the aspects mentioned above becomes certain. However, the stations of "glad tidings" and "warning" are programs that are realized afterward.

  2. Inviting toward Allah is a stage that comes after glad tidings and warning, because glad tidings and warning are means to prepare individuals to accept the truth. Once the readiness to accept is achieved through encouragement and threat, the invitation toward Allah begins; it is only here that the invitation will be effective.

  3. Although all the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله actions are by the permission and command of Allah, here only the program of invitation is restricted by the Lord's permission. This is because the most difficult and important task of the prophets is this very invitation toward Allah, as they must move people along a path contrary to their whims and desires. In this stage, there must be the permission, command, and help of Allah for it to be accomplished. Furthermore, it clarifies that the Prophet has nothing of his own, and whatever he says is by Allah's permission. [^1]

  4. The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله being a "Siraaj Muneer" (radiant lamp)—considering that "Siraaj" means "lamp" and "Muneer" means "light-giving"—refers to the miracles, the proofs of truthfulness, and the signs of the sincerity of the Prophet's invitation. He is a bright lamp that is its own witness; he dispels darkness and gloom, and draws eyes and hearts toward himself. Just as the arrival of the sun is the proof of the sun, his existence is the proof of his truthfulness.

It is noteworthy that the word "Siraaj" appears four times in the Holy Quran, and in three instances it means "the sun," including in Surah Nuh, verse 16, where He says: "And He made the moon a light therein, and made the sun a lamp (Siraaj)."

We said "Siraaj" originally means a lamp which, in the past, used a wick and "combustible oil," and today uses electrical power and the like as a source of light and illumination. However, according to Raghib in Al-Mufradat, this word gradually came to be applied to any source of light and illumination. Its application to the sun is because its light gushes from within it, and unlike the moon, it does not acquire light from another source.

The existence of the Prophet is like a shining sun that dispels the darkness of ignorance, polytheism, and disbelief from the horizon of the human soul. However, just as the blind do not benefit from sunlight, and bats whose eyes lack the ability to see this light hide themselves from it, the stubborn and blind-hearted have never benefited and will never benefit from this light; the likes of Abu Jahl would put their fingers in their ears so as not to hear the melody of his Quran.

Darkness and gloom are always a source of anxiety and terror, while light is a cause of tranquility. Thieves utilize the darkness of night, and the predatory animals of the wilderness often emerge from their dens in the dark of night.

Darkness is a cause of scattering, while light is a cause of gathering and assembly. For this reason, if we light a lamp on a dark night in the middle of a desert, various insects will gather around it in a short time.

Illumination and light are the cause of the growth of trees, the nurturing of flowers, the ripening of fruits, and in short, all vital activities. Likening the existence of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله to a source of light evokes all these concepts in the mind.

The existence of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله is a source of tranquility, the cause for the flight of the thieves of religion and faith and the ruthless wolves of society, a cause for composure of the heart, and the nurturing and growth of the spirit of faith and morality—in short, the source of life, vitality, and movement. The history of his life is a living witness to this subject.

We said that in the other two verses under discussion, five important duties of the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله are stated following the mention of his five attributes. First, He says:

"And give glad tidings to the believers that for them from Allah is a great grace" (وَ بَشِّرِ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ بِأَنَّ لَهُمْ مِنَ اللَّهِ فَضْلًا كَبِيراً).

This indicates that the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله "giving of glad tidings" is not limited only to the reward for the good deeds of the believers; rather, Allah bestows so much of His grace upon them that it completely overturns the balance between deed and reward, as other verses of the Quran are eloquent witnesses to this claim.

In one place He says: "Whoever brings a good deed shall have ten times the like thereof" (Al-An'am: 160).

In another place He says: "The likeness of those who spend their wealth in the way of Allah is as the likeness of a grain that grows seven ears, in every ear a hundred grains. And Allah multiplies for whom He wills" (Al-Baqarah: 261), according to which the reward for spending is sometimes seven hundred times, and sometimes more than thousands of times.

And sometimes it goes even beyond this and says: "No soul knows what has been hidden for them of the delight of the eyes" (As-Sajdah: 17).

In this way, He takes the dimensions of the "Great Grace" (Fadl Kabir) further and beyond what can be contained in imagination or conception.

After that, He moves to the second and third commands, saying: "And do not obey the disbelievers and the hypocrites" (وَ لَا تُطِعِ الْكَافِرِينَ وَ الْمُنَافِقِينَ).

Undoubtedly, the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله never obeyed the disbelievers or the hypocrites, but the importance of the subject is such that He emphasizes it specifically as a reinforcement for the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله himself and as a warning and example for others. This is because one of the major dangers facing true leaders is being drawn toward compromise and surrender during the journey, the grounds for which are sometimes prepared through threats and sometimes through the offering of concessions—to the point where a person might mistakenly think the way to reach the goal is to yield to such compromise and surrender; the very compromise and surrender whose result is the failure of all efforts and the neutralization of all struggles.

The history of Islam shows that many times the disbelievers or groups of hypocrites tried to pull the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله toward such a position. Sometimes they suggested that he should not speak ill of the idols or criticize them; sometimes they said, "Let us worship your God for one year and you worship our gods for one year"; sometimes they said, "Give us a respite to continue our programs for another year and then we will believe"; sometimes they suggested, "Drive away the destitute and poor believers from around you so that we, the influential wealthy, may join you"; and sometimes they declared readiness to give financial concessions, sensitive positions and ranks, beautiful women, and the like! It is certain that all of these were dangerous traps in the path of the rapid progress of Islam and the uprooting of disbelief and hypocrisy. If the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله had shown any flexibility or softness toward one of these proposals, the foundations of the Islamic revolution would have collapsed and the efforts would never have reached fruition.

Then, in the fourth and fifth commands, He says: "And disregard their annoyance, and put your trust in Allah; and Allah is sufficient as a Disposer of affairs" (وَ دَعْ أَذَاهُمْ وَ تَوَكَّلْ عَلَى اللَّهِ وَ كَفَى بِاللَّهِ وَكِيلًا).

This part of the verse shows that they had put the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله under severe pressure to make him surrender, and they inflicted various types of annoyance—whether through verbal abuse, slander, and insolence, or through physical harm, or through economic blockade—upon him and his companions. Of course, this took one form during the Meccan period and another during the Medinan period, for "Adha" (annoyance/harm) is a word that encompasses all types of harm.

Raghib says in Al-Mufradat: "Adha means any kind of harm that reaches a living being, whether in soul, body, or those related to him, whether worldly or otherworldly."

Of course, this word is sometimes used in the verses of the Quran specifically for "verbal annoyance," such as verse 61 of Surah At-Tawbah: "And among them are those who annoy the Prophet and say, 'He is an ear (all ears/gullible)'."

But in other verses, it is also used for physical harm, such as verse 16 of Surah An-Nisa: "And the two who commit it (the shameful act) among you, punish (annoy) them both (and carry out the legal penalty upon them)."

History tells us that the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and the early believers stood like a mountain against all types of annoyance and never accepted the disgrace of surrender or defeat, and finally succeeded in their goals.

The reason for this resistance and victory was that very trust in Allah and reliance on His Pure Essence—the God for Whom all difficulties are easy before His will, and "If the sword of the world moves from its place, it shall not cut a vein unless God wills." Yes, it is sufficient for a person's supporter and refuge to be such a God.

From what we have said, this reality becomes clear: the content of the above verse is not something that was abrogated by the revelation of the command for Jihad—as some commentators have imagined. Rather, it appears that these verses were revealed long after the command for Jihad and alongside the events related to Surah Al-Ahzab. This is a decree for all ages and centuries: that divine leaders should not spend their vital energies paying attention to the annoying acts of the opponents. If they do pay attention and spend their active forces countering these matters, the enemy has reached his goal, for he wants to occupy the opponent's mind and waste his energies through this means. Here, disregard and the command "disregard their annoyance" (da' adhahum) is the only solution.

It is also noteworthy that the five commands mentioned in the last two verses are complementary and related to each other. Giving glad tidings to the believers to attract faithful forces, not compromising or surrendering to the disbelievers and hypocrites, disregarding their annoyances, and trusting in Allah form a collection in which the way to reach the destination is hidden; it is a comprehensive instruction for all wayfarers of the path of truth.

[^1]: In the first volume, under verse 119 of Surah Al-Baqarah, we have provided a detailed discussion on this subject under the title "Two Important Educational Principles" (Volume 1, new edition, page 435).

[^1]: There is a possibility that the qualification "by His permission" (bi-idhnihi) refers back to all the preceding attributes, but the apparent meaning of the verse is that it refers only to the matter of "inviting toward Allah."

Ayah

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوٓا۟ إِذَا نَكَحْتُمُ ٱلْمُؤْمِنَـٰتِ ثُمَّ طَلَّقْتُمُوهُنَّ مِن قَبْلِ أَن تَمَسُّوهُنَّ فَمَا لَكُمْ عَلَيْهِنَّ مِنْ عِدَّةٍ تَعْتَدُّونَهَا ۖ فَمَتِّعُوهُنَّ وَسَرِّحُوهُنَّ سَرَاحًا جَمِيلًا (٤٩)
O you who have faith! When you marry faithful women and then divorce them before you touch them, there shall be no period for you to reckon. But provide for them and release them in a graceful manner. (49)

Commentary

A Glimpse into the Rules of Divorce and Honorable Separation

The various sections of the verses in this Surah (Al-Ahzab) are presented as diverse sets; some are addressed to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) and others to all believers. Thus, it sometimes says, "O Prophet!" and at other times, "O you who have believed!" Necessary instructions are provided in parallel within these verses, indicating that both the person of the Prophet and the general body of believers are intended in these programs.

The verse under discussion is one of these addresses where the speech is directed toward all people of faith, whereas in the preceding verses, the speech was apparently directed to the person of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله), and in the upcoming verses, the turn for addressing the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) arrives once more. This forms what is termed "ordered distribution" (Laff wa Nashr Murattab) in a portion of this Surah.

It states: "O you who have believed, when you marry believing women and then divorce them before you have touched them, then there is no period of waiting (iddah) for you to count against them" (يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا إِذَا نَكَحْتُمُ الْمُؤْمِنَاتِ ثُمَّ طَلَّقْتُمُوهُنَّ مِنْ قَبْلِ أَنْ تَمَسُّوهُنَّ فَمَا لَكُمْ عَلَيْهِنَّ مِنْ عِدَّةٍ تَعْتَدُّونَهَا).

Here, Allah has stated an exception to the rule of the waiting period (iddah) for divorced women: if the divorce occurs before consummation, observing the iddah is not necessary. From this expression, it is understood that the general rule of iddah had been established prior to this verse.

The use of the term "believing women" (al-mu'minat) does not imply that marrying non-Muslim women is entirely prohibited; rather, it may be an indication of their priority. Therefore, it does not contradict the narrations and the famous fatwas of jurists who permit temporary marriage with women of the People of the Book.

Furthermore, from the expression "for you" (lakum) and also the phrase "you count" (ta'taddunaha), it is inferred that the woman's observance of the iddah is considered a type of right for the man. This must be so, because it is possible that the woman is actually pregnant, and abandoning the iddah to marry another man would cause the status of the child to become uncertain, thereby trampling the man's right in this regard. Beyond this, observing the iddah provides an opportunity for the man and woman—if they had consented to divorce under the influence of temporary emotions—to find a chance for reconsideration and reconciliation. This is a right for both the woman and the man.

As for the objection raised by some that if the iddah is the man's right, he should be able to waive it, this is incorrect. In jurisprudence, we have many rights that cannot be waived, such as the right the survivors of a deceased person have in his property, or the right the poor have in Zakat; none of these can be nullified by a waiver.

Then, He addresses another rule regarding "women who are divorced before sexual intercourse," which was also mentioned in Surah Al-Baqarah, saying: "Provide for them (with a suitable gift)" (فَمَتِّعُوهُنَّ).

Undoubtedly, providing a suitable gift to the woman is obligatory in cases where no dower (mahr) has been specified for her, as stated in verse 236 of Surah Al-Baqarah: "There is no blame upon you if you divorce women while you have not touched them or specified for them an obligation (dower). But give them a gift (suitable to their circumstances)."

Therefore, although the verse under discussion is general and includes both cases where a dower has been specified and where it has not, by the context of the verse in Surah Al-Baqarah, we limit the verse under discussion to the case where no dower has been specified. This is because if the dower was specified and consummation did not occur, the payment of half the dower is obligatory (as stated in verse 237 of Surah Al-Baqarah).

Some commentators and jurists have also suggested the possibility that the command to "provide a suitable gift" in the verse under discussion is general, even including cases where the dower was specified; however, in those cases, it takes on a recommended (mustahabb) aspect, while in cases where no dower was specified, it is obligatory (wajib).

An indication of this meaning is also seen in some verses and narrations [1]. Regarding how much this "gift" should be, the Holy Quran in Surah Al-Baqarah explains it briefly, saying: "a provision according to what is fair" (مَتَاعًا بِالْمَعْرُوفِ) (Al-Baqarah: 236). In the same verse, it says: "upon the wealthy according to his capability and upon the poor according to his capability" (عَلَى الْمُوسِعِ قَدَرُهُ وَعَلَى الْمُقْتِرِ قَدَرُهُ).

Therefore, if Islamic narrations mention items such as a house, a servant, clothing, and the like, these are expressions of instances of this general principle, which varies according to the husband's means and the woman's status.

The final ruling of the verse under discussion is: "and release them in a handsome manner" (وَسَرِّحُوهُنَّ سَرَاحًا جَمِيلًا). "Handsome release" (sarahan jamila) means a release accompanied by kindness and respect, and the abandonment of any form of violence, injustice, oppression, or disrespect. In short, as stated in verse 229 of Surah Al-Baqarah, one must either retain the spouse honorably or release her with kindness and respect: "either keep [her] in an acceptable manner or release [her] with good treatment" (فَإِمْسَاكٌ بِمَعْرُوفٍ أَوْ تَسْرِيحٌ بِإِحْسَانٍ).

Both the continuation of marriage and the separation must be coupled with human standards; it is not the case that whenever a husband decides on separation, he considers every form of unkindness, oppression, slander, and violence toward his wife permissible. Such behavior is certainly un-Islamic.

Some commentators have interpreted "handsome release" to mean performing the divorce according to the Islamic Sunnah, and this meaning is reflected in a narration found in the Tafsir of "Ali ibn Ibrahim" and "Uyun al-Akhbar." However, it is certain that "handsome release" is not limited to this meaning, although it is one of its clear examples.

Other commentators have understood "handsome release" here to mean permission to leave the house and relocate, because the woman here is not obligated to observe the iddah; therefore, she should be released to go wherever she wishes.

However, considering that the expression "handsome release" or its equivalent appears in other verses of the Quran even regarding women who must observe the iddah, the above meaning seems unlikely.

Regarding the original meaning of "sarah" and its linguistic root, and why it is used in common parlance to mean "releasing," we provided an explanation under verse 28 of this same Surah (Al-Ahzab).

[^1]: Such as verse 241 of Surah Al-Baqarah, and numerous narrations in this regard found in Chapter 50 of the Chapters on Dowers in the Book of Marriage in Wasa'il al-Shia (Vol. 15, p. 59). Among them, we read in a narration from Ali (عليه السلام): "For every divorced woman there should be a suitable gift, except for the woman of Khul' (who seeks divorce by returning her dower or something else to her husband to gain his consent for divorce)."

Ayah

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلنَّبِىُّ إِنَّآ أَحْلَلْنَا لَكَ أَزْوَٰجَكَ ٱلَّـٰتِىٓ ءَاتَيْتَ أُجُورَهُنَّ وَمَا مَلَكَتْ يَمِينُكَ مِمَّآ أَفَآءَ ٱللَّهُ عَلَيْكَ وَبَنَاتِ عَمِّكَ وَبَنَاتِ عَمَّـٰتِكَ وَبَنَاتِ خَالِكَ وَبَنَاتِ خَـٰلَـٰتِكَ ٱلَّـٰتِى هَاجَرْنَ مَعَكَ وَٱمْرَأَةً مُّؤْمِنَةً إِن وَهَبَتْ نَفْسَهَا لِلنَّبِىِّ إِنْ أَرَادَ ٱلنَّبِىُّ أَن يَسْتَنكِحَهَا خَالِصَةً لَّكَ مِن دُونِ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ ۗ قَدْ عَلِمْنَا مَا فَرَضْنَا عَلَيْهِمْ فِىٓ أَزْوَٰجِهِمْ وَمَا مَلَكَتْ أَيْمَـٰنُهُمْ لِكَيْلَا يَكُونَ عَلَيْكَ حَرَجٌ ۗ وَكَانَ ٱللَّهُ غَفُورًا رَّحِيمًا (٥٠)
O Prophet! Indeed We have made lawful to you your wives whom you have given their dowries, and those whom your right hand owns, of those whom Allah gave you as spoils of war, and the daughters of your paternal uncle, and the daughters of your paternal aunts, and the daughters of your maternal uncle, and the daughters of your maternal aunts who migrated with you, and a faithful woman if she offers herself to the Prophet and the Prophet desires to take her in marriage (a privilege exclusively for you, not for [the rest of] the faithful; We know what We have made lawful for them with respect to their wives and those whom their right hands own, so that there may be no blame on you ), and Allah is all-forgiving, all-merciful. (50)

Commentary

With These Women You May Marry

We have noted that sections of the verses in this Surah follow the duties of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) and the believers in an ordered manner of "fold and unfold" (distribution and summary). Therefore, after mentioning some rulings related to divorcing women in the previous verse, the address here turns to the person of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) himself, explaining seven categories of women with whom marriage was permissible for him:

  1. First, He says: "O Prophet! We have made lawful for you your wives to whom you have paid their dowers" (يَا أَيُّهَا النَّبِيُّ إِنَّا أَحْلَلْنَا لَكَ أَزْوَاجَكَ اللَّاتِي آتَيْتَ أُجُورَهُنَّ).

By the context of the subsequent phrases, these refer to women who had no kinship ties with the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) and married him. Perhaps the mention of paying the dowry (Mahr) is because it was customary to pay the dowry in cash when marrying non-relatives. Furthermore, hastening the payment of the dowry is better, especially when the wife is in need of it. However, in any case, this is not an obligatory requirement, and with mutual agreement, the dowry may remain as a debt upon the husband, either in whole or in part.

  1. "And those [bondwomen] whom your right hand possesses from what Allah has given you as spoils of war" (وَ مَا مَلَكَتْ يَمِينُكَ مِمَّا أَفَاءَ اللَّهُ عَلَيْكَ).

"Afa’a Allah" is derived from the root "fay’" (rhyming with shay’), referring to wealth obtained without hardship. Thus, it is applied to war spoils and also to Anfal (natural resources belonging to the Islamic government that have no specific owner).

Al-Raghib says in Al-Mufradat: "Fay’" means returning and reverting to a good state. If "shadow" is called fay’, it is because it has a returning nature. He then adds that effortless wealth is also called fay’ because, despite all its benefits, it is transient and fleeting like a shadow! It is true that war spoils sometimes involve great trouble, but since they involve less trouble compared to other wealth, and sometimes vast riches are obtained in a single raid, the term "fay’" is applied to them.

Regarding which of the Prophet’s (صلى الله عليه وآله) wives this ruling applied to, some commentators have said: One of the Prophet’s (صلى الله عليه وآله) wives named "Maria the Copt" was from the spoils, and two other wives named "Safiyyah" and "Juwayriyyah" were from the Anfal, whom the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) emancipated from slavery and took as his wives. This was itself part of Islam's general program for the gradual liberation of slaves and the restoration of their human dignity.

  1. "And the daughters of your paternal uncle and the daughters of your paternal aunts and the daughters of your maternal uncle and the daughters of your maternal aunts who emigrated with you—these too are lawful for you" (وَ بَنَاتِ عَمِّكَ وَ بَنَاتِ عَمَّاتِكَ وَ بَنَاتِ خَالِكَ وَ بَنَاتِ خَالَاتِكَ اللَّاتِي هَاجَرْنَ مَعَكَ).

Thus, among all relatives, only marriage with paternal cousins (male and female side) and maternal cousins (male and female side) was permitted and legitimate, with the condition that they had emigrated with the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله).

The restriction to these four groups is clear, but the condition of emigration is because, in those days, emigration was a proof of faith, and non-emigration was a sign of disbelief; or it is because emigration granted them greater merit, and the aim of the verse is to describe women of character and virtue who are suitable to be the Prophet’s (صلى الله عليه وآله) spouses.

As to whether these four categories, mentioned as a general rule in the verse, had an actual instance among the Prophet’s (صلى الله عليه وآله) wives, the only case that can be mentioned is the Prophet’s (صلى الله عليه وآله) marriage to "Zaynab bint Jahsh," whose eventful story was mentioned earlier in this Surah, for Zaynab was the daughter of the Prophet’s (صلى الله عليه وآله) paternal aunt, and Jahsh was the husband of his aunt. [1]

  1. "And a believing woman if she gives herself to the Prophet (and does not stipulate any dowry for herself), if the Prophet wishes to marry her" (وَ امْرَأَةً مُؤْمِنَةً إِنْ وَهَبَتْ نَفْسَهَا لِلنَّبِيِّ إِنْ أَرَادَ النَّبِيُّ أَنْ يَسْتَنْكِحَهَا).

"But such a marriage is permitted only for you, and not for the rest of the believers" (خَالِصَةً لَكَ مِنْ دُونِ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ).

"We certainly know what We have ordained for them concerning their wives and those whom their right hands possess, and what their interests required" (قَدْ عَلِمْنَا مَا فَرَضْنَا عَلَيْهِمْ فِي أَزْوَاجِهِمْ وَ مَا مَلَكَتْ أَيْمَانُهُمْ).

Therefore, if We have placed restrictions on them in some matters related to marriage, it was based on interests that governed their lives and yours, and none of these rulings and regulations are without reason.

He then adds: "This is so that there may be no difficulty or constraint upon you (in fulfilling your mission)" and that you may be able to carry out your responsibilities in performing this duty (لِكَيْلَا يَكُونَ عَلَيْكَ حَرَجٌ).

"And Allah is Ever-Forgiving, Most Merciful" (وَ كَانَ اللَّهُ غَفُوراً رَحِيماً).

Regarding the last group (women without dowry), the following points should be noted:

  1. Undoubtedly, the permission to "take a wife without a dowry" was exclusive to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله), and the verse is explicit on this matter. For this reason, it is among the established facts of Islamic jurisprudence. Therefore, no one else has the right to marry a woman without a dowry (whether small or large). Even if the dowry is not mentioned during the marriage contract ceremony and there is no determining context, the "Mahr al-Mithl" (customary dowry) must be paid. "Mahr al-Mithl" refers to the dowry that women with similar conditions and characteristics usually set for themselves.

  2. There is discussion among commentators as to whether this general ruling found an actual instance in the case of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله). Some, such as Ibn Abbas and other commentators, believe that the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) did not marry any woman in this manner. Therefore, the above ruling was only a general permission for the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) that was never actually utilized.

However, others have mentioned the names of three or four women among the Prophet’s (صلى الله عليه وآله) wives who entered into marriage with him without a dowry: "Maymunah" daughter of al-Harith, "Zaynab" daughter of Khuzaymah (who was from the Ansar), a woman from Banu Asad named "Umm Sharik" daughter of Jabir, and "Khawlah" daughter of Hakim.

For instance, it is mentioned in some narrations that when "Khawlah" gave herself to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله), Aisha raised an objection and said: "What is the matter with women that they offer themselves without a dowry?!" At this moment, the above verse was revealed. Then Aisha turned to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) and said: "I see that Allah hastens to fulfill your desire" (this was a form of sarcasm toward the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله)). But the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) replied: "And you too, if you obey Allah, He will hasten to fulfill your purpose!" [1]

Undoubtedly, such women only sought to gain the spiritual honor that would come to them through marriage to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله); hence, they were ready to be his wives without any dowry. However, as we said, the existence of such an instance for the above ruling is not historically certain. What is certain is that Allah had given such permission to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) for a philosophy that will be mentioned later.

  1. It is clearly understood from this verse that performing the marriage contract with the word "Hibah" (gift) was exclusive to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله), and no other individual can perform the marriage contract with such a word. However, if the contract is performed with the words "marriage" (izdiwaj) or "nikah," it is permissible even if no mention is made of the dowry, because as we said, in the absence of mentioning the dowry, the "Mahr al-Mithl" must be paid (in reality, it is as if the Mahr al-Mithl was explicitly stated).

A Glimpse into the Philosophy of the Prophet’s (صلى الله عليه وآله) Polygamy

The final phrase in the above verse actually points to the philosophy of these rulings specific to the noble Prophet of Islam. This phrase says: The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) has conditions that others do not have, and this very difference has caused a difference in the rulings.

In clearer terms, it says: The goal was to lift some of the restrictions and difficulties from the Prophet’s (صلى الله عليه وآله) shoulders through these rulings.

This is a subtle expression showing that the Prophet’s (صلى الله عليه وآله) marriage to multiple and various women was to solve a series of social and political problems in his life.

For we know that when the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) raised the call of Islam, he was all alone, and for a long time, only a limited and small number of people believed in him. He rose against all the superstitious beliefs of his era and environment and declared war on them all. It was natural that all the tribes and clans of that environment would mobilize against him.

He had to use all means to break the unholy alliance of the enemies, one of which was creating kinship ties through marriage with various tribes. This was because the strongest bond among the Arabs of the Age of Ignorance was considered the kinship bond; they always considered the son-in-law of the tribe as one of their own, deemed it necessary to defend him, and considered leaving him alone a sin.

We have many clues showing that the Prophet’s (صلى الله عليه وآله) marriages, at least in many cases, had a political aspect. Some of his marriages, like the one with "Zaynab," were to break an Ignorant custom, the details of which we explained under verse 37 of this Surah. Others were to reduce enmity, or to establish friendship and attract the affection of individuals or stubborn and fanatical tribes.

It is clear that someone who, at the age of 25—the prime of his youth—marries a forty-year-old widow and remains content with only this one widow until the age of 53, thus passing his youth and reaching old age, and then engages in multiple marriages, must have a reason and philosophy.
