By no account can this be linked to sexual motives. For although the issue of multiple marriages was very simple and common among the Arabs of that day—sometimes even the first wife would go to propose for the second wife, and they recognized no limit to taking wives—multiple marriages for the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) in his youth faced no social barrier, nor heavy financial conditions, nor was it considered the slightest defect.

Interestingly, it is recorded in histories that the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) married only one "virgin" woman, and that was Aisha; all his other wives were widows who naturally could not arouse much desire from a sexual perspective. [1] We even read in some histories that the Prophet married several women where only the contract ceremony was performed, and he never had intercourse with them; in some cases, he even contented himself with only proposing to some women of the tribes. [2] They were happy and boasted just by the fact that a woman from their tribe was called the wife of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) and that this honor had been achieved for them. In this way, their social bond and connection with the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) became stronger, and they became more determined in defending him.

On the other hand, although the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) was certainly not a sterile man, he left behind few children. If these marriages had been performed for the sexual attraction of these women, many children should have been left behind by him.

It is also noteworthy that some of these women, like Aisha, were very young when they became the Prophet’s (صلى الله عليه وآله) wife, and years passed before she could be a real wife to him. This shows that marriage with such a girl had other motives, and the main goal was those we mentioned above.

Although the enemies of Islam have tried to use the Prophet’s (صلى الله عليه وآله) multiple marriages as a pretext for the most severe biased attacks and to create false legends from them, the Prophet’s high age at the time of these multiple marriages on one hand, the specific age and tribal conditions of these women on the other, and various clues—some of which we mentioned above—on the third hand, bring the truth to light and expose the conspiracies of the biased.

[^1]: Regarding why "amm" (paternal uncle) is singular and "ammat" (paternal aunts) is plural, and similarly "khal" (maternal uncle) is singular and "khalat" (maternal aunts) is plural, some commentators have mentioned reasons which "Fadil Miqdad" has cited in Kanz al-Irfan. However, the best explanation is that "amm" and "khal" are usually used as generic nouns in the Arabic language, whereas "ammah" and "khalah" are not. This is the convention of the linguists, which Ibn al-Arabi also reported (Kanz al-Irfan, Vol. 2, p. 241), and Alusi in Ruh al-Ma'ani also preferred this view over all others.

[^1]: Majma' al-Bayan under the verse discussed. In the commentary of Qurtubi, the phrase "By Allah, I see that your Lord only hastens to fulfill your desire" is also mentioned, and Alusi in Ruh al-Ma'ani also cited it under the verse. The offensiveness of this expression and the inappropriate concept hidden within it is hidden from no one, but the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) passes over it in a beautiful and magnanimous manner.

[^1] and [^2]: Bihar al-Anwar, Vol. 22, pp. 191 and 192.

Ayah

۞ تُرْجِى مَن تَشَآءُ مِنْهُنَّ وَتُـْٔوِىٓ إِلَيْكَ مَن تَشَآءُ ۖ وَمَنِ ٱبْتَغَيْتَ مِمَّنْ عَزَلْتَ فَلَا جُنَاحَ عَلَيْكَ ۚ ذَٰلِكَ أَدْنَىٰٓ أَن تَقَرَّ أَعْيُنُهُنَّ وَلَا يَحْزَنَّ وَيَرْضَيْنَ بِمَآ ءَاتَيْتَهُنَّ كُلُّهُنَّ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ يَعْلَمُ مَا فِى قُلُوبِكُمْ ۚ وَكَانَ ٱللَّهُ عَلِيمًا حَلِيمًا (٥١)
You may put off whichever of them you wish and consort with whichever of them you wish, and there is no sin upon you [in receiving again] any [of them] whom you may seek [to consort with] from among those you have set aside [earlier]. That makes it likelier that they will be comforted and not feel unhappy, and all of them will be pleased with what you give them. Allah knows what is in your hearts, and Allah is all-knowing, all-forbearing. (51)

Circumstances of Revelation

In the commentary on verses 28 and 29 of this same Surah and the explanation of their circumstances of revelation, we mentioned that a group of the Prophet’s (صلى الله عليه وآله) wives—according to what commentators have narrated—asked the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) to increase their allowance and living expenses (because their eyes had fallen upon the spoils of war, and they imagined they should receive a large share of it). The aforementioned verses were revealed and explicitly warned them that if they desired the world and its adornments, they should separate from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) forever; but if they desired Allah, His Messenger, and the Day of Recompense, they must be content with his simple life.

Furthermore, regarding the distribution of the Prophet’s (صلى الله عليه وآله) time among them, they also had rivalries that placed the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) in a difficult position despite his many preoccupations and important duties. Although the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) made the necessary efforts to observe justice among them, their disputes continued. The above verse was revealed and left the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) completely free in the distribution of his time among them, and simultaneously announced to them that this is a Divine decree so that no worry or misunderstanding would arise for them. [1]

Commentary

Resolving Another Difficulty in the Prophet’s (صلى الله عليه وآله) Life

A great Divine leader like the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله), especially at a time when he is caught in the furnace of severe events and dangerous conspiracies are being plotted against him from within and without, cannot occupy his mind too much with his personal and private life. He must possess relative peace in his domestic life so that he can devote himself with peace of mind to solving the multitude of problems surrounding him from every side.

The turmoil of personal life and being preoccupied with family matters during these critical and stormy moments is extremely dangerous.

Although, according to previous discussions and the evidence we provided in the explanation of the preceding verse, the Prophet’s (صلى الله عليه وآله) multiple marriages mostly had political, social, and emotional dimensions—and were in fact part of the program for fulfilling his Divine mission—nevertheless, at times, differences among the wives and their common feminine rivalries would stir a storm within the Prophet’s (صلى الله عليه وآله) house and occupy his mind.

It is here that Allah granted another privilege to His Prophet and ended these incidents and conflicts forever, setting the Prophet’s (صلى الله عليه وآله) mind at ease and making him free from this concern.

As we read in the verse under discussion, He said: "You may defer [the turn of] whom you will of them and take to yourself whom you will" (تُرْجِي مَنْ تَشاءُ مِنْهُنَّ وَ تُؤْوِي إِلَيْكَ مَنْ تَشاءُ).

"Turji" is from the root "Arja’" meaning to defer or postpone, and "Tu’wi" is from the root "Iwa’" meaning to give someone a place near oneself.

We know that one of the laws of Islam regarding multiple wives is that the husband must divide his time among them justly. If he stays one night with one, he must stay the next night with another; there is no difference among the wives in this regard, and this subject is referred to in books of Islamic jurisprudence as "Haqq al-Qasm" (the right of sharing time).

One of the characteristics of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) was that, due to the special conditions of his stormy and critical life—especially during the time he was in Medina when nearly one war was imposed upon him every month, and during this same time he had multiple wives—the observance of the right of sharing time was waived for him by the decree of the above verse. He could distribute his time in any manner, although even in this state, he observed equality and justice as much as possible, as is explicitly stated in Islamic histories.

However, the existence of this Divine decree gave peace to the Prophet’s (صلى الله عليه وآله) wives and his domestic environment.

Then He adds: "And whomsoever you desire [to invite] of those you had set aside, there is no sin upon you" (وَ مَنِ ابْتَغَيْتَ مِمَّنْ عَزَلْتَ فَلا جُناحَ عَلَيْكَ).

In this way, not only is the choice yours at the beginning, but this choice remains in effect as time continues. In technical terms, this choice is a "continuous choice" rather than an "initial" one. With this broad and extensive decree, any excuse regarding your life program in relation to your wives will be cut off, and you can focus your mind on the great and heavy responsibilities of the Message.

And so that the Prophet’s wives would also know that, besides the honor they gain from being his spouses, by submitting to this special program regarding the distribution of the Prophet’s (صلى الله عليه وآله) time, they are showing a kind of altruism and sacrifice, and no blame or criticism is directed at them because they have submitted to the command of Allah, He adds:

"That is more likely that their eyes will be refreshed and they will not grieve and that they will all be pleased with what you give them" (ذلِكَ أَدْنى‌ أَنْ تَقَرَّ أَعْيُنُهُنَّ وَ لا يَحْزَنَّ وَ يَرْضَيْنَ بِما آتَيْتَهُنَّ كُلُّهُنَ‌).

This is because, firstly, this is a general decree regarding all of them and there is no discrimination involved; and secondly, it is a decree from Allah that has been legislated for important interests. Therefore, they should submit to it with pleasure and desire, and not only should they not be worried, but they should be pleased.

Yet, as we mentioned above, the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) observed equality in the distribution of his time as much as possible, except in cases where special conditions necessitated inequality. This itself was another matter that caused their satisfaction, as they observed that the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله), despite having the choice, strove to establish equality.

At the end of the verse, He concludes the matter with this sentence: "And Allah knows what is in your hearts. And Allah is All-Knowing, Most Forbearing" (وَ اللَّهُ يَعْلَمُ ما فِي قُلُوبِكُمْ وَ كانَ اللَّهُ عَلِيماً حَلِيماً).

Yes, Allah knows which decree you have heart-felt satisfaction and submission toward, and which one you are displeased with.

He knows for which of your wives you have a greater inclination and for which you have less, and how you observe Allah’s command in dealing with these inclinations.

Likewise, He knows those who sit in corners and criticize such Divine decrees regarding the person of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) and object to it in their hearts, and those who accept everything with open arms.

Therefore, the expression "your hearts" (قلوبكم) is a broad term that includes the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله), his wives, and all the believers who approach these decrees with satisfaction and submission, or those who object and deny, even if they do not make it manifest.

Key Point: Was this decree applicable to all the Prophet’s (صلى الله عليه وآله) wives?

In Islamic jurisprudence, under the chapter of the Prophet’s (صلى الله عليه وآله) characteristics, this issue is discussed: was the equal distribution of time among multiple wives—as is obligatory for the general Muslim population—also obligatory for the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله), or did the Prophet have the exceptional decree of "choice" (Takhyir)?

The famous and well-known view among our jurists and a group of Sunni jurists is that he was an exception to this rule, and they consider the above verse as proof, which says:

"You may defer whom you will of them and take to yourself whom you will."

This is because the placement of this sentence after the discussion regarding all the Prophet’s wives necessitates that the plural pronoun (hunna - "them") refers back to all of them, and this is a point accepted by the jurists and many commentators.

However, some consider the aforementioned pronoun to refer specifically to the women who offered themselves to him without a dower (Mahr). This is while, firstly, it is not historically proven whether this decree ever found an external application or not; some believe it was only in one case that a woman married the Prophet in this manner, and in any case, the essence of the matter is not historically certain.

Secondly, this interpretation is contrary to the apparent meaning of the verse and is not consistent with the circumstances of revelation mentioned for the verse. Therefore, it must be accepted that the said decree is a general one.

[1]: Adapted from "Majma' al-Bayan" and other commentaries.

Ayah

لَّا يَحِلُّ لَكَ ٱلنِّسَآءُ مِنۢ بَعْدُ وَلَآ أَن تَبَدَّلَ بِهِنَّ مِنْ أَزْوَٰجٍ وَلَوْ أَعْجَبَكَ حُسْنُهُنَّ إِلَّا مَا مَلَكَتْ يَمِينُكَ ۗ وَكَانَ ٱللَّهُ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ رَّقِيبًا (٥٢)
Beyond that, women are not lawful for you, nor that you should change them for other wives even though their beauty should impress you, except those whom your right hand owns. Allah is watchful over all things. (52)

Commentary

Another Important Ruling Regarding the Prophet's Wives

In this verse, another ruling concerning the wives of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) is stated, declaring: "Beyond this, [other] women are not lawful for you, nor [is it lawful] for you to substitute them for [other] wives, even if their beauty should please you, except those whom your right hand possesses. And Allah is ever, over all things, an Observer" (لَّا يَحِلُّ لَكَ النِّسَاءُ مِن بَعْدُ وَلَا أَن تَبَدَّلَ بِهِنَّ مِنْ أَزْوَاجٍ وَلَوْ أَعْجَبَكَ حُسْنُهُنَّ إِلَّا مَا مَلَكَتْ يَمِينُكَ وَكَانَ اللَّهُ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَيْءٍ رَّقِيبًا).

Commentators and Muslim jurists have extensive discussions regarding the interpretation of this verse, and various narrations have been recorded in Islamic sources in this regard. We will first mention what appears to be the literal meaning of the verse in connection with the preceding and succeeding verses—regardless of the commentators' opinions—and then move on to other matters.

The literal meaning of the expression "beyond this" (مِن بَعْدُ) is that after this, remarriage is forbidden for you. Therefore, "after" either means a temporal "after," meaning "from this time forward, do not choose another wife," or it means after you gave your wives the choice—according to the Divine command in the previous verses—between a simple life in your house or separation, and they willingly preferred to continue their marriage with you; after that, you must not marry any other woman.

Furthermore, you cannot divorce some of them and choose another wife in their place. In other words, neither increase their number nor replace the existing ones.

Key Points

1- The Philosophy of This Ruling

This restriction is not considered a deficiency for the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) himself; rather, it is a ruling with a very calculated philosophy, because:

According to evidence derived from history, the Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله) was under pressure from various individuals and tribes to take wives from them. Each Muslim tribe took pride in having one of their women become a wife of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله). As previously mentioned, some women were even willing to offer themselves as a "gift" (hibah) to him without any dowry and marry him without any conditions.

Of course, marital ties with these tribes and clans were, to some extent, a solution for the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) and his social and political goals. However, it is natural that if this exceeds a certain limit, it becomes a problem in itself. Every clan and tribe would have such an expectation, and if the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) wanted to respond to their expectations and take women—even if only through a marriage contract and not a wedding—it would create numerous troubles.

Therefore, the All-Wise Allah blocked this path with a firm law and forbade him from any further marriage or replacing his current wives.

In the midst of this, there might have been individuals who used the excuse that "your wives are mostly widows, and among them are elderly women who possess no beauty; it is fitting that you marry a beautiful woman." The Quran specifically emphasizes this point as well: that even if there are beautiful women, you will not have the right to marry them.

Additionally, gratitude required that after his wives remained loyal to him and preferred the simple spiritual life of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) over everything else, Allah should issue such a command to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) to preserve their status.

As for the case of bondwomen (concubines) being permitted, it is because the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله) difficulty and burden came from the side of free women; thus, there was no necessity for this ruling to be restricted in that regard, although history testifies that the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) did not make use of this exception either.

This is what appears from the literal meaning of the verse.

2- Conflicting Narrations

In several narrations—some of which are weak in their chain of transmission and some of which are noteworthy—the phrase "Beyond this, [other] women are not lawful for you" has been interpreted as referring to the women whose prohibition was stated in verses 23 and 24 of Surah An-Nisa (mothers, daughters, sisters, paternal aunts, maternal aunts, etc.).

In the conclusion of some of these reports, it is explicitly stated: "How is it possible that women are lawful for others but forbidden for the Prophet?! No woman was forbidden to him except those forbidden to everyone." [^1] Of course, it seems very unlikely that this verse refers to the verses in Surah An-Nisa, but the problem is that some of these narrations explicitly state that "beyond this" means after the prohibitions of Surah An-Nisa.

Therefore, it is better to set aside the interpretation of these narrations, which are "isolated reports" (akhbar al-ahad), and so to speak, "entrust their knowledge to their people, namely the Infallibles (عليه السلام)," because they do not harmonize with the literal meaning of the verse, and we are bound by the literal meaning, while the aforementioned reports are speculative (zanni).

Another point is that a large group believes that the verse under discussion prohibited any further marriage for the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله), but this ruling was later abrogated, and the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) was again given permission to marry, although he did not make use of it. They even consider the verse "Indeed, We have made lawful for you your wives to whom you have given their dowries..." (إِنَّا أَحْلَلْنَا لَكَ أَزْوَاجَكَ اللَّاتِي آتَيْتَ أُجُورَهُنَّ), which was revealed before the verse under discussion, to be its abrogator (nasikh), believing that although it is written before it in the Quran, its revelation occurred after it! Even "Fazil Miqdad" in "Kanz al-Irfan" mentions that the famous fatwa among the companions is this. [^2] This belief, while in clear contradiction with the above narrations, is also inconsistent with the literal appearance of the verses, because the verses show that "Indeed, We have made lawful for you your wives..." was revealed before the verse under discussion, and the issue of abrogation requires definitive proof.

In any case, we have nothing more reliable or clearer here than the literal meaning of the verse itself.

[^1]: Tafsir "Nur al-Thaqalayn," Vol. 4, pp. 294 and 295. [^2]: "Kanz al-Irfan," Vol. 2, p. 244.

According to it, any new marriage or substitution of wives was prohibited for the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) after the revelation of the above verse, and this ruling served important interests which we mentioned above.

3- Can One Look at a Future Spouse Before Marriage?

A group of commentators has taken the phrase "even if their beauty should please you" (وَلَوْ أَعْجَبَكَ حُسْنُهُنَّ) as evidence for a well-known ruling also mentioned in Islamic narrations: that someone who wants to marry a woman can look at her beforehand—a look that clarifies her appearance and physique for him.

The philosophy of this ruling is that a person should choose his spouse with full insight, preventing future regret and remorse that could jeopardize the marriage bond. As stated in a hadith from the Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله) to one of his companions who wanted to marry a woman:

"Look at her, for it is more likely to sustain [the affection] between you." [^1]

In another hadith from Imam al-Sadiq (عليه السلام), we read in response to the question: "Can a man look closely at a woman when he intends to marry her, looking at her face and the back of her head?" He said:

"Yes, there is no harm if a man looks at a woman when he intends to marry her; he may look at her back and her face." [^2]

Of course, hadiths in this regard are numerous, but some of them explicitly state:

[^1]: Tafsir al-Qurtubi, Vol. 8, p. 5303. [^2]: Wasa'il al-Shi'ah, Vol. 14, Chapters on the Preliminaries of Marriage, Chapter 36, Hadith 3.

that at this time, the look must not be out of lust or for the purpose of seeking pleasure.

It is also clear that this ruling is specific to cases where a person truly wants to investigate a woman so that if the conditions are met, he may marry her. However, someone who has not yet decided on marriage cannot look at women merely based on the possibility of marriage or as a general searcher.

Of course, some have suggested the possibility that the above verse refers to looks that fall upon a woman unintentionally and by chance; in this case, the verse would not indicate the aforementioned ruling, and the basis for this ruling would only be the narrations. However, the phrase "even if their beauty should please you" is not consistent with accidental and fleeting glances. Therefore, its indication of the above ruling does not seem far-fetched.

Ayahs

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ لَا تَدْخُلُوا۟ بُيُوتَ ٱلنَّبِىِّ إِلَّآ أَن يُؤْذَنَ لَكُمْ إِلَىٰ طَعَامٍ غَيْرَ نَـٰظِرِينَ إِنَىٰهُ وَلَـٰكِنْ إِذَا دُعِيتُمْ فَٱدْخُلُوا۟ فَإِذَا طَعِمْتُمْ فَٱنتَشِرُوا۟ وَلَا مُسْتَـْٔنِسِينَ لِحَدِيثٍ ۚ إِنَّ ذَٰلِكُمْ كَانَ يُؤْذِى ٱلنَّبِىَّ فَيَسْتَحْىِۦ مِنكُمْ ۖ وَٱللَّهُ لَا يَسْتَحْىِۦ مِنَ ٱلْحَقِّ ۚ وَإِذَا سَأَلْتُمُوهُنَّ مَتَـٰعًا فَسْـَٔلُوهُنَّ مِن وَرَآءِ حِجَابٍ ۚ ذَٰلِكُمْ أَطْهَرُ لِقُلُوبِكُمْ وَقُلُوبِهِنَّ ۚ وَمَا كَانَ لَكُمْ أَن تُؤْذُوا۟ رَسُولَ ٱللَّهِ وَلَآ أَن تَنكِحُوٓا۟ أَزْوَٰجَهُۥ مِنۢ بَعْدِهِۦٓ أَبَدًا ۚ إِنَّ ذَٰلِكُمْ كَانَ عِندَ ٱللَّهِ عَظِيمًا (٥٣)
O you who have faith! Do not enter the Prophet’s houses for a meal until you are granted permission, without hanging around for it to be readied. But enter when you are invited, and disperse when you have taken your meal, without cozying up for chats. Such conduct on your part offends the Prophet, and he is ashamed of [asking] you [to leave]; but Allah is not ashamed of [expressing] the truth. When you ask [his] womenfolk for something, do so from behind a curtain. That is more chaste for your hearts and theirs. You should not offend the Apostle of Allah, nor may you ever marry his wives after him. Indeed that would be a grave [sin] with Allah. (53)
إِن تُبْدُوا۟ شَيْـًٔا أَوْ تُخْفُوهُ فَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ كَانَ بِكُلِّ شَىْءٍ عَلِيمًا (٥٤)
Whether you disclose anything or hide it, Allah indeed knows all things. (54)

Circumstances of Revelation

Regarding the circumstances of the revelation of this verse, commentators have recorded the following:

When the Messenger of Allah ﷺ married "Zaynab bint Jahsh," he provided a relatively elaborate wedding feast (walimah) for the people. (As previously mentioned, these detailed rulings were perhaps intended to ensure that the breaking of the Pre-Islamic ignorance (Jahiliyyah) tradition regarding the prohibition of marrying the "divorced wives of adopted sons" would be carried out with the utmost decisiveness and have a widespread impact on the environment. Furthermore, it aimed to abolish the Jahiliyyah custom where marrying the "widows of freed slaves" was considered a disgrace and a shame).

Anas, who was the special servant of the Prophet ﷺ, says: The Prophet ﷺ commanded me to invite his companions for food. I invited everyone; they came in groups, ate, and left the room, until I said, "O Prophet of Allah! There is no one left whom I have not invited." He said, "Since it is so, clear the spread." They removed the spread and the crowd dispersed, but three individuals remained in the Prophet's ﷺ room, engaged in discussion and conversation.

When their conversation grew long, the Prophet ﷺ stood up, and I stood up with him, hoping they would take notice and go about their business. The Prophet went out until he reached the chamber of Aisha, then returned again. I came in his service and saw they were still sitting. Then the above verse was revealed, conveying the necessary instructions for dealing with such matters [1]. Additionally, some evidence suggests that occasionally neighbors and other people would come to some of the Prophet's ﷺ wives to borrow items, as was customary. Although they committed nothing wrong according to the simple lifestyle of that time, the above verse was revealed to preserve the dignity of the Prophet's ﷺ wives, commanding the believers that whenever they wished to take something from them, they should do so from behind a curtain.

In another narration, it is stated that some opponents of the Prophet ﷺ said: "How is it that the Prophet ﷺ has married some of our widows? By Allah, if he passes away, we shall marry his wives!" The above verse was revealed, completely prohibiting marriage to the Prophet's ﷺ wives after him, thereby putting an end to this conspiracy 2.

Commentary

Once again, the address in this verse is to the believers, and it explains another part of Islamic rulings, particularly those related to the etiquette of social interaction with the Prophet ﷺ and the Household of Prophethood, using short, eloquent, and explicit sentences.

First, it says: "O you who have believed! Do not enter the houses of the Prophet unless permission is given to you for a meal, without waiting for its preparation" (يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا لَا تَدْخُلُوا بُيُوتَ النَّبِيِّ إِلَّا أَنْ يُؤْذَنَ لَكُمْ إِلَىٰ طَعَامٍ غَيْرَ نَاظِرِينَ إِنَاهُ) [1]. In this way, it expresses one of the important etiquettes of social interaction in an environment where such manners were seldom observed. Although the discourse is about the house of the Prophet ﷺ, this ruling is certainly not exclusive to him; in no case should one enter anyone's house without permission (as also mentioned in Surah An-Nur, verse 27). We even read in the accounts of the Prophet ﷺ that when he wished to enter the house of his daughter Fatimah (peace be upon her), he would stand outside the door and ask for permission. One day, Jabir ibn Abdullah was with him; after obtaining permission for himself, he also obtained permission for Jabir! 2 Furthermore, when invited for a meal, they must be punctual and not cause untimely inconvenience for the host.

[1]: Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 8, p. 366, under the discussed verse. 2: Ibid, pp. 366 and 368. [1]: "Inahu" is from the root "ana yani," meaning the arrival of the time for something, and here it means the readiness of the food for consumption. 2: Al-Kafi, Vol. 5, p. 528.

Then, addressing the second ruling, it says: "But when you are invited, then enter; and when you have eaten, disperse" (وَلَٰكِنْ إِذَا دُعِيتُمْ فَادْخُلُوا فَإِذَا طَعِمْتُمْ فَانْتَشِرُوا).

This ruling is, in fact, an emphasis and completion of the previous one: do not enter a house to which you are invited at an inappropriate time, do not ignore the acceptance of the invitation, and do not linger for a long time after eating. It is obvious that violating these matters causes trouble and headache for the host and is inconsistent with moral principles.

In the third ruling, He says: "And do not stay lingering for conversation" (وَلَا مُسْتَأْنِسِينَ لِحَدِيثٍ) in the house of the Prophet (or any other host). Of course, if the host desires such a gathering of intimacy, it is an exception. The discourse concerns cases where the invitation is solely for a meal, not for a social gathering. In such instances, one must leave the gathering after eating, especially if the house is one like the house of the Prophet ﷺ, which is the center for fulfilling the greatest divine missions, and distracting matters should not occupy His time.

Then, He explains the reason for this ruling: "Indeed, that was troubling the Prophet, and he is shy of [dismissing] you. But Allah is not shy of the truth" (إِنَّ ذَٰلِكُمْ كَانَ يُؤْذِي النَّبِيَّ فَيَسْتَحْيِي مِنْكُمْ ۖ وَاللَّهُ لَا يَسْتَحْيِي مِنَ الْحَقِّ).

Of course, the Prophet of Allah also did not hesitate to state the truth in matters that were not personal or private. However, for individuals to state their own rights is not graceful, whereas it is beautiful and appropriate when stated by others. The case of the verse is of this nature; moral principles required that the Prophet ﷺ should not defend Himself, but rather Allah should defend Him.

Then, He states the fourth ruling regarding "Hijab" as follows: "And when you ask them [his wives] for something, ask them from behind a screen" (وَإِذَا سَأَلْتُمُوهُنَّ مَتَاعًا فَاسْأَلُوهُنَّ مِنْ وَرَاءِ حِجَابٍ).

As we mentioned, it was and is common among Arabs and many other people to temporarily borrow some living essentials from a neighbor when needed. The house of the Prophet ﷺ was no exception to this rule, and people would occasionally come to borrow something from the Prophet's ﷺ wives. It is clear that the Prophet's wives being exposed to public view (even with Islamic covering) was not ideal; therefore, it was commanded that people should take items from behind a curtain or behind the door.

A point that must be noted here is that the meaning of "Hijab" in this verse is not the covering of women, but rather an additional ruling specific to the wives of the Prophet ﷺ. This was because, due to the special circumstances of the Prophet's wives, people were obliged to ask for things from behind a curtain, and the wives were not to appear before people even with Islamic covering in such instances. Of course, this ruling was not issued regarding other women; for them, observing the standard covering is sufficient.

The evidence for this is that the word "Hijab," although used in everyday language to mean a woman's covering, does not have such a concept in its literal meaning, nor in the terminology of our jurists (fuqaha).

"Hijab" literally means something that acts as a barrier between two things [1]. For this reason, the membrane stretched between the intestines, heart, and lungs is called the "hijab-e-hajiz" (diaphragm).

In the Holy Quran, this word is used everywhere to mean a curtain or barrier, such as in verse 45 of Surah Al-Isra: "We placed between you and those who do not believe in the Hereafter a concealed partition (hijaban mastura)."

In verse 32 of Surah Sad, we also read: "Until it [the sun] was hidden by the curtain [of the horizon] (bil-hijab)."

And in verse 51 of Surah Ash-Shura, it says: "And it is not for any human being that Allah should speak to him except by revelation or from behind a partition (min wara'i hijab)."

In the words of jurists from the earliest times until now, the word "satr" has usually been used regarding the covering of women, and the same term or similar ones have appeared in Islamic narrations. The use of the word "Hijab" for women's covering is a terminology that has mostly emerged in our era, and if it is found in histories and narrations, it is very rare.

Another proof is found in a narration from Anas ibn Malik, the special servant of the Prophet ﷺ, who says: "I am the most knowledgeable of all regarding this verse of Hijab. When Zaynab married the Prophet ﷺ and was with him in the house, he prepared food and invited the people to a feast. However, a group remained sitting and talking after eating. At that moment, the verse 'O you who have believed! Do not enter the houses of the Prophet...' until '...from behind a screen' was revealed. At that moment, a curtain was dropped and the crowd stood up" [1]. In another narration, Anas says: "He dropped the curtain between me and himself, and when the crowd saw this, they stood up and dispersed" [1].

Therefore, Islam has not commanded Muslim women to be "curtain-dwellers" (secluded), and the expression "those behind the curtain" regarding women and similar expressions do not have an Islamic basis. What is necessary for a Muslim woman is to have the Islamic covering (satr). However, for the wives of the Prophet ﷺ, because of the presence of many enemies and malicious fault-finders, and because they might be exposed to accusations or become a pretext for the black-hearted, this specific instruction was given to them—or rather, given to the people—that when requesting something from them, they should present their request from behind a curtain.

Specifically, the expression "wara'" (behind) is evidence of this meaning.

Thus, after this command, the Quran explains its philosophy: "That is purer for your hearts and their hearts" (ذَٰلِكُمْ أَطْهَرُ لِقُلُوبِكُمْ وَقُلُوبِهِنَّ).

Although this type of reasoning is not incompatible with a recommended (mustahabb) ruling, the appearance of the command in the phrase "fas'aluhunna" (ask them) as an obligation (wujub) is not undermined, as this type of reasoning has occasionally appeared in the context of other mandatory rulings as well.

[1]: Lisan al-Arab, root "hajaba". [1]: Sahih Bukhari, Vol. 6, p. 149. [1]: Sahih Bukhari, Vol. 6, p. 149.

He states the fifth ruling in this way: "And it is not [conceivable] for you to harm the Messenger of Allah" (وَمَا كَانَ لَكُمْ أَنْ تُؤْذُوا رَسُولَ اللَّهِ). Although the act of causing harm is reflected in this very verse—such as going to the Prophet's house at an inappropriate time and staying after eating to cause inconvenience—and it is also mentioned in the narrations of the circumstances of revelation that some blind-hearted individuals had sworn to marry his wives after the Prophet ﷺ, which was another form of harm, the concept of the verse is general and includes any kind of annoyance or harm.

Finally, He states the sixth and last ruling regarding the prohibition of marrying the Prophet's wives after him: "Nor to marry his wives after him, ever. Indeed, that would be in the sight of Allah an enormity" (وَلَا أَنْ تَنْكِحُوا أَزْواجَهُ مِنْ بَعْدِهِ أَبَداً إِنَّ ذلِكُمْ كانَ عِنْدَ اللَّهِ عَظِيماً).

Here a question arises: How could Allah deprive the Prophet's ﷺ wives—some of whom were relatively young at the time of his passing—of the right to choose a spouse?

The answer to this question is clear when considering the philosophy of this prohibition.

Firstly, as we learned from the circumstances of revelation, some had made such a decision as a form of revenge and insult to the sacred station of the Prophet ﷺ, seeking to strike a blow to His dignity.

Secondly, if this matter were permitted, a group might have used the fact that they had taken the Prophet's ﷺ wife after him as a means of exploitation, seeking to gain social status under this pretext, or they might have distorted Islam by claiming special knowledge of the Prophet's ﷺ household and His teachings. Or hypocrites might have spread matters among the people through this channel that were contrary to the Prophet's status (reflect on this).

This danger becomes more tangible when we know that a group had prepared themselves for this; some had spoken of it and some perhaps only held it in their hearts. Among those named by some Sunni commentators here is Talhah [1].

Allah, who is aware of hidden and manifest secrets, issued a decisive ruling to thwart this ugly conspiracy and completely blocked these matters. To strengthen its foundations, He gave the Prophet's ﷺ wives the title "Mothers of the Believers" (Umm al-Mu'minin) so they would know that marrying them is like marrying one's own mother!

Given what has been said, it becomes clear why it was necessary for the Prophet's ﷺ wives to welcome this deprivation. In the course of human life, important issues sometimes arise for which one must show sacrifice and selflessness, and forgo some of one's legitimate rights. Especially since great honors always carry heavy responsibilities. Undoubtedly, the Prophet's ﷺ wives gained a great honor through their marriage to the Prophet ﷺ; possessing such an honor requires such sacrifice as well. For this reason, the Prophet's ﷺ wives lived with great respect among the Islamic Ummah after him, were very satisfied and happy with their situation, and considered that deprivation insignificant compared to these honors.

The second verse under discussion sternly warns the people, saying: "Whether you reveal a thing or conceal it, indeed Allah is ever, of all things, Knowing" (إِنْ تُبْدُوا شَيْئاً أَوْ تُخْفُوهُ فَإِنَّ اللَّهَ كانَ بِكُلِّ شَيْ‌ءٍ عَلِيماً). Do not think that Allah is unaware of your harmful plans toward His Prophet; whether those they uttered or those they decided in their hearts, He knows them all well, and He treats everyone according to their deeds and intentions.

Key Points

In line with the discussion in the above verses regarding the duties of Muslims when invited to the Prophet's feast, it is appropriate to bring a portion of Islamic teachings related to the principle of "hospitality, the right of the guest, and the duties of the host":

1- Hospitality Islam has placed special importance on the issue of hospitality, to the extent that we read in a narration from the noble Prophet of Islam ﷺ: "The guest is a guide to the path of Paradise!" [1]. The importance and respect for the guest are such that in Islam, a guest is regarded as a heavenly gift. The Messenger of Allah ﷺ says: "When Allah intends good for a people, He sends them a gift." They asked, "O Prophet of Allah! What is that gift?" He said, "The guest; he descends with his own provision, and departs with the sins of the household" (and they are forgiven) 2. Interestingly, someone said to the Prophet ﷺ: "May my father and mother be sacrificed for you, my practice is that I perform ablution perfectly, establish prayer, pay Zakat on time, and welcome the guest with open arms for the sake of Allah." He said: "Bravo! Bravo! Bravo! Hell has no way over you! Indeed, Allah has cleared you of stinginess if you are like that!" There is much to say in this regard, but for brevity, we suffice with this amount.

[1]: Tafsir Qurtubi, Vol. 8, p. 5310. [1]: Bihar al-Anwar, Vol. 75, p. 460 (Hadith 14), Chapter 93.

2- Observing Simplicity in Hosting Despite all the importance a guest holds, elaborate and ostentatious hosting is not only not considered good in Islam but is officially discouraged. Islam recommends that hosting be simple and has drawn an interesting, fair line between the guest and the host: "The host should not withhold what he has, and the guest should not expect more than that!" Imam Sadiq (peace be upon him) says: "The believer does not feel shy before his brother, and I do not know which of the two is more amazing: the one who, upon entering his brother's house, forces him into elaborate trouble? Or the one who personally seeks elaborate trouble for his brother?" [1]. Salman al-Farsi narrates from the noble Prophet of Islam ﷺ that he said: "That we should not go to elaborate trouble for the guest with what we do not have, and that we should offer him what is present with us" 2.

3- The Right of the Guest We said that from the Islamic perspective, a guest is a heavenly gift and a messenger of Allah, and one must cherish him like one's own soul and show him the utmost respect. To the extent that the Commander of the Faithful, Ali (peace be upon him), narrates from the Prophet ﷺ: "It is among the rights of the guest that you walk with him and escort him from your premises to the open" [1]. And as long as it does not lead to elaborate trouble, one must provide the means for his comfort and ease, to the point that we have in a narration that one of the rights of the guest is even to provide a toothpick for him: "The Messenger of Allah ﷺ said: Indeed, it is among the rights of the guest that his host prepares the toothpick for him!" 2.

Sometimes guests are shy and bashful individuals, and for this reason, it has been commanded not to ask them about eating, but rather to prepare the food spread; if they wish, they will eat. As Imam Sadiq (peace be upon him) says: "Do not say to your brother when he enters upon you, 'Have you eaten anything today?' but rather bring near to him what you have, for the generous, the truly generous, is he who gives what he has present" [3]. Among the duties of the host before God is not to belittle the food he has prepared, for God's blessing, whatever it may be, is dear and respected. However, it is common among the affluent and those who seek elaborate trouble that no matter how colorful they make the spread, they say: "It is but a trivial thing and not worthy of your status!" Just as the guest also has the duty not to belittle it.

In a narration from Imam Sadiq (peace be upon him), we read: "Perished is the man who belittles for his brother what he has present, and perished is the man who belittles from his brother what is offered to him!" [4].

[1]: Bihar al-Anwar, Vol. 75, p. 453. 2: Mahajjat al-Bayda, Vol. 3, p. 29 (Chapter 3). [1]: Bihar al-Anwar, Vol. 75, p. 451. 2 & [3]: Bihar al-Anwar, Vol. 75, p. 455. [4]: Mahajjat al-Bayda, Vol. 3, p. 30.

Islam is so meticulous in honoring the guest that it says: "When the guest enters, help him [with his belongings], but when he is leaving the house, do not help him, lest it be thought that you are eager for him to leave!" [1].

4- Duties of the Guest Responsibilities are always mutual; just as the host has important duties toward the guest, the guest also has significant duties in the opposite direction. In addition to what was mentioned in the above hadiths, the guest is obliged to do what the host suggests regarding his home; for example, to accept wherever he suggests for sitting. Imam Sadiq (peace be upon him) says: "When one of you enters the dwelling of his brother, let him sit where the master of the house commands, for the master of the house knows the private areas of his home better than the one entering it" 2.

In short, the issue of hospitality, the etiquette of hosting, and the duties and characteristics of each have occupied an extensive discussion in Islamic social etiquette. Those who want more explanation in this regard should refer to Bihar al-Anwar, chapters 88 to 94 of the chapters of Kitab al-Ishrah from Volume 17, and the book Mahajjat al-Bayda, Volume 3, Chapter 4, "The Virtue of Hospitality."

However, with great regret, in our era, which is the era of the dominance of materialism over the world, this ancient human tradition has become so limited that in some Western societies, it has almost been abolished. We have heard that when some of them come to Islamic countries and see the extent of hosting and hospitality—which is still maintained warmly and full of emotion in the authentic families of this land—they are amazed: how is it possible for individuals to put the best available household items and their most valuable food at the service of guests with whom they sometimes have little connection, and perhaps only met during a short journey?!

But attention to the Islamic hadiths, a portion of which was mentioned above, clarifies the reason for this altruism and sacrifice and specifies the spiritual calculations in this regard—calculations that are an incomprehensible culture to materialists.

[1]: Bihar al-Anwar, Vol. 75, p. 455 (Hadith 27). 2: Bihar al-Anwar, Vol. 75, p. 451.

Ayah

لَّا جُنَاحَ عَلَيْهِنَّ فِىٓ ءَابَآئِهِنَّ وَلَآ أَبْنَآئِهِنَّ وَلَآ إِخْوَٰنِهِنَّ وَلَآ أَبْنَآءِ إِخْوَٰنِهِنَّ وَلَآ أَبْنَآءِ أَخَوَٰتِهِنَّ وَلَا نِسَآئِهِنَّ وَلَا مَا مَلَكَتْ أَيْمَـٰنُهُنَّ ۗ وَٱتَّقِينَ ٱللَّهَ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ كَانَ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ شَهِيدًا (٥٥)
There is no sin on them [in socializing freely] with their fathers, or their sons, or their brothers, or their brothers’ sons, or the sons of their sisters, or their own womenfolk, or what their right hands own. Be wary of Allah. Indeed Allah is witness to all things. (55)

Occasion of Revelation

Some commentators have narrated that after the revelation of the verse regarding the Hijab (the previous verse), the fathers, children, and relatives of the Prophet’s صلى الله عليه وآله wives asked him: "O Messenger of Allah! Should we also speak to them from behind a curtain?" Then the above verse was revealed, answering them that this ruling does not include them.

Commentary

Instances Exempted from the Law of Hijab

Since the previous verse contained an absolute ruling regarding the Hijab for the wives of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, and the absolute nature of this ruling might have created the misconception that even their Mahram (unmarriageable) relatives were obliged to observe it and should only communicate with them from behind a curtain, the above verse was revealed to explain the ruling on this matter.

It says: "There is no sin upon the wives of the Prophet for [not being veiled before] their fathers, their sons, their brothers, their brothers' sons, their sisters' sons, their own women, or those whom their right hands possess" (لا جُناحَ عَلَيْهِنَّ فِي آبائِهِنَّ وَ لا- أَبْنائِهِنَّ وَ لا إِخْوانِهِنَّ وَ لا أَبْناءِ إِخْوانِهِنَّ وَ لا أَبْناءِ أَخَواتِهِنَّ وَ لا نِسائِهِنَّ وَ لا ما مَلَكَتْ أَيْمانُهُنَ‌).

In other words, their Mahrams, who are limited to these six groups, are exempted. If it is asked why other individuals who are also among the Mahrams—such as paternal and maternal uncles—are not mentioned in these six groups, the answer is:

Since the Quran observes eloquence and rhetoric in its highest form, and one of the principles of eloquence is that no redundant word should exist in speech, it refrained from mentioning "paternal uncles" and "maternal uncles" here. This is because by mentioning the brothers' sons and sisters' sons, the Mahram status of paternal and maternal uncles becomes clear. This is because Mahram status is always mutual; just as a brother's son is a Mahram to a person, that person is also a Mahram to their brother's son (and we know such a woman is considered a paternal aunt). Likewise, just as a sister's son is a Mahram to her, she is also a Mahram to her sister's son (and we know such a woman is considered a maternal aunt).

When a paternal aunt and a maternal aunt are Mahrams to their brother's son and sister's son, then a paternal uncle and a maternal uncle will also be Mahrams to their brother's daughter and sister's daughter (as there is no difference between a "paternal uncle" and a "paternal aunt," or a "maternal uncle" and a "maternal aunt" in this regard). This is one of the subtleties of the Quran (reflect on this).

Another question arises here: "father-in-law" and "step-son" are also considered among a woman's Mahrams; why is there no mention of them here? While in verse 31 of Surah Al-Nur, they are mentioned as Mahrams.

The answer to this question is also clear: because this verse specifically discusses the ruling for the wives of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, and we know that at the time these verses were revealed, neither the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله father was alive, nor his grandfathers, nor did He have any living sons (again, reflect on this). [^1] The omission of "foster" (radha'i) brothers and sisters and the like is because they are legally considered the same as biological brothers, sisters, and other Mahrams, and do not require a separate mention.

At the end of the verse, the tone changes from "third person" to "direct address," speaking to the wives of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله: "And fear Allah, for indeed Allah is a Witness over all things," and nothing is hidden or concealed from Him (وَ اتَّقِينَ اللَّهَ إِنَّ اللَّهَ كانَ عَلى‌ كُلِّ شَيْ‌ءٍ شَهِيداً).

This is because the Hijab, the curtain, and the like are all nothing more than means for protection against sin; the real root is Taqwa (piety/God-consciousness). If it does not exist, even these means will be of no benefit.

It also seems necessary to mention here that "their women" (نسائهن) refers to fellow-believing and Muslim women. As we stated in the commentary of Surah Al-Nur, it is not appropriate for Muslim women to be without cover before non-Muslim women, as they might describe what they see to their husbands. [^1] As for the phrase "or those whom their right hands possess" (أَوْ ما مَلَكَتْ أَيْمانُهُنَ‌), as we also mentioned in the commentary of Surah Al-Nur, it has a broad meaning that includes both female and male servants. However, according to some Islamic narrations, it is specific to female servants. Therefore, mentioning them after the general mention of women might be because it includes non-Muslim female servants as well (reflect on this).

[^1]: Historians have mentioned three sons for the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله: "Qasim" and "Abdullah" (nicknamed Tayyib and Tahir) who were from Khadija and both passed away in childhood in Mecca; and "Ibrahim" who was born in the eighth year of the Hijra and lived no more than 16 or 18 months. In any case, none of them existed at the time of the revelation of Surah Al-Ahzab, and Ibrahim was born after this event and passed away in childhood (refer to Usd al-Ghabah and other books of history and biography).

[^1]: Refer to Volume 14 of Tafsir Nemouneh, page 451.

Ayahs

إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ وَمَلَـٰٓئِكَتَهُۥ يُصَلُّونَ عَلَى ٱلنَّبِىِّ ۚ يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ صَلُّوا۟ عَلَيْهِ وَسَلِّمُوا۟ تَسْلِيمًا (٥٦)
Indeed Allah and His angels bless the Prophet; O you who have faith! Invoke blessings on him and invoke Peace upon him in a worthy manner. (56)
إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ يُؤْذُونَ ٱللَّهَ وَرَسُولَهُۥ لَعَنَهُمُ ٱللَّهُ فِى ٱلدُّنْيَا وَٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ وَأَعَدَّ لَهُمْ عَذَابًا مُّهِينًا (٥٧)
Indeed those who offend Allah and His Apostle are cursed by Allah in the world and the Hereafter, and He has prepared a humiliating punishment for them. (57)
وَٱلَّذِينَ يُؤْذُونَ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ وَٱلْمُؤْمِنَـٰتِ بِغَيْرِ مَا ٱكْتَسَبُوا۟ فَقَدِ ٱحْتَمَلُوا۟ بُهْتَـٰنًا وَإِثْمًا مُّبِينًا (٥٨)
Those who offend faithful men and women undeservedly, certainly bear the guilt of slander and flagrant sin. (58)

Commentary

[^1]

Salutations and Blessings upon the Prophet (ص)

Following the discussions in the previous verses regarding maintaining the sanctity of the Prophet (ص) and the prohibition of annoying him, the verses under discussion first speak of the special affection of Allah

[^1]: It is interesting to note that the commencement of [writing] these verses occurred on the night of the Prophet's (ص) birth in the month of Rabi' al-Awwal, in the year 1404 AH.

and the angels toward the Prophet (ص), and then issue a command to the believers in this regard. Subsequently, they present the painful and ominous consequences for those who annoy the Prophet (ص), and in the final stage, they recount the great sin of those who annoy the believers through slander.

First, He says: "Indeed, Allah and His angels send blessings upon the Prophet" (إِنَّ اللَّهَ وَ مَلائِكَتَهُ يُصَلُّونَ عَلَى النَّبِيِ‌).

The status of the Prophet (ص) is so exalted that the Creator of the universe and all the angels—to whom the management of this world is entrusted by Divine command—send blessings upon him. Now that this is the case, you too should harmonize yourselves with this message of the universe: "O you who have believed, ask [Allah to confer] blessing upon him and ask [Allah to grant him] peace and submit [to him] in [complete] submission" (يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا صَلُّوا عَلَيْهِ وَ سَلِّمُوا تَسْلِيماً).

He is a precious gem of the world of creation, and if by Divine grace he has been placed within your reach, do not hold him cheap; do not forget his worth and status in the presence of the Lord and before the angels of all the heavens. He is a human being who rose from among you, but not an ordinary human; he is one in whose existence an entire world is summarized.

Here, several points must be noted:

1- "Salat" and "Salawat" (its plural), when attributed to Allah, mean the sending of mercy; and when attributed to the angels and the believers, they mean the seeking of mercy. [^1]

2- The expression "Yusallun" (يصلون) in the present continuous tense indicates continuity; meaning that Allah and the angels constantly and perpetually send mercy and blessings upon him—a continuous and eternal mercy and blessing.

[^1]: Raghib in "Al-Mufradat" has expressed this same meaning in different words.

3- What is the difference between "Sallu" (صلوا) and "Sallimu" (سلموا)? Commentators have various discussions. What seems most appropriate according to the linguistic roots of these two words and the apparent context of the Quranic verse is that: "Sallu" is a command to seek mercy and send blessings upon the Prophet, while "Sallimu" either means submission to the commands of the Noble Prophet of Islam, as mentioned in verse 65 of Surah An-Nisa: ثُمَّ لا يَجِدُوا فِي أَنْفُسِهِمْ حَرَجاً مِمَّا قَضَيْتَ وَ يُسَلِّمُوا تَسْلِيماً ("...and then find within themselves no discomfort from what you have judged and submit in [full, willing] submission").

As we read in a narration from Imam al-Sadiq (عليه السلام): Abu Basir asked him, "We have understood the meaning of 'Salat' upon the Prophet (ص), but what is the meaning of 'Taslim' (submission) to him?" The Imam replied:

"It is submission to him in all matters." [^1]

Or, it means sending greetings (Salam) to the Prophet (ص) in the form of "As-Salamu 'alayka ya Rasul Allah" and the like, the content of which is a request for the Prophet's (ص) well-being from the presence of Allah.

Abu Hamza al-Thumali narrates from one of the companions of the Prophet (ص) named Ka'b: "When the above verse was revealed, we said: 'We know how to greet you (Salam), but how is the Salat upon you?' He replied: 'Say:

O Allah, bless Muhammad and the family of Muhammad as You blessed Ibrahim; indeed, You are Praiseworthy and Glorious. And grant benediction upon Muhammad and the family of Muhammad as You granted benediction upon Ibrahim and the family of Ibrahim; indeed, You are Praiseworthy and Glorious.'"

From this hadith, both the manner of Salat (blessing) upon the Prophet and the meaning of Salam become clear. [^1] Although these two meanings for Salam seem quite different, with careful consideration, they can be directed toward a single point: verbal and practical submission to the Prophet (ص). For one who sends Salam to him and requests his well-being from Allah, loves him and recognizes him as a Prophet whose obedience is mandatory.

[^1]: Majma' al-Bayan, under the verses discussed—the second hadith is narrated in both Shi'a and Sunni books through numerous chains and with similar wordings.

4- It is noteworthy that regarding the quality of Salawat upon the Prophet (ص), it is explicitly stated in countless narrations from both Sunni and Ahl al-Bayt sources that one must add "the family of Muhammad" (Al-i Muhammad) when sending Salawat upon "Muhammad."

In "Al-Durr al-Manthur," it is narrated from Sahih Bukhari, Muslim, Abu Dawood, Tirmidhi, Nasa'i, Ibn Majah, Ibn Mardawayh, and other narrators from Ka'b ibn 'Ujrah that "A man said to the Prophet (ص): 'We know how the Salam upon you is, but how should the Salat upon you be?' The Prophet replied: 'Say:

O Allah, bless Muhammad and the family of Muhammad as You blessed Ibrahim; indeed, You are Praiseworthy and Glorious. O Allah, grant benediction upon Muhammad and the family of Muhammad as You granted benediction upon Ibrahim and the family of Ibrahim; indeed, You are Praiseworthy and Glorious.'"

The aforementioned author (of Al-Durr al-Manthur), in addition to the above hadith, has narrated eighteen other hadiths, all of which explicitly state that "the family of Muhammad" must be mentioned when sending Salawat.

He has narrated these hadiths from the well-known and famous books of the Ahl al-Sunnah from a group of companions, including Ibn Abbas, Talhah, Abu Sa'id al-Khudri, Abu Hurairah, Abu Mas'ud al-Ansari, Buraydah, Ibn Mas'ud, Ka'b ibn 'Ujrah, and the Commander of the Faithful, Ali (عليه السلام). [^1] In Sahih Bukhari, which is the most famous source of hadith for our Sunni brothers, several narrations are cited in this regard; those interested in further detail may refer to the text of the book itself. [^2] In Sahih Muslim, two narrations are also mentioned in this regard. [^3] It is strange that in this very book, despite "Muhammad and the family of Muhammad" being mentioned together several times in these two hadiths, the title chosen for the chapter is "Chapter of Salat upon the Prophet (ص)" (without mentioning the Family)!! It is also noteworthy that in some Sunni narrations and many Shi'a narrations, even the word "and" (على) does not separate "Muhammad" and "the family of Muhammad"; rather, the quality of Salat is in this form: "O Allah, bless Muhammad and the family of Muhammad" (اللهم صل على محمد و آل محمد).

We conclude this discussion with another hadith from the Noble Prophet of Islam (ص):

Ibn Hajar narrates in "Al-Sawa'iq" that the Prophet (ص) said:

"Do not send a truncated (incomplete) Salat upon me!" They asked: "What is a truncated Salat?" He replied: "That you say: 'O Allah, bless Muhammad' and then stop. Rather, say: 'O Allah, bless Muhammad and the family of Muhammad.'" [^1] It is because of these narrations that a group of great Sunni jurists consider adding "the family of Muhammad" to the name of the Prophet in the Tashahhud of the prayer to be obligatory. [^2]

[^1]: Tafsir Al-Durr al-Manthur under the verse, as quoted in Tafsir Al-Mizan, Vol. 16, pp. 365-366. [^2]: Sahih Bukhari, Vol. 6, p. 151. [^3]: Sahih Muslim, Vol. 1, p. 305, Chapter of Salat upon the Prophet (ص).

5- Is sending Salawat upon the Prophet (ص) obligatory or not? And if it is obligatory, where is it so? This is a question that jurists answer:

All jurists of the Ahl al-Bayt consider it obligatory in the first and second Tashahhud of the prayer, and recommended (Mustahabb) otherwise. In addition to the hadiths reached through the Ahl al-Bayt in this regard, there are many narrations in Sunni books indicating its obligatoriness.

Among them, in a famous narration, Aisha says: "I heard the Messenger of Allah (ص) say: 'No prayer is accepted except with purification and with Salat upon me.'"

Among the Sunni jurists, Al-Shafi'i considers it obligatory in the second Tashahhud, as does Ahmad [ibn Hanbal] in one of the two narrations reported from him, along with a group of other jurists. However, some, like Abu Hanifa, do not consider it obligatory. [^3]

[^1]: Al-Sawa'iq, p. 144. [^2]: Allamah Hilli in "Al-Tadhkirah," in the discussion of Tashahhud, narrates this view from Imam Ahmad and some Shafi'is, in addition to all Shi'a scholars. [^3]: Tadhkirat al-Allamah, Vol. 1, p. 126.

Interestingly, Al-Shafi'i has explicitly included this fatwa in his famous poem, where he says:

"O Household of the Messenger of Allah, love for you Is an obligation from Allah revealed in the Quran. It is sufficient for your great status that indeed Whoever does not send blessings upon you, has no prayer." [^1]

The next verse, in fact, presents the opposite point of the previous verse, saying:

"Indeed, those who annoy Allah and His Messenger—Allah has cursed them in this world and the Hereafter and has prepared for them a humiliating punishment" (إِنَّ الَّذِينَ يُؤْذُونَ اللَّهَ وَ رَسُولَهُ لَعَنَهُمُ اللَّهُ فِي الدُّنْيا وَ الْآخِرَةِ وَ أَعَدَّ لَهُمْ عَذاباً مُهِيناً).

Regarding what is meant by "annoying the Lord," some have said it refers to disbelief (Kufr) and atheism, which provoke Allah's anger; for "annoyance" in the case of Allah can have no meaning other than provoking anger.

There is also the possibility that annoying Allah is the same as annoying the Prophet (ص) and the believers, and the mention of Allah is for importance and emphasis.

As for annoying the Prophet of Islam (ص), it has a broad meaning and includes any action that causes him pain, whether it be disbelief, atheism, or opposing Allah's commands. Likewise,

[^1]: In the precious book "Al-Ghadir," the attribution of these verses to Imam Al-Shafi'i is cited from "Sharh al-Mawahib" by Al-Zarqani, Vol. 7, p. 7, and several others.

it includes false attributions and slander, or causing inconvenience when he invites them to his home, as mentioned in verse 53 of this same Surah: إِنَّ ذلِكُمْ كانَ يُؤْذِي النَّبِيَ‌ ("Indeed, that [behavior] was annoying the Prophet").

Or the subject mentioned in verse 61 of Surah At-Tawbah, where they accused the Prophet (ص) of being gullible and simple-minded because of the flexibility he showed toward people's words:

وَ مِنْهُمُ الَّذِينَ يُؤْذُونَ النَّبِيَّ وَ يَقُولُونَ هُوَ أُذُنٌ‌ ("And among them are those who annoy the Prophet and say: 'He is an ear [who listens to everything]'") and the like.

Even from the narrations recorded under the verse, it is understood that annoying the Prophet's family, especially Ali (عليه السلام) and his daughter Fatimah al-Zahra (عليها السلام), is also included in this verse. In Sahih Bukhari, part five, it is stated that the Messenger of Allah said:

"Fatimah is a part of me; whoever angers her, angers me." [^1]

This same hadith appears in Sahih Muslim in this form: "Fatimah is a part of me; whatever annoys her, annoys me." [^2] A similar meaning has been narrated regarding Ali (عليه السلام) from the Noble Prophet of Islam (ص). [^3]

As for "curse" (La'n) in the above verse, as we have said before, it means being distanced from Allah's mercy, and this is the exact opposite of the mercy and Salat mentioned in the previous verse.

In reality, being cursed and expelled from mercy—and that too by a God whose mercy is vast and infinite—is considered the worst type of punishment, especially since this expulsion from mercy is both in this world and the Hereafter (as it is in the verse under discussion).

Perhaps for this reason, the issue of "curse" is mentioned before the "humiliating punishment."

The expression "prepared" (A'adda) is evidence of the emphasis and significance of this punishment.

The last verse under discussion speaks of annoying the believers and assigns it extraordinary importance after the annoyance of Allah and the Prophet (ص). It says: "And those who annoy believing men and believing women for [something] other than what they have earned have admitted [upon themselves] a slander and manifest sin" (وَ الَّذِينَ يُؤْذُونَ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ وَ الْمُؤْمِناتِ بِغَيْرِ مَا اكْتَسَبُوا فَقَدِ احْتَمَلُوا بُهْتاناً وَ إِثْماً مُبِيناً). This is because the believer, through faith, has a connection with Allah and His Prophet, and for this reason, they are placed here alongside Allah and His Prophet.

The expression "for [something] other than what they have earned" indicates that they have not committed a sin that warrants annoyance or harm. From this, it becomes clear that whenever they commit a sin that necessitates a legal penalty (Hadd), retribution (Qisas), or discretionary punishment (Ta'zir), the execution of these matters regarding them is not problematic. Likewise, commanding the right and forbidding the wrong is not included in this statement.

Placing "slander" (Buhtan) before "manifest sin" (Ithm Mubin) is due to its importance, as slander is considered one of the greatest forms of annoyance, and the wound resulting from it is even harder than wounds from a spear or dagger, as the Arabic poet said:

"The wounds of the spearhead can heal, But the wound of the tongue does not heal."

In Islamic narrations, extraordinary importance has been given to this matter. In a hadith from Imam al-Sadiq (عليه السلام), we read that Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, says:

"Let him who annoys My believing servant receive notice of war from Me!" [^1]

Some commentators have said that from the tone of the verse, it is understood that there was a group in Medina who spread rumors about the believers and made false attributions to them (and even the Prophet of Allah was not safe from the tongues of these annoyers). This same group is not small in other societies, especially in today's societies, and their work is to plot against the good and the pure, and to fabricate and craft lies and slanders.

The Quran has severely subjected them to its harshest attacks and identified their actions as slander and manifest sin.

Evidence for this will also appear in the subsequent verses.

In another hadith narrated by Imam Ali ibn Musa al-Rida (عليه السلام) from his grandfather the Prophet, it is stated:

"Whoever slanders a believing man or a believing woman, or says something about them that is not in them, Allah the Exalted will station him on the Day of Resurrection upon a mound of fire until he comes out of [proves] what he said about them!" [^2]

[^1]: Usul al-Kafi, Vol. 2, p. 35. [^2]: Bihar al-Anwar, Vol. 75, p. 194.

Ayahs

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلنَّبِىُّ قُل لِّأَزْوَٰجِكَ وَبَنَاتِكَ وَنِسَآءِ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ يُدْنِينَ عَلَيْهِنَّ مِن جَلَـٰبِيبِهِنَّ ۚ ذَٰلِكَ أَدْنَىٰٓ أَن يُعْرَفْنَ فَلَا يُؤْذَيْنَ ۗ وَكَانَ ٱللَّهُ غَفُورًا رَّحِيمًا (٥٩)
O Prophet! Tell your wives and your daughters and the women of the faithful to draw closely over themselves their chadors [when going out]. That makes it likely for them to be recognized and not be troubled, and Allah is all-forgiving, all-merciful. (59)
۞ لَّئِن لَّمْ يَنتَهِ ٱلْمُنَـٰفِقُونَ وَٱلَّذِينَ فِى قُلُوبِهِم مَّرَضٌ وَٱلْمُرْجِفُونَ فِى ٱلْمَدِينَةِ لَنُغْرِيَنَّكَ بِهِمْ ثُمَّ لَا يُجَاوِرُونَكَ فِيهَآ إِلَّا قَلِيلًا (٦٠)
If the hypocrites and those in whose hearts is a sickness, and the rumourmongers in the city do not desist, We will prompt you [to take action] against them; then they will not be your neighbours in it except briefly. (60)
مَّلْعُونِينَ ۖ أَيْنَمَا ثُقِفُوٓا۟ أُخِذُوا۟ وَقُتِّلُوا۟ تَقْتِيلًا (٦١)
Accursed, they will be seized wherever they are confronted and slain violently: (61)
سُنَّةَ ٱللَّهِ فِى ٱلَّذِينَ خَلَوْا۟ مِن قَبْلُ ۖ وَلَن تَجِدَ لِسُنَّةِ ٱللَّهِ تَبْدِيلًا (٦٢)
Allah’s precedent with those who passed away before, and you will never find any change in Allah’s precedent. (62)

Occasion of Revelation

In the Tafsir of Ali ibn Ibrahim, regarding the occasion of revelation for the first verse, it is stated: In those days, Muslim women would go to the mosque and offer prayers behind the Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله. At night, when they went for the Maghrib and Isha prayers, some dissolute youths and hooligans would sit in their path, harassing and bothering them with jokes and improper speech. Consequently, the above verse was revealed, commanding them to observe their hijab completely so that they would be clearly recognized [as chaste women] and no one would find an excuse to harass them.

In the same book, regarding the occasion of revelation for the second verse, we read: A group of hypocrites were in Medina who would spread various rumors among the people about the Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله whenever he went on certain military expeditions (ghazawat). Sometimes they would say, "The Prophet has been killed," and at other times, "He has been captured." The Muslims who were unable to fight and had remained in Medina became deeply distressed. They brought their complaint to the Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله, and this verse was revealed, severely threatening these rumor-mongers. [^1]

[^1]: Tafsir of Ali ibn Ibrahim, as quoted in Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 4, p. 307.

Commentary

A Severe Warning to Harassers and Rumor-mongers!

Following the prohibition of annoying the Messenger of Allah صلي الله عليه وآله and the believers in the previous verses, the text here focuses on one specific instance of harassment and takes action to prevent it through two methods:

First, it commands the believing women to remove any excuse or pretext from the hands of mischief-makers. Then, with a severe threat that is rare in the verses of the Quran, it attacks the hypocrites, the harassers, and the rumor-mongers.

In the first part, it says: "O Prophet! Tell your wives and your daughters and the women of the believers to draw their long veils over themselves, so that they may be recognized and not be annoyed" (يَا أَيُّهَا النَّبِيُّ قُلْ لِأَزْوَاجِكَ وَبَنَاتِكَ وَنِسَاءِ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ يُدْنِينَ عَلَيْهِنَّ مِنْ جَلَابِيبِهِنَّ ذَلِكَ أَدْنَى أَنْ يُعْرَفْنَ فَلَا يُؤْذَيْنَ).

Regarding what is meant by "being recognized," there are two views among commentators that are not mutually exclusive:

First, at that time, it was customary for slave girls to leave the house without covering their heads and necks. Since they were not in a good situation regarding moral protection, some dissolute youths would occasionally harass them. Here, free Muslim women were commanded to fully observe the Islamic hijab so they would be distinguished from slave girls and not give the dissolute an excuse for harassment.

It is obvious that the meaning of this statement is not that the hooligans had the right to harass slave girls; rather, the intent is to take away the pretext from corrupt individuals.

Second, the goal is that Muslim women should not be negligent or indifferent in wearing the hijab, like some loose women who, despite having a hijab, are so reckless and careless that parts of their bodies are often visible, and this very thing attracts the attention of dissolute individuals to them.

Regarding the meaning of "jilbab," commentators and lexicographers have mentioned several meanings:

  1. A large sheet (chador) or a large piece of cloth longer than a headscarf that covers the head, neck, and chest.
  2. A head-covering (maqna'ah) or a veil (khimar).
  3. A loose shirt. [^1]

Although these meanings differ, the common denominator among all of them is that the body is covered by it. (Additionally, it should be noted that "jilbab" can be recited with both a kasra or a fatha on the letter jim).

However, it seems more likely that it refers to a covering larger than a headscarf and smaller than a chador, as emphasized by the author of Lisan al-Arab.

The meaning of "yudnina" (to draw near) is that women should bring the jilbab close to their bodies so that it protects them properly, rather than leaving it loose such that it occasionally slips aside and the body becomes visible—or in our simple terms, they should "tidy up" their clothing.

As for those who have tried to use this phrase to argue that the face must also be covered, there is no indication of this meaning, and very few commentators have included covering the face within the concept of the verse. [^1]

[^1]: Lisan al-Arab, Majma' al-Bahrayn, Mufradat al-Raghib, Qutr al-Muhit, and Taj al-Arus.

[^1]: Regarding the philosophy of hijab and its importance, as well as the exception of the face and hands up to the wrists, we have had a detailed discussion in Volume 14 of Tafsir Nemouneh under verses 30 and 31 of Surah An-Nur (page 442 onwards).

In any case, it is understood from this verse that the ruling on "hijab and covering" for free women had been revealed before this time, but some, out of simple-mindedness, were not properly observant of it. The above verse emphasizes that they should be precise in observing it.

And since the revelation of this ruling might have distressed a group of believing women regarding their past, at the end of the verse it adds: "And Allah is ever Forgiving and Merciful" (وَكَانَ اللَّهُ غَفُورًا رَحِيمًا).

If you have been negligent in this matter until now because of ignorance, Allah will forgive you; repent and return to Him, and fulfill the duty of modesty and covering well.

Following the instruction given to believing women in the previous verse, the text addresses the other dimension of this issue—namely, the malicious activities of the scoundrels and hooligans—saying: "If the hypocrites and those in whose hearts is a disease and those who spread false news in Medina do not cease, We will surely incite you against them; then they will not remain your neighbors therein except for a little while" (لَئِنْ لَمْ يَنْتَهِ الْمُنَافِقُونَ وَالَّذِينَ فِي قُلُوبِهِمْ مَرَضٌ وَالْمُرْجِفُونَ فِي الْمَدِينَةِ لَنُغْرِيَنَّكَ بِهِمْ ثُمَّ لَا يُجَاوِرُونَكَ فِيهَا إِلَّا قَلِيلًا). ^2

"Murjifun" is derived from the root "irjaf," meaning the spreading of falsehoods for the purpose of making others sad. The origin of "irjaf" means agitation and instability, and since false rumors create public agitation, this word is applied to them.

"Nughriyannaka" is from the root "ighra," meaning to invite someone to perform an action or learn something, accompanied by encouragement and incitement.

From the tone of the verse, it is understood that three groups were engaged in sabotage in Medina, each implementing their sinister goals in some way, and this had become a systematic program and trend rather than being personal or individual.

First were the "hypocrites" who strove to overthrow Islam through anti-Islamic conspiracies.

Second were the scoundrels and hooligans whom the Quran describes as those with diseased hearts (الَّذِينَ فِي قُلُوبِهِمْ مَرَضٌ), just as this expression appeared in verse 32 of this same Surah Al-Ahzab regarding lustful and sensual individuals: فَلَا تَخْضَعْنَ بِالْقَوْلِ فَيَطْمَعَ الَّذِي فِي قَلْبِهِ مَرَضٌ ("O wives of the Prophet, do not be soft in speech, lest he in whose heart is a disease should covet").

The third group were those who spread rumors in Medina, especially when the Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله and the army of Islam went on expeditions, weakening the morale of those left behind and giving false news of the defeat of the Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله and the believers. According to some commentators, this group was the Jews.

Thus, the Quran has placed all three groups under a severe threat.

There is also the possibility in the interpretation of the verse that these three destructive programs were all the work of the hypocrites, and separating them from one another is a separation of attributes, not of persons.

In any case, the Quran says: If these people continue their ugly and shameful acts, We will issue the command for a general attack and an assault against them, so that with a popular movement of the believers, We may uproot them all from Medina, and they will no longer be able to stay in this city.

And when they are driven out of this city and expelled from the protection of the Islamic government, "Accursed, wherever they are found, they shall be seized and killed with a [terrible] slaughter" (مَلْعُونِينَ أَيْنَمَا ثُقِفُوا أُخِذُوا وَقُتِّلُوا تَقْتِيلًا).

"Thuqifu" is from the root "thaqf" and "thaqafat," meaning to come upon something with precision and skill; and if science and culture are called "thaqafat," it is for this same reason.

This indicates that after this general attack, they will not be safe anywhere; they will be searched for and found with precision and sent to the realm of non-existence.

Regarding whether this verse means they will be searched for outside Medina and killed, or if they will face such a fate if they remain inside Medina after the general decree of exile, two possibilities exist. At the same time, there is no contradiction between the two; meaning that after the immunity of these "diseased-hearted, rumor-mongering, and destructive conspirators" is revoked and the decree for their expulsion from Medina is issued, whether they stay there or go out, they will not be safe from the hands of the brave and self-sacrificing Muslims!

Then, in the final verse under discussion, it adds that this is not a new command: "This is the way of Allah with those who passed away before" (سُنَّةَ اللَّهِ فِي الَّذِينَ خَلَوْا مِنْ قَبْلُ). Whenever destructive groups exceed the limits of shamelessness and conspiracy, the command for a general attack against them is issued.

And because this ruling is a divine Sunnah, it will never change, for "you will never find in the way of Allah any change" (وَلَنْ تَجِدَ لِسُنَّةِ اللَّهِ تَبْدِيلًا).

This expression actually defines the seriousness of this threat, so they may know that the matter is completely certain and deep-rooted, and there is no room for change or substitution in it; they must either end their shameful acts or await such a painful fate.

Key Points

1- Start with Yourself!

In the instruction given in the verses under discussion regarding the full observance of the Islamic hijab, where it tells the Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله to convey this command, the wives of the Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله are mentioned first, then his daughters, and then the believing women. This indicates that any reform must begin with oneself and one's own family, and this is a program for all human reformers.

Among the wives and daughters, he mentions the wives first because they are closer to the man, as daughters take husbands and move to the homes of their spouses.

2- Prevention through Two Ways

Since social vices often do not have a single cause, the struggle against them must begin from all sides. Interestingly, in the above verses, to prevent the harassment of dissolute individuals, it first commands the believing women not to give them a pretext, and then it puts the harassers in their place with the most severe threat.

This is also a program for all times and all people: both the friend must be reformed, and the enemy must be put in their place with power.

3- The Strong Position of the Muslims

From the severe and powerful threats in the above verses, it is clearly understood that after the conclusion of the "Banu Qurayza" affair and the uprooting of this group of dangerous internal enemies, the position of the Muslims in Medina became completely stabilized. The only opposition came from the hypocrites who were anonymously in the ranks of the Muslims, or a group of scoundrels, hooligans, and rumor-mongers. The Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله confronted them from a position of power and warned them severely that if they did not stop their poisoning and conspiracies, their account would be settled with a single onslaught and attack. This decisive, completely serious, and calculated confrontation had its effect.

4- Uprooting the Source of Corruption

Is what is mentioned in the above verses for uprooting vices—such as the conspiracies of the hypocrites, the continuous harassment of the honor of Muslims, and the mischief-making of rumor-mongers—permissible in other ages and centuries for Islamic governments as well?

Few commentators have discussed this matter, but it seems that this ruling, like other Islamic rulings, is not restricted to a specific time, place, or persons.

If indeed the poisoning and conspiracy exceed the limit and become a trend, facing the Islamic society with serious dangers, what is to prevent the Islamic government from implementing the instruction of the above verses given to the Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله and mobilizing the people to crush the roots of corruption?!
