However, undoubtedly, such actions and the like—especially since it is introduced as an unchangeable Sunnah—are not permitted arbitrarily and must only take place with the permission of the Wali al-Amr of the Muslims and the judges of Sharia.

5- The Unchangeable Divine Sunnahs

In the above verses, we read that the Quran mentions the uprooting of conspirators with a general attack as one of the unchangeable divine Sunnahs that existed in previous nations as well.

Similar expressions have appeared in other parts of the Quran.

For instance, in this same Surah Al-Ahzab, verse 38, after issuing the permission to break the wrong pre-Islamic custom regarding the prohibition of marrying the "divorced wife of an adopted son," it says: There is no sin or crime upon the Prophet in carrying out divine commands, whatever they may be. Then it adds: سُنَّةَ اللَّهِ فِي الَّذِينَ خَلَوْا مِنْ قَبْلُ وَكَانَ أَمْرُ اللَّهِ قَدَرًا مَقْدُورًا ("This is the Sunnah of the Lord which was also among previous nations and former prophets, and the command of Allah has been a determined decree based on fixed and unchangeable criteria").

In Surah Fatir, verse 43, after threatening the disbelieving and criminal nations with destruction, it says: فَهَلْ يَنْظُرُونَ إِلَّا سُنَّتَ الْأَوَّلِينَ فَلَنْ تَجِدَ لِسُنَّتِ اللَّهِ تَبْدِيلًا وَلَنْ تَجِدَ لِسُنَّتِ اللَّهِ تَحْوِيلًا ("Do they await anything but the fate that befell the former peoples? But you will never find in the way of Allah any change, nor will you ever find in the way of Allah any alteration. What befell them shall befall these!").

In verse 85 of Surah Ghafir, after clarifying that the faith of the stubborn disbelievers among previous nations was of no use when they witnessed the annihilating punishment, it adds: سُنَّتَ اللَّهِ الَّتِي قَدْ خَلَتْ فِي عِبَادِهِ وَخَسِرَ هُنَالِكَ الْكَافِرُونَ ("This is the Sunnah of Allah which has passed among His servants, and there the disbelievers were lost in ruin").

And in Surah Al-Fath, verse 23, after discussing the victory of the believers, the defeat of the disbelievers, and the lack of any protector or helper for them in wars, it adds: سُنَّةَ اللَّهِ الَّتِي قَدْ خَلَتْ مِنْ قَبْلُ وَلَنْ تَجِدَ لِسُنَّةِ اللَّهِ تَبْدِيلًا ("This is the Sunnah of the Lord which has been before, and never will you find in the Sunnah of Allah any change").

Also, in Surah Al-Isra, verse 77, when it describes the conspiracy to exile or destroy the Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله, it adds: "If they had carried out this act of theirs, they would not have remained after you except for a little while": سُنَّةَ مَنْ قَدْ أَرْسَلْنَا قَبْلَكَ مِنْ رُسُلِنَا وَلَا تَجِدُ لِسُنَّتِنَا تَحْوِيلًا ("This is the Sunnah of those We sent before you of Our messengers, and you will find no alteration in Our Sunnah").

From the sum of these verses, it is well understood that "Sunnah" in such cases refers to the fixed and fundamental "ontological" (takwini) or "legislative" (tashri'i) laws of Allah that never undergo change. In other words, Allah has principles and laws in the realms of creation and legislation that, like the fundamental laws customary among the people of the world, are not subject to alteration or change. These laws governed past nations, and they will govern the nations of today and the future.

Helping the prophets, the defeat of the disbelievers, the necessity of acting upon divine commands even if they are unpleasant to the environment, the uselessness of repentance at the time of the descent of divine punishment, and the like, are part of these eternal Sunnahs.

These expressions, on one hand, provide encouragement and peace to all travelers on the path of truth, and on the other hand, clarify the coherence and unity of the call of the prophets and the integration of the laws governing the system of creation and the system of human life; in truth, it is one of the branches of Monotheism (Tawhid).

Ayahs

يَسْـَٔلُكَ ٱلنَّاسُ عَنِ ٱلسَّاعَةِ ۖ قُلْ إِنَّمَا عِلْمُهَا عِندَ ٱللَّهِ ۚ وَمَا يُدْرِيكَ لَعَلَّ ٱلسَّاعَةَ تَكُونُ قَرِيبًا (٦٣)
The people question you concerning the Hour. Say, ‘Its knowledge is only with Allah.’ What do you know, maybe the Hour is near. (63)
إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَعَنَ ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ وَأَعَدَّ لَهُمْ سَعِيرًا (٦٤)
Indeed Allah has cursed the faithless and prepared for them a blaze, (64)
خَـٰلِدِينَ فِيهَآ أَبَدًا ۖ لَّا يَجِدُونَ وَلِيًّا وَلَا نَصِيرًا (٦٥)
in which they will remain forever, and will not find any guardian or helper. (65)
يَوْمَ تُقَلَّبُ وُجُوهُهُمْ فِى ٱلنَّارِ يَقُولُونَ يَـٰلَيْتَنَآ أَطَعْنَا ٱللَّهَ وَأَطَعْنَا ٱلرَّسُولَا۠ (٦٦)
The day when their faces are turned about in the Fire, they will say, ‘We wish we had obeyed Allah and obeyed the Apostle!’ (66)
وَقَالُوا۟ رَبَّنَآ إِنَّآ أَطَعْنَا سَادَتَنَا وَكُبَرَآءَنَا فَأَضَلُّونَا ٱلسَّبِيلَا۠ (٦٧)
They will say, ‘Our Lord! We obeyed our leaders and elders, and they led us astray from the way.’ (67)
رَبَّنَآ ءَاتِهِمْ ضِعْفَيْنِ مِنَ ٱلْعَذَابِ وَٱلْعَنْهُمْ لَعْنًا كَبِيرًا (٦٨)
Our Lord! Give them a double punishment and curse them with a mighty curse.’ (68)

Commentary

They Ask: When Will the Resurrection Occur?!

The preceding verses spoke of the conspiracies of the hypocrites and the wicked; the verses under discussion point to another of their destructive programs. Sometimes out of mockery, or with the intent to instill doubt and hesitation in the hearts of the simple-minded, they would pose this question: When will the Resurrection—with all those descriptions that Muhammad (صلى الله عليه وآله) reports—take place?

It says: "People ask you concerning the Hour [of Resurrection]" (يَسْئَلُكَ النَّاسُ عَنِ السَّاعَةِ).

It is also possible that some believers, out of curiosity or to gain more information, posed such a question to the Noble Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله). However, considering the verses that follow this one, it becomes clear that the first interpretation is closer to the meaning of the verse.

Evidence for this is another verse on the same subject in Surah Ash-Shura: "And what can make you know? Perhaps the Hour is near. Those who do not believe in it seek to hasten it, while those who believe are fearful of it and know that it is the truth" (Ash-Shura, verses 17 and 18).

Then, in the verse under discussion, He answers them as such: "Say [O Prophet!]: Knowledge of it is only with Allah, and no one besides Him is aware of this matter" (قُلْ إِنَّما عِلْمُها عِنْدَ اللَّهِ).

Even the Messenger Prophets and the archangels are unaware of it.

Following that, He adds: "And what can make you know? Perhaps the Hour is near" (وَ ما يُدْرِيكَ لَعَلَّ السَّاعَةَ تَكُونُ قَرِيباً).

Therefore, one must always be prepared to welcome the occurrence of the Resurrection. Fundamentally, the philosophy behind its concealment is precisely so that no one feels [falsely] secure, nor considers the Resurrection distant, nor deems themselves exempt from Divine punishment and retribution.

Then, He turns to threatening the disbelievers and mentions a portion of their painful punishment, saying: "Indeed, Allah has cursed the disbelievers [removed them from His mercy] and has prepared for them a blazing Fire" (إِنَّ اللَّهَ لَعَنَ الْكافِرِينَ وَ أَعَدَّ لَهُمْ سَعِيراً).

"They will abide therein forever; they will find no protector (wali) nor helper (nasir)" (خالِدِينَ فِيها أَبَداً لا يَجِدُونَ وَلِيًّا وَ لا نَصِيراً). The difference between a "wali" and a "nasir" here is that a "wali" takes charge of the entire affair, whereas a "nasir" is someone who assists a person to reach their goal. However, these disbelievers on the Day of Resurrection will have neither a protector nor a helper.

Then, He describes another part of their painful torment on the Day of Resurrection, saying: "Remember the day when their faces will be turned about in the Fire" (يَوْMَ تُقَلَّبُ وُجُوهُهُمْ فِي النَّارِ).

This "turning about" is either in terms of the color of their faces—which sometimes become red and bruised, and at other times yellow and withered—or in terms of being placed upon the flames of the Fire, such that sometimes this side of their face is placed on the fire, and sometimes the other side (we seek refuge in Allah).

It is here that their regretful cries rise up and "they say: 'Oh, would that we had obeyed Allah and obeyed the Messenger!'" (يَقُولُونَ يا لَيْتَنا أَطَعْنَا اللَّهَ وَ أَطَعْنَا الرَّسُولَا). For if we had obeyed, such a painful fate would not have been awaiting us.

"And they say: 'Our Lord! Indeed, we obeyed our chiefs and our elders, and they led us astray from the [right] path'" (وَ قالُوا رَبَّنا إِنَّا أَطَعْنا سادَتَنا وَ كُبَراءَنا فَأَضَلُّونَا السَّبِيلَا) [1]. "Sadah" is the plural of "Sayyid," meaning a great master who holds the management of important cities or a country. "Kubara" is the plural of "Kabir," meaning great individuals, whether in terms of age, knowledge, social status, or the like. In this way, "Sadah" refers to the major leaders of the environment, and "Kubara" are those who manage affairs under their supervision and are considered their deputies and consultants. In reality, [they say] we placed obedience to the chiefs in place of obedience to Allah, and obedience to the elders in place of obedience to the Prophets, and thus we fell into various deviations and miseries.

Naturally, the criteria for "mastery" and "greatness" among them were the same criteria of force, bullying, illegitimate wealth, and deception. The choice of these two terms here is perhaps to make their excuse appear somewhat justified, as if to say: "We were influenced by their outward grandeur."

[1]: The 'Alif' in "al-Rasula" and "al-Sabila" (considering that nunation/tanwin never combines with 'Alif and Lam') is the "Alif of Absolute" (Alif al-Itlaq) used for the rhythmic harmony of the verse endings.

At this point, these strayed denizens of Hell become agitated and ask Allah for the severe punishment of those who misled them, saying: "Our Lord! Give them double punishment" (one punishment for their own misguidance and one for misleading us!) (رَبَّنا آتِهِمْ ضِعْفَيْنِ مِنَ الْعَذابِ). "And curse them with a great curse!" (وَ الْعَنْهُمْ لَعْناً كَبِيراً).

Certainly, they deserve punishment and cursing, but the "double punishment" and "great curse" are due to the effort and exertion they put into misleading others.

Interestingly, in Surah Al-A'raf, when these misled followers request double punishment for their leaders and chiefs, it is said: "For each is double, but you do not know" (Surah Al-A'raf, verse 38) [1]. The doubling of the punishment for the leaders of disbelief and misguidance is clear, but why is the punishment of these misled followers doubled?! The reason is that they have one punishment for their own misguidance, and another punishment for strengthening and aiding the oppressors. This is because oppressors cannot achieve anything alone; rather, their followers are the ones who fan the flames of the conflict and heat the furnace of their oppression and disbelief—though undoubtedly, in comparison to one another, the punishment of the leaders is harsher and more painful.

We have had a more detailed discussion on this subject in the commentary of verse 30 of this same Surah.

[1]: It is noteworthy that in the verses under discussion, "dif'ayn" (two doubles) is used, while in the verse of Surah Al-A'raf, "dif'" (double) is used; however, upon careful examination of the concept of "dif'," it becomes clear that both carry the same meaning.

Ayahs

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ لَا تَكُونُوا۟ كَٱلَّذِينَ ءَاذَوْا۟ مُوسَىٰ فَبَرَّأَهُ ٱللَّهُ مِمَّا قَالُوا۟ ۚ وَكَانَ عِندَ ٱللَّهِ وَجِيهًا (٦٩)
O you who have faith! Do not be like those who offended Moses, whereat Allah cleared him of what they alleged, and he was distinguished in Allah’s sight. (69)
يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ ٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ وَقُولُوا۟ قَوْلًا سَدِيدًا (٧٠)
O you who have faith! Be wary of Allah, and speak upright words. (70)
يُصْلِحْ لَكُمْ أَعْمَـٰلَكُمْ وَيَغْفِرْ لَكُمْ ذُنُوبَكُمْ ۗ وَمَن يُطِعِ ٱللَّهَ وَرَسُولَهُۥ فَقَدْ فَازَ فَوْزًا عَظِيمًا (٧١)
He will rectify your conduct for you and forgive you your sins. Whoever obeys Allah and His Apostle will certainly achieve a great success. (71)

Commentary

What Unjust Accusations Were Made Against Moses (عليه السلام)?

Following the discussions in the previous verses regarding respecting the status of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) and refraining from any form of annoyance toward him, the discourse here turns to the believers, saying: "O you who have believed, do not be like those who annoyed Moses; then Allah cleared him of what they said, and he was honorable in the sight of Allah" (يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا لَا تَكُونُوا كَالَّذِينَ آذَوْا مُوسَىٰ فَبَرَّأَهُ اللَّهُ مِمَّا قَالُوا ۚ وَكَانَ عِنْدَ اللَّهِ وَجِيهًا).

The selection of Moses from among all the prophets who were subjected to annoyance is because the harassers among the Children of Israel tormented him more than any other prophet. Furthermore, some of these harassments bore a resemblance to the annoyances caused by the hypocrites toward the Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله).

There is discussion among commentators as to what is meant by the "annoyance of Moses (عليه السلام)" here and why the Quran has mentioned it in a general manner. Various possibilities have been suggested in the interpretation of the verse, including:

  1. According to a narration, Moses (عليه السلام) and Aaron (عليه السلام) went up a mountain, and Aaron passed away. The rumor-mongers of the Children of Israel attributed his death to Moses (عليه السلام). Allah clarified the truth of the matter and exposed the rumor-makers.

  2. As we detailed in the commentary of the final verses of Surah Al-Qasas, the deceitful Korah (Qarun), in order to avoid submitting to the law of Zakat and paying the rights of the needy, hatched a conspiracy. He arranged for a prostitute to stand up among the crowd and accuse Moses (عليه السلام) of having an illicit relationship with her. By Divine grace, not only did this conspiracy fail, but the woman herself testified to the purity of Moses (عليه السلام) and the plot of Korah.

  3. A group of Moses' (عليه السلام) enemies accused him of sorcery, madness, and attributing lies to God. However, Allah cleared him of these unjust accusations through manifest miracles.

  4. A group of the ignorant among the Children of Israel accused him of having certain physical defects, such as leprosy and the like, because whenever he wished to perform a ritual bath (ghusl) and wash himself, he would never strip in front of others. However, one day, while he intended to bathe in a secluded spot away from the crowd, he placed his clothes on a stone. The stone began to move and carried his clothes away, and the Children of Israel saw his body and realized he had no defect or flaw!

  5. The excuse-seeking of the Children of Israel was another factor in annoying Moses (عليه السلام). Sometimes they demanded that he show God to them; sometimes they said that uniform food (Manna and Salwa) was not suitable for them; and sometimes they said, "We are not prepared to enter Jerusalem and fight the giants (Amalika); you and your Lord go and conquer it, then we will enter."

However, what is closer to the meaning of the verse is that the noble verse expresses a general and comprehensive rule, for the Children of Israel annoyed Moses (عليه السلام) from various aspects—annoyances that were not unlike the harassment some people in Medina caused the Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله), such as spreading rumors, relaying lies, and the unjust accusation made against the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله) wife (the details of which were discussed in the commentary of Surah An-Nur, Vol. 14, under verses 11 to 20), as well as the criticisms regarding the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله) marriage to Zaynab, and the disturbances they caused in his house or by calling out to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) in an impolite manner.

As for attributing sorcery, madness, and the like, or physical defects—though they occurred in the case of Moses—they do not align well with the address "O you who have believed" regarding the Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله). This is because neither Moses (عليه السلام) nor the Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله) were accused of "sorcery" or "madness" by the believers. Likewise, the accusation of physical defects, assuming it occurred regarding Moses (عليه السلام) and Allah cleared him, has no historical instance regarding the Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله).

In any case, it can be inferred from this verse that whenever someone is honorable (Wajih) and possesses status and rank in the sight of Allah, Allah will undertake his defense against the harassers who cast unjust accusations upon him. Be pure and maintain your honor in the sight of Allah; He will reveal your purity in due time, no matter how hard the ill-wishers strive to accuse you.

We read a similar meaning in the story of the truthful and chaste Joseph (Yusuf), how Allah cleared him of the great and dangerous accusation of the wife of the Al-Aziz of Egypt.

Similarly, in the case of Maryam, the mother of Jesus (عليه السلام), her nursing infant testified to her purity and silenced the tongues of the ill-wishers among the Children of Israel who sought to accuse her.

It also seems necessary to mention that this address was not exclusive to the believers of the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله) time. It is possible for people to come into existence after him who act in a way that grieves his holy spirit, belittle his religion, waste his efforts, or consign his legacy to oblivion; they too would be included in this verse.

Therefore, in some narrations transmitted through the Ahl al-Bayt (علیهم السلام), we read: "Those who annoyed Ali (عليه السلام) and his children are included in this verse." [1]

The final word in the commentary of the verse is that, considering the lives of the great prophets who were not safe from the sharp tongues of the ignorant and the hypocrites, one should not expect that pure and believing individuals will never be afflicted by such people. As Imam al-Sadiq (عليه السلام) says: "The satisfaction of all people cannot be attained, and the tongues of people cannot be controlled." At the end of this hadith, the Imam adds: "Was Moses not accused of defects? And was he not annoyed? Until Allah cleared him." [1]

Speak the Truth So That Your Deeds May Be Rectified

The following verse, following the discussions about rumor-mongers and foul-mouthed harassers, issues a command that is, in fact, the cure for this great social malady. It says: "O you who have believed, fear Allah and speak words of appropriate justice [right and straight]" (يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا اتَّقُوا اللَّهَ وَقُولُوا قَوْلًا سَدِيدًا).

The word "Sadid" is derived from the root "Sadd," meaning "firm and solid," impenetrable, and in accordance with truth and reality. It is a speech that, like a strong dam, blocks the waves of corruption and falsehood. If some commentators have interpreted it as "correct," and others as "being free from lies and vanity," or "harmony between the internal and external," or "rectitude and guidance," they all return to the comprehensive meaning mentioned above.

The next verse states the result of "Qawl Sadid" (straight speech) and truthful words as follows: "He will [then] amend for you your deeds and forgive you your sins" (يُصْلِحْ لَكُمْ أَعْمَالَكُمْ وَيَغْفِرْ لَكُمْ ذُنُوبَكُمْ).

In reality, Taqwa (piety/God-consciousness) is the foundation for rectifying the tongue and the source of truthful speech. Truthful speech is one of the effective factors in rectifying deeds, and the rectification of deeds leads to the forgiveness of sins, for "Indeed, good deeds do away with misdeeds" (Surah Hud, Verse 114).

Scholars of ethics have said: The tongue is the most blessed organ of the body and the most effective means of obedience, guidance, and reform; yet at the same time, it is considered the most dangerous and most sinful organ of the body, to the extent that about thirty major sins originate from this small organ. [1] In a hadith from the Noble Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله), we read: "The faith of a servant of Allah does not become straight until his heart becomes straight, and his heart does not become straight until his tongue becomes straight." [2]

Interestingly, in another hadith from Imam al-Sajjad (عليه السلام), it is stated that "The tongue of every human being asks about the well-being of the other organs every morning, saying: 'How did you begin your morning?' They all say in response to this expression of affection by the tongue: 'We are well if you leave us alone!' Then they add: 'We adjure you by Allah to be mindful of us, for we are only rewarded through you and punished through you.'" [1]

There are many narrations in this regard, all indicating the extraordinary importance of the role of the tongue and its rectification in the reform of ethics and the purification of the human soul. For this reason, we read in a hadith: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله) never sat on this pulpit except that he recited this verse: 'O you who have believed, fear Allah and speak words of appropriate justice.'" [2]

At the end of the verse, it adds: "And whoever obeys Allah and His Messenger has certainly attained a great attainment" (وَمَنْ يُطِعِ اللَّهَ وَرَسُولَهُ فَقَدْ فازَ فَوْزاً عَظِيماً).

What salvation and victory could be greater and higher than for a person's deeds to be purified, his sins forgiven, and for him to be honored in the presence of Allah?!

[1]: Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 4, p. 308. [1]: Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 4, p. 309. [1]: Al-Ghazali in "Ihya' Ulum al-Din" has enumerated twenty major sins or deviations that originate from the tongue, which are: 1- Lying 2- Backbiting 3- Tale-bearing 4- Hypocrisy in speech (being double-tongued) 5- Undue praise 6- Foul language and insults 7- Vain singing and improper poetry 8- Excessive joking 9- Ridicule and mockery 10- Disclosing others' secrets 11- Breaking promises 12- Undue cursing 13- Hostility and quarreling 14- Disputation and arguing 15- Talking about vain matters 16- Loquacity (excessive talking) 17- Talking about matters that do not concern one 18- Describing gatherings of wine, gambling, and sin 19- Questioning and searching into matters beyond human comprehension 20- Affectation and pretension in speech. We have added ten other important subjects to it: 1- Slander 2- False testimony 3- Spreading indecency and baseless rumors 4- Self-praise 5- Undue persistence 6- Harshness in speech 7- Annoying with the tongue 8- Blaming someone who does not deserve blame 9- Ingratitude for blessings through the tongue 10- Promoting falsehood. [2]: Bihar al-Anwar, Vol. 71, p. 78. [1]: Bihar al-Anwar, Vol. 71, p. 278. [2]: Al-Durr al-Manthur (as cited in Tafsir al-Mizan, Vol. 16, p. 376).

Ayahs

إِنَّا عَرَضْنَا ٱلْأَمَانَةَ عَلَى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ وَٱلْجِبَالِ فَأَبَيْنَ أَن يَحْمِلْنَهَا وَأَشْفَقْنَ مِنْهَا وَحَمَلَهَا ٱلْإِنسَـٰنُ ۖ إِنَّهُۥ كَانَ ظَلُومًا جَهُولًا (٧٢)
Indeed We presented the Trust to the heavens and the earth and the mountains, but they refused to undertake it and were apprehensive of it; but man undertook it. Indeed he is most unjust and ignorant. (72)
لِّيُعَذِّبَ ٱللَّهُ ٱلْمُنَـٰفِقِينَ وَٱلْمُنَـٰفِقَـٰتِ وَٱلْمُشْرِكِينَ وَٱلْمُشْرِكَـٰتِ وَيَتُوبَ ٱللَّهُ عَلَى ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ وَٱلْمُؤْمِنَـٰتِ ۗ وَكَانَ ٱللَّهُ غَفُورًا رَّحِيمًۢا (٧٣)
Allah will surely punish the hypocrites, men and women, and the polytheists, men and women, and Allah will turn clemently to the faithful, men and women, and Allah is all-forgiving, all-merciful. (73)

Commentary

Carrying the Divine Trust—The Greatest Honor of Mankind

These two verses, which are the final verses of Surah Al-Ahzab, complete the important matters discussed in this Surah regarding faith, righteous deeds, Jihad, sacrifice, chastity, etiquette, and ethics. They demonstrate how man possesses a highly distinguished position, capable of being the bearer of the great Divine mission, and how, if he remains ignorant of his own existential values, he wrongs himself and falls to the lowest of the low! First, He states the greatest and most important distinction of man over the entire world of creation, saying: "Indeed, We offered the Trust to the heavens and the earth and the mountains" (إِنَّا عَرَضْنَا الْأَمانَةَ عَلَى السَّماواتِ وَ الْأَرْضِ وَ الْجِبالِ‌).

"But these great entities of the world of creation refused to bear it and expressed their inability, and were afraid of it!" (فَأَبَيْنَ أَنْ يَحْمِلْنَها وَ أَشْفَقْنَ مِنْها).

It is evident that their refusal was not out of arrogance, as we read regarding Satan and his refusal to prostrate to Adam: "He refused and was arrogant" (Surah Al-Baqarah, Verse 34). Rather, their refusal was accompanied by ishfaq, meaning fear and dread mixed with mindfulness and humility.

But in the midst of this, man—this wonder of the world of creation—stepped forward "and bore it!" (وَ حَمَلَهَا الْإِنْسانُ‌).

Yet, alas, "from the very beginning he wronged himself; he did not recognize his own worth and did not fulfill what was worthy of bearing this Trust" (إِنَّهُ كانَ ظَلُوماً جَهُولًا).

The great commentators of Islam have spoken extensively regarding this verse and have made great efforts to uncover the true meaning of the "Trust" (al-amanah), expressing various views. We select the best of them by searching for the clues hidden within the verse itself.

Fundamentally, in this profound verse, focus must be placed on five points:

  1. What is meant by the "Trust"—the Divine Trust?
  2. What does it mean to offer it to the heavens, the earth, and the mountains?
  3. Why and how did these beings refuse to bear this Trust?
  4. How did man become the bearer of this burden of Trust?
  5. Why and how was he "unjust" (zhalum) and "ignorant" (jahul)?

Regarding the "Trust," various interpretations have been mentioned, including: - The Trust refers to "Divine Wilayah" (Guardianship) and the perfection of the attribute of servitude ('ubudiyyah), which is achieved through knowledge and righteous deeds. - It refers to the attribute of "choice and free will," which distinguishes man from other creatures. - It refers to "reason" ('aql), which is the criterion for religious obligation (taklif) and the basis for reward and punishment. - It refers to the "limbs of the human body": the eye is a Divine Trust that must be protected and not used in the path of sin; the ear, hand, foot, and tongue are each other trusts whose preservation is obligatory. - It refers to "trusts that people take from one another" and the fulfillment of covenants. - It refers to the "Knowledge of Allah" (Ma'rifatullah). - It refers to "Divine obligations and duties" such as prayer, fasting, and Hajj.

However, with a little reflection, it becomes clear that these various interpretations are not contradictory; rather, some can be merged into others. Some look at a specific aspect of the matter, while others look at the whole.

To obtain a comprehensive answer, we must look at man and see what he possesses that the heavens, the earth, and the mountains lack. Man is a being with extraordinary potential who, by utilizing it, can become the ultimate manifestation of "the Vicegerent of Allah" (Khalifatullah). He can reach the pinnacle of honor through the acquisition of knowledge, self-purification, and perfections, even surpassing the angels of heaven.

This potential is coupled with freedom of will and choice; meaning he traverses this path—which starts from zero and goes toward infinity—with his own feet and by his own choice.

The heavens, the earth, and the mountains possess a type of Divine knowledge; they also perform the remembrance (dhikr) and glorification (tasbih) of God and are humble and prostrate before His greatness. However, all of this is inherent, ontological (takwini), and compulsory; for this reason, there is no evolutionary progress in it.

The only being whose ascending and descending arcs are infinite, who is capable of flying boundlessly toward the peak of perfection, and who performs all these actions with will and choice, is "Man." This is that very Divine Trust that all creatures declined to bear, and man entered the arena and carried it on his shoulders alone! Therefore, in the following verse, we see that He divides humans into three groups: believers, disbelievers, and hypocrites.

Thus, in a short and concise sentence, it must be said: The Divine Trust is that very capability for unlimited evolution, mixed with will and choice, reaching the station of the "Perfect Man" and the special servant of God, and the acceptance of Divine Wilayah.

But why is this matter expressed as a "Trust," even though our existence and everything we have are God's trusts? This is because of the importance of this great distinction of humans; otherwise, other blessings are also Divine trusts, but they hold less importance in comparison.

Here, one can refer to this Trust with another expression and say: The Divine Trust is "commitment and the acceptance of responsibility."

Therefore, those who considered the Trust to be the attribute of choice and free will have pointed to a corner of this great Trust, just as those who interpreted it as "reason," "bodily limbs," "trusts between people," "religious duties," or "obligations in general" have each reached out toward a branch of this great, fruitful tree and picked a fruit.

But what is meant by offering this Trust to the heavens and the earth? Does it mean that God granted them a portion of reason and consciousness, then proposed the bearing of this great Trust to them? Or does "offering" mean comparison? That is, when this Trust was compared with their potential, they declared their lack of merit for accepting this great Trust through their "existential state" (lisan al-hal) and capacity.

Of course, the second meaning seems more appropriate. In this way, the heavens, the earth, and the mountains all cried out through their existential state that bearing this Trust is beyond our capacity.

From here, the answer to the third question also becomes clear: why and how these beings refused to bear this great Trust and showed a fear mixed with respect.

And from this, the nature of man's bearing of this Divine Trust becomes clear, because man was created in such a way that he could carry the burden of commitment and responsibility, accept Divine Wilayah, and journey on the path of servitude and perfection toward the Eternal Beloved, traversing this path with his own feet and with the help of his Lord.

As for the fact that in numerous narrations reaching us from the Ahl al-Bayt عليهم السلام, this Divine Trust has been interpreted as "the acceptance of the Wilayah of the Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام, and his progeny," it is because the Wilayah of the Prophets and Imams is a powerful ray of that Universal Divine Wilayah. Reaching the station of servitude and traversing the path of perfection is not possible except by accepting the Wilayah of the Friends of Allah (Awliya' Allah).

In a tradition from Imam Ali ibn Musa al-Rida عليه السلام, we read that when he was asked about the interpretation of the verse of the "offering of the Trust," he said: «الامانة الولاية، من ادعاها بغير حق كفر» "The Trust is the Wilayah; whoever claims it without right has exited the fold of Muslims." [1]

In another tradition from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, we read that when he was asked about the interpretation of this verse, he said: «الامانة الولاية، و الانسان هو ابو الشرور المنافق» "The Trust is the Wilayah, and the 'man' described as unjust and ignorant is the one who is the master of many sins and a hypocrite." [2]

Another point that seems necessary to mention here is: Under verse 172 of Surah Al-A'raf, regarding the "World of Particles" ('Alam al-Dharr), we said it appears that the taking of the Divine covenant for Monotheism (Tawhid) was through the nature (fitrah), potential, and essence of man, and the World of Particles is this very world of potential and nature. Regarding the acceptance of the Divine Trust, it must also be said that this acceptance was not a conventional or ceremonial acceptance, but rather an ontological (takwini) acceptance according to the world of potential.

The only question that remains is the issue of man being "unjust" (zhalum) and "ignorant" (jahul). Is the description of man with these two attributes—which outwardly seem to be a rebuke and condemnation—because of his acceptance of this Trust?!

[1] and [2]: Tafsir al-Burhan, Vol. 3, under the verse in question (p. 341).

Certainly, the answer to this question is negative, because the acceptance of this Trust is man's greatest honor and distinction. How could it be possible to condemn him for accepting such a high station?

Rather, these descriptions are because of the forgetfulness of most humans, their self-wronging, and their lack of awareness of the value and status of man—the very thing that began in the lineage of Adam through "Cain" (Qabil) and the line of the Cainites and continues even now.

A human to whom "the pinnacle of the Throne whistles a call," a child of Adam upon whose head the crown of "We have honored" (karramna) has been placed, humans who, according to "Indeed, I am making a vicegerent on the earth," are God's representatives on earth, a being who was the teacher of angels and the one to whom the angels of heaven prostrated—how "unjust" and "ignorant" must he be to consign these great and lofty values to oblivion, to make himself a prisoner in this dust-heap, to place himself in the ranks of devils, and to fall to the lowest of the low?!

Yes, the acceptance of this deviant path—which unfortunately has had many travelers from the beginning and continues to do so—is the best evidence of man being unjust and ignorant. Therefore, even Adam himself, who was at the beginning of this chain and possessed the station of infallibility ('ismah), confesses that he wronged himself: "Our Lord, we have wronged ourselves, and if You do not forgive us and have mercy upon us, we will surely be among the losers" (Surah Al-A'raf, Verse 23).

In reality, that very "leaving of the better" (tark al-awla) that occurred from him resulted from forgetting a portion of the greatness of this massive Trust! In any case, it must be confessed that man—outwardly small and weak—is the wonder of the world of creation, who has been able to carry a burden of Trust that the heavens and the earths were unable to bear, provided he does not forget his own station. [1]

The next verse is, in fact, an explanation of the reason for offering this Trust to man; it is an explanation of the reality that human individuals, after bearing this great Divine Trust, were divided into three groups: hypocrites, polytheists, and believers. It says: "[The purpose was] so that Allah may punish the hypocrite men and hypocrite women, and the polytheist men and polytheist women, and that Allah may turn in mercy to the believing men and believing women. And Allah is ever Forgiving and Merciful" (لِيُعَذِّبَ اللَّهُ الْمُنافِقِينَ وَ الْمُنافِقاتِ وَ الْمُشْرِكِينَ وَ الْمُشْرِكاتِ وَ يَتُوبَ اللَّهُ عَلَى الْمُؤْمِنِينَ وَ الْمُؤْمِناتِ وَ كانَ اللَّهُ غَفُوراً رَحِيماً).

Regarding what kind of "L" (lam) the "L" in li-yu'adhdhiba (so that He may punish) is from the perspective of Arabic grammar, two possibilities exist:

First, it is the "L of consequence" (lam al-ghayah), which is mentioned to state the end result of something. Therefore, the concept of the verse is: "The end result of bearing this Trust was that a group followed the path of hypocrisy and a group the path of polytheism, and because of betrayal of this Divine Trust, they became subject to His punishment; and a group of the people of faith, because of fulfilling this Trust and standing by their duty toward it, became subject to His mercy."

Second, it is the "L of cause" (lam al-'illah), and a sentence is implied. Therefore, the interpretation of the verse is: "The goal of offering the Trust was that all humans be placed in the crucible of trial, so that everyone might manifest their inner self and receive punishment or reward according to their merit."

Here, several points should be noted

  1. Placing the people of hypocrisy before the polytheists is because the hypocrite pretends to be a trustee while being a traitor, whereas the polytheist's betrayal is overt; thus, the hypocrite is more deserving of punishment.
  1. Placing these two groups before the believers might be because the end of the previous verse concluded with "unjust" (zhalum) and "ignorant" (jahul); "unjust" and "ignorant" correspond to the hypocrite and the polytheist—the hypocrite is "unjust" and the polytheist is "ignorant."
  1. The word "Allah" appears once regarding the punishment of the hypocrites and polytheists, and once regarding the reward of the believers. This is because the first two groups share one fate, while the account of the believers is separate from them.
  1. The expression of "repentance" (tawbah)—instead of reward—regarding the believers might be because the greatest fear of the believers is from the slips that may have occasionally occurred from them; thus, He gives them assurance and peace that He will include their slips in His forgiveness. Or it is because God's "repentance" toward His servants is His return to mercy, and we know that within the word "mercy," all blessings and rewards are hidden.
  1. Describing the Lord as "Forgiving" (Ghafur) and "Merciful" (Rahim) may be in contrast to "unjust" and "ignorant," or in proportion to the mention of repentance regarding the believing men and women.

Now that, by the grace of the Lord, we reach the end of Surah Al-Ahzab, we deem it necessary to mention that the harmony between the beginning and the end of this Surah is worthy of reflection and attention. This Surah (Surah Al-Ahzab) began by addressing the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله regarding God-wariness (taqwa), forbidding the following of disbelievers and hypocrites, and relying on Allah being All-Knowing and All-Wise; and it ends with the mention of the greatest issue of human life—namely, bearing the Divine Trust—and then the division of humans into three groups: hypocrites, disbelievers, and believers, relying on Allah being Forgiving and Merciful. Between these two discussions, many topics were raised regarding these three groups and their behavior toward this Divine Trust, all of which complemented and clarified one another.

O Lord! Place us among those who "sincerely" accepted Your Trust, "lovingly" guarded it, and stood by their duties toward it. O Allah! Place us among the believers who have been included in Your forgiveness and mercy, not among the hypocrites and polytheists who, because of being "unjust" and "ignorant," became deserving of punishment.

O Allah! In this age and time when the "factions" (ahzab) of disbelief have once again surrounded the "Medina of Islam," send Your fierce storm of wrath and anger upon them, destroy their palaces over their heads, and grant us that steadfastness and perseverance to stand like a mountain in these sensitive moments and guard the Medina of Islam—Amen, O Lord of the Worlds.

End of Surah Al-Ahzab and end of Volume 17 of Tafsir Nemouneh. Friday, 2nd of Rabi' al-Thani, 1404 (January 6, 1984).

[1]: From what we said in the commentary of the verse, it became clear that there is no need for us to assume anything implied (muqaddar) in the verse, as a group of commentators have said, interpreting the verse to mean that the offering of the Divine Trust to the heavens, earth, and mountains refers to offering it to their inhabitants—namely, the angels! For this reason, they said, "Those who refused to accept the Trust fulfilled it, and those who accepted the Trust betrayed it." This interpretation is not only contrary to the apparent meaning due to the need for implication, but it is also objectionable because angels possess a type of obligation and carry a part of this Trust. Beyond all this, interpreting the "inhabitants of mountains" as angels is not without strangeness (reflect on this).
